# ostk — full text mirror

> Concatenated full text of every documented page on https://ostk.ai/.
> Curated index lives at https://ostk.ai/llms.txt (per https://llmstxt.org spec).

---

# Home — like containers, for AI agents

URL: https://ostk.ai/

SYSTEM_MANIFEST // OSTK

# Like containers,for AI agents.

Plus the kernel underneath.

Containers gave processes isolation, packaging, and reproducibility. ostk gives those to AI coding agents — and ships the kernel that coordinates the agents, audits their work, and signs the record.

Run Claude, Cursor, your own scripts, or a local model on the same codebase. They don't collide. They leave a signed trail. You stay in control.

Even with one agent, you get a signed audit trail and capability bounds. With two, you get the rest.

DRIVE_YOUR_AGENT Wire ostk into Claude Code, Cursor, or any MCP-capable tool. Keep your UI; gain coordination. ~10 MIN (/docs/mcp/) RUN_A_LOCAL_MODEL Run a local model through ollama or mlx, with ostk's terminal UI as the chat surface. No API bill. Everything stays local. ~15 MIN (/start/local/) SERVER_MODE Run `ostk daemon` on the host. CI runners, remote machines, and Discord bots connect on the Unix socket and drive agents. ~10 MIN (/start/server/)

TERMINAL

$ curl -fsSL https://ostk.ai/install.sh | sh

$ cd ~/projects/your-repo && ostk init

macOS (universal arm64+x86_64; arm64+macFUSE variant separate), Linux musl arm64/x86_64, Windows x86_64 zip. All on releases (https://github.com/os-tack/ostk.ai/releases/latest), GPG-signed. ~30 seconds.

BINARY: ONE_RUST_EXECUTABLE

DEPS: NONE_AT_RUNTIME

RUNTIME: LOCAL_ONLY

TRUST: GPG_SIGNED

WHAT_AGENTS_BREAK

Three things break the moment you run more than one agent. ostk fixes all three at the kernel layer, without changing how you drive your agents.

01

### They collide.

Two agents edit the same file. Last write wins. Silently. ostk catches every conflict before it lands. Nothing disappears; the second writer sees a clean error.

→ Read why (/why#collision)

02

### They drift.

Each agent has its own context. They re-discover the same things. They forget what was decided. ostk gives every agent a shared state directory to read from — the project develops a memory.

→ Read why (/why#drift)

03

### They leave no trace.

You can't replay, audit, or prove what shipped. ostk records every action in a hash-chained, Ed25519-signed audit trail. Replay any session, byte for byte.

→ Read why (/why#trace)

CONTAINERS_VS_OSTK

Containers gave processes isolation. They didn't ship coordination. ostk does.

PROBLEM

CONTAINERS

OSTK

Isolation

cgroups + namespaces

Capability pins + scoped agent stacks

Packaging

OCI image

Agentfile + signed release package

Coordination across instances

Bolt-on (Kubernetes, etc.)

Built into the kernel

Audit of actions

Out of band

Hash-chained event log per project

Identity & trust

Image signing (sigstore, etc.)

GPG web of trust, four signing tiers

Reproducibility

Image digest

journal.jsonl replay + signed claims

Resource bounds

CPU/memory limits

Token/turn budgets per agent

Not a wrapper around containers. Containers contain processes; ostk contains agents.

WHAT_YOU_GET

NO_LOST_WRITES

Compare-and-swap file edits. Conflicts surface; silent overwrites don't happen.

EVERY_ACTION_AUDITED

Append-only events, hash-chained, Ed25519-signed. Replay any session.

CAPABILITY_BOUNDS

Capability pins deny by default. Agents that try to escape get a hard no, logged.

NO_KEYS_IN_CONTEXT

API keys resolved from keychain at call time. Never in the agent's prompt.

SHARED_CACHE

Prompt cache shared across the fleet. One fetch, many agents.

LOCAL_FIRST

No cloud sync, no telemetry. Works offline. Your code stays on your machine.

START_HERE (/start/) VIEW_SOURCE (https://github.com/os-tack/ostk.ai)

---

# Why ostk — the three problems it solves

URL: https://ostk.ai/why/

DIAGNOSIS // 01

# Why ostk

ostk is a Rust binary that sits underneath whatever drives your agents. Three things break without it.

You're using AI agents to code. ostk fixes the three things below without changing how you drive them.

PROBLEM_01 // COLLISION

## They collide.

Two agents edit the same file. One finishes a second after the other. Whose version wins is whoever wrote last — silently. The first agent's work is gone, and nothing tells you it happened.

ostk's answer: the second writer always sees the conflict. Whichever agent edits stale gets a loud failure with a diff, not a silent loss. Nothing disappears between you and what your agents actually did.

How: optimistic concurrency at the filesystem layer (architecture (/docs/architecture/)).

PROBLEM_02 // DRIFT

## They drift.

Each agent runs in its own context window. They re-discover the same things. They forget what was already decided. They don't see each other's work. You repeat yourself across sessions and across agents.

ostk's answer: a shared state directory at `.ostk/`. Decisions, open questions ("needles"), agent identity, audit history — all observed by every agent that boots into the project. New agents resume context. Old agents leave breadcrumbs. The project develops a memory.

One observed state. The directory is the database.

PROBLEM_03 // NO_TRACE

## They leave no trace.

Something went wrong. Or right, and you want to reproduce it. There's no record of which agent did what against which version of which code, signed by whom. You can't replay. You can't audit. You can't prove what shipped.

ostk's answer: every action is an append-only event in `.ostk/journal.jsonl`, hash-chained and Ed25519-signed in-process. Replay it. Diff it across sessions. Sign a release against it. Capability boundaries (pin caps (/docs/pins/)) say what each agent is allowed to touch — escapes are denied and logged.

A signed trail. Bounded capability. Reproducibility from first principles.

## THE_SHAPE

One Rust binary, sitting underneath whatever drives your agents.

ostk doesn't replace Claude Code, Cursor, your script, or your local Llama. It sits under them. They write through it. They observe through it. The agent driver doesn't change — but coordination, audit, and capability appear underneath.

One install per machine. `ostk init` per project. After that:

- →Drive an agent over MCP (/docs/mcp/) — Claude Code, Cursor, anything that speaks the protocol.
- →Or use ostk's own TUI (/start/) with a local model (ollama, mlx) for an offline, no-API-bill workflow.
- →Or run a server and hit it from CI, scripts, or a remote machine.

Same kernel underneath all three. Same audit. Same trust boundaries. Different first 10 minutes.

## WHAT_YOU_GIVE_UP

The trade-offs the rest of the page didn't lead with.

A learning curve. The vocabulary — humanfile (/docs/humanfile/), agentfile (/docs/agentfile/), pin caps (/docs/pins/), needles (/docs/needles/), tack (/docs/tack-grammar/) — takes a session or two to internalize. The five-minute start (/start/) covers the floor; the kernel spec (/docs/kernel-spec/) covers the ceiling.

A daemon process. ostk runs a background daemon per project. Memory cost depends on what's loaded — a baseline daemon is tens of MB; with embeddings resident it can be hundreds. Use `ostk daemon restart` to swap a rebuilt binary in cleanly; `pkill -f "ostk daemon"` if you just want it gone.

A directory you can't ignore. `.ostk/` is the substrate. Lose it and you lose the audit chain (the project still works — you just lose history). Back it up like you'd back up `.git/`.

A small state footprint. Per-project state in `.ostk/`; per-machine state in `~/.local/share/ostk/` and `~/.cache/ostk/`. Nothing else.

## NEXT

→ START_HERE Pick a path. Three branches, ten to fifteen minutes each. (/start/) → HOW_IT_WORKS The model. Kernel, drivers, agents, the audit chain. (/docs/architecture/)

---

# Start here — pick your path

URL: https://ostk.ai/start/

INITIALIZATION // PICK_PATH

# Start here.

ostk is one binary. There are three ways to drive it. Same kernel underneath; the first ten minutes differ.

## UNIVERSAL // BEFORE_YOU_PICK

These two steps are identical for every path. Do them once per machine, then branch. If you already ran them from the homepage, skip to PICK_A_PATH.

STEP_1 // INSTALL

curl -fsSL https://ostk.ai/install.sh | sh

Auto-detects platform, downloads the binary, verifies the GPG signature against the operator's release key (/security/), installs to `~/.local/bin`. macOS (universal), Linux musl arm64/x86_64, Windows x86_64 zip.

STEP_2 // INITIALIZE_PROJECT

`cd ~/projects/your-repo && ostk init`

Creates `.ostk/` with default HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/), Agentfile (/docs/agentfile/), and pin caps. Use `ostk init --guided` for an interactive setup.

## PICK_A_PATH

MCP_BRIDGE — drive ostk from your existing agent tool (recommended) You already use Claude Code, Cursor, or another MCP-capable tool. Wire ostk in as an MCP server: your agent gets coordinated writes, audit, and capability boundaries while you keep your existing UI. Requires an MCP-capable agent driver. Best for: anyone with a working agent setup who wants ostk's substrate underneath. ~10 MIN (/docs/mcp/) LOCAL_TUI — chat with a local model in ostk's terminal UI Run a model on your machine via ollama or Apple's mlx. Use ostk's TUI as the chat interface. No API bill, no third-party model traffic. Slower first response (model warm-up), but everything stays local. Best for: local-first developers, offline work, hardware experimentation. ~15 MIN (/start/local/) SERVER_MODE — run ostk as a service for CI or remote agents Run `ostk daemon` on the host. Other tools — your CI runner, a remote machine over SSH-forwarded socket, a Discord bot — connect to the same Unix socket and drive agents. Same kernel, same audit; just exposed across machine boundaries. Best for: CI integration, multi-developer projects, server-side agent fleets. ~10 MIN (/start/server/)

Picked one and changed your mind? They share the same `.ostk/` state. Switch any time, even mid-project — the audit and pin caps follow you.

## AFTER_YOU'RE_RUNNING

TRUST_MODEL Tiers, GPG signing, the four-tier ladder. (/docs/trust-model/) NEEDLES Open questions and decisions across sessions. (/docs/needles/) COMMANDS Full CLI reference. (/docs/commands/)

---

# Start — Local Model TUI

URL: https://ostk.ai/start/local/

START_PATH // LOCAL_TUI

# Start // Local Model TUI

Point ostk at a model running on your machine. ostk's TUI becomes the chat surface; everything else — audit, capability bounds, secret handling — works the same as the hosted path.

## WHAT_YOU'LL_DO

01

Verify install and run ostk init in your project (universal steps from /start).

02

Pick a backend: ollama (cross-platform, default) or mlx (Apple Silicon-only, faster on M-series).

03

Pull or load a model. Tool-capable models recommended — not every local model supports function calling.

04

Spawn an agent with the model alias. ostk routes to the local server; audit + pin caps still apply.

05

Open ostk tui to chat. First response is slower (model warm-up); subsequent calls are fast.

Total time: ~15 minutes (most of it is model download). You need ostk v4.4.2+ and either ollama or mlx_lm installed locally.

BACKEND_01 // OLLAMA

## Run on ollama (default path)

ollama runs models on macOS, Linux, and Windows. It exposes an OpenAI-wire HTTP server at `localhost:11434` by default. ostk's OpenAI-wire driver routes models prefixed `ollama/` or `local/` to that endpoint.

Source: `src/cpu/providers.rs:74` (Ollama prefix routing), `src/cpu/openrouter.rs` (OpenAI-wire client).

1.1

### Install ollama

TERMINAL

```
# macOS / Linux
$ curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Or via Homebrew
$ brew install ollama
```

ollama auto-starts a daemon on macOS. On Linux, run `ollama serve` in a separate terminal.

1.2

### Pull a tool-capable model

Not every model on ollama supports function calling — and ostk needs function calling to dispatch tool calls. Verified-capable picks:

`qwen2.5-coder:32b` strong tool support, ~20GB on disk, ~32GB RAM at runtime

`qwen2.5-coder:7b` ~5GB on disk, fits on 16GB-RAM laptops, weaker tool reasoning

`llama3.1:70b` strong general, ~40GB on disk, needs ~64GB RAM

`llama3.1:8b` solid baseline, ~5GB on disk, fits on 16GB-RAM laptops

`deepseek-r1:70b` does NOT support tools — will fail

Tool support is per-model and per-build. Verified 2026-04. Check `ollama list --json | jq '.models[].capabilities'` for the current state on your machine.

TERMINAL

```
$ ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:32b
pulling manifest
pulling [================] 19.8 GB
verifying sha256
ok
```

1.3

### Spawn an agent with the local model

Pass the model alias prefixed with `ollama/`:

TERMINAL

```
$ cd ~/projects/your-repo
$ ostk init # skip if already done

$ ostk _agent --name worker --model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b \
--budget 10 \
--task "Explain the structure of this repo"
```

The `--budget` is a USD cap (`10` means $10). For local models the dollar bound is symbolic — there's no per-token cost — but the field is required and `0` / `unlimited` / negative are rejected at parse time. `_agent` is the lowest-level spawn primitive (hidden in `--help`); most users go through `ostk tui` or an Agentfile via `ostk run`. Source: `src/main.rs:1057-1073`, `src/commands/agent_cmd.rs:239`.

BACKEND_02 // MLX

## Run on Apple mlx (Apple Silicon only)

Apple's mlx framework runs quantized models on M-series chips. On M-series hardware mlx is typically faster than ollama for the same model size; pick mlx if you have the chip. ostk's primary mlx model is Ternary-Bonsai-8B (https://prismml.com/news/ternary-bonsai), a ternary-quantized model purpose-built for low-latency local inference — it ships as the kernel default. ostk routes `mlx/`-prefixed models to a `mlx_lm.server` at `localhost:11942` using the same OpenAI-wire format.

Source: `src/cpu/providers.rs:69-72` (MLX prefix routing), `src/cpu/mlx_lifecycle.rs` (server lifecycle), `src/commands/mlx.rs` (mlx-specific verbs).

2.1

### Install mlx_lm

TERMINAL

```
# Apple Silicon Mac required
$ pip install mlx-lm
```

2.2

### Start mlx_lm.server

ostk's MLX integration expects the server on `localhost:11942`. Set the model with `MLX_MODEL_ID`:

TERMINAL

```
# Primary: Ternary-Bonsai-8B (the kernel default)
$ MLX_MODEL_ID=prism-ml/Ternary-Bonsai-8B-mlx-2bit \
mlx_lm.server --port 11942

# Or a community 4-bit Coder model if you want a larger context
$ MLX_MODEL_ID=mlx-community/Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct-4bit \
mlx_lm.server --port 11942
```

ostk remaps the alias (e.g. `mlx/ternary-bonsai-8b`) to the wire model-id from `MLX_MODEL_ID`. Port 11942 is the ostk default for mlx (see env-vars (/docs/env-vars/)). Source: `src/cpu/providers.rs:22-26`.

2.3

### Spawn an agent with the mlx model

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk _agent --name worker --model mlx/ternary-bonsai-8b \
--budget 10 \
--task "Run cargo check and report"
```

Tool calls on mlx models come back inside the text output, not as a separate structured field — quantized models often skip the structured envelope. ostk extracts them either way. Same `--budget` semantics as the ollama path (USD cap; symbolic for local models). Source: `src/cpu/mod.rs:466` (enum), `:765` (mlx), `:789` (ollama).

03

## CHAT_IN_THE_TUI

Open the terminal UI and confirm the agent is running on your local model:

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk tui
ostk v4.4.2
fleet: 1 alive | worker (ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b)
needles: 0 open
boot confidence: 1.00 ◉

[you] _
```

Type at the prompt. The agent answers. Tool calls (file reads, edits, shell) flow through ostk's audit chain. Open `.ostk/journal.jsonl` in another window — every action lands there with `"model":"ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b"` for attribution.

Source: `src/main.rs:822,2273` (Tui command + dispatch).

## CAVEATS

Tool support is per-model Some local models (notably reasoning-only models like deepseek-r1) cannot emit tool calls. ostk will report "API error 400: model does not support tools." Switch to a tool-capable model.

Latency is higher A 32B-parameter model on a laptop is slower than Sonnet over the wire. First response includes model warm-up. Subsequent calls are fast — mlx tracks a LocalKV prompt cache; ollama relies on its own server-side warmth.

Context window is smaller Hosted Sonnet has 200k tokens; ollama models default to 128k (ostk's per-driver declared window — effective context depends on model + quantization); mlx Bonsai is 65k declared but lower in practice (see /docs/models). Long sessions hit context limits sooner — type :clear in the TUI to cycle context (tack verbs are TUI commands prefixed with a colon; see /docs/tack-grammar).

Cache reuse is local-only The fleet-shared prompt cache benefit only applies to hosted providers (Anthropic). Local models don't share cache across machines. The trade-off is privacy + zero-cost-per-token.

Quantization affects quality Generic mlx-2bit quants of full-precision models lose accuracy on coding tasks; prefer mlx-4bit or mlx-fp16 for those. Ternary-Bonsai is different — it is quantization-aware ternary, designed to run at low precision; the 2-bit variant is the intended deployment. ollama defaults to Q4_K_M which is a good general balance.

## TROUBLESHOOTING

"streaming error: request failed (localhost:11434)" ollama daemon is not running. macOS: open Ollama.app or run `ollama serve`. Linux: `systemctl --user start ollama` or `ollama serve` in another terminal.

"model does not support tools" Your model has no function-calling. Switch: `ostk _agent ... --model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b`.

"connection refused (localhost:11942)" mlx_lm.server is not running. Start it with `MLX_MODEL_ID=... mlx_lm.server --port 11942`.

Agent hallucinates tool calls but never invokes them mlx/ollama emit Hybrid-style tool calls (text-form). ostk parses them via extract_text_tool_calls. If the model is too small (<7B), it may emit malformed tool blocks. Try a larger model.

Out of memory during pull Disk space, not RAM. ollama models are 5–40 GB. Delete with `ollama rm <model>`.

Out of memory during inference Pick a smaller quantization (q4 instead of q8) or a smaller model (7b instead of 32b). Quality drops with quantization (see Caveats); 4-bit is the usual sweet spot.

ollama or olleh on a non-default port Set OLLAMA_HOST=http://localhost:NNNN (or OLLEH_HOST) before invoking ostk; the kernel reads these at driver-construction time. Source: src/cpu/mod.rs:834,988,996.

## WHAT'S_NEXT

→ MODELS Provider support matrix, capability tiers, alias resolution. (/docs/models/) → TUI Keybindings, panes, the tack bar, status ribbon. (/docs/tui/) → NEEDLES Cross-session memory: open questions, decisions, references. (/docs/needles/) → MCP_BRIDGE Combine local TUI with external MCP-driven agents. (/docs/mcp/)

RELATED: START_HERE (/start/) ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) COMMANDS (/docs/commands/)

---

# Start — Server Mode

URL: https://ostk.ai/start/server/

START_PATH // SERVER_MODE

# Start // Server Mode

The kernel daemon listens on a local Unix socket. Drive it from CI, a remote shell over SSH, or an external bot. Same audit chain, same capability bounds; just exposed across machine boundaries.

DOCUMENTATION_STATUS

This walkthrough is in progress. The pieces below are real and shipped at v4.4.2; the connecting narrative for CI and remote workflows is being written. Follow up on the linked references for the current state.

If you've wired ostk into CI or a Discord bot already and have notes that would help others, send them to os-tack/ostk-site issues (https://github.com/os-tack/ostk-site/issues).

## BUILDING_BLOCKS

The pieces that make server-mode real today.

`ostk daemon` canonical command

Starts the kernel daemon. Holds the socket at `.ostk/ostk.sock`, the process table, and the scheduler. Survives client disconnects. Run it on the host where the project lives. (`ostk listen` is a legacy alias.)

`ostk mcp` stdio bridge

The MCP stdio bridge any external client can launch as a subprocess. Forwards to the running daemon. See /docs/mcp (/docs/mcp/) for the inbound protocol and configuration.

`SSH socket-forward` remote driving

Drive a remote daemon by forwarding its Unix socket over SSH. Pattern: run `ostk daemon` on the server, then on your laptop:

TERMINAL

```
$ ssh -L /tmp/remote-ostk.sock:.ostk/ostk.sock user@server
```

Then point your local `OSTK_SOCKET` at `/tmp/remote-ostk.sock`. Audit lands on the server's `.ostk/journal.jsonl`; your laptop is just a thin client.

`ostk.ai.discord pattern` bot integration

The reference Discord-bot integration shows the bot-driving-daemon shape: bot connects to `.ostk/ostk.sock` over the MCP wire, dispatches user-typed commands as agents, posts back to the channel. Source: os-tack/ostk.ai.discord (https://github.com/os-tack/ostk.ai.discord).

`CI runner` in-process

For CI, the simplest pattern is in-process: the runner clones a repo with a populated `.ostk/`, calls `ostk init` if needed, and dispatches an Agentfile via `ostk run`. The daemon is local to the CI job; audit lands in the workspace and gets uploaded as a build artifact.

## WHAT_THIS_PAGE_NEEDS

- ·End-to-end CI walkthrough (GitHub Actions example).
- ·SSH socket-forward worked example with full env wiring.
- ·Multi-operator wiring (federation across machines, claim graph).
- ·Authentication / capability-pin model for remote callers.
- ·Discord-bot quickstart pulled out of the reference repo.

RELATED: START_HERE (/start/) MCP_BRIDGE (/docs/mcp/) SESSION_TOPOLOGY (/docs/session-topology/) ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/)

# System Architecture

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/architecture/

SECTION_01 // ARCHITECTURE

# System Architecture

Six problems show up the moment you put agents in front of a real codebase. ostk's architecture is the composition that solves them. Each section below names a problem, walks the composition that addresses it, and points at the primitives that compose.

For the verb-by-verb surface, run `ostk help` or `ostk abi list`. For normative spec text, see `docs/spec/llmos-posix-kernel.md` (https://github.com/os-tack/haystack/blob/main/docs/spec/llmos-posix-kernel.md). This page is the operator's reading guide — what composes into what.

PROBLEM_01 // DAEMON_DIES_MID_TASK

## Your daemon crashes while an agent is working. What happens to the work?

Nothing is lost. ostk writes the audit ring (`.ostk/journal.jsonl`) as a write-ahead log; every turn boundary writes a drain snapshot of the agent's state. When the daemon comes back up, `fold(journal.jsonl)` replays the log and rehydrates each session from its last drain. The client reconnects on the same socket; from the agent's perspective, the disconnect was a long pause.

COMPOSES_FROM

`.ostk/journal.jsonl` (WAL) · drain snapshots · Connection Continuity (invariant X) · Anchor Exclusivity (VIII).

Source: `src/util/audit.rs`, `src/serve/server.rs`, `src/kernel/drain.rs`.

PROBLEM_02 // AGENT_TRIES_TO_ESCAPE

## An agent gets creative and writes outside its lane. Then what?

Three layers compose. Pin caps deny by default at the tool layer — every `fs_ops` or `bash` call is checked before bytes hit disk. The OS sandbox (Landlock on Linux, Seatbelt on macOS) physically blocks writes outside allowed paths regardless of how the agent tries. Bypass detection flags any file that changed outside the kernel surface on next read. Tool-layer covers the expected path; the sandbox covers the escape path; bypass detection covers the unexpected. No single layer is load-bearing.

COMPOSES_FROM

pin caps (/docs/pins/) · tool-layer interception · nono sandbox · bypass detection.

Source: `src/serve/policy.rs`, `src/kernel/sandbox.rs`, `src/serve/tools/fs_ops.rs`.

PROBLEM_03 // PROVE_WHAT_SHIPPED

## You need to prove what an agent did, not just trust the log.

The audit chain isn't a log — it's the substrate's only truth. Every kernel-mediated action is an append-only event in `.ostk/journal.jsonl`, hash-chained against the previous row's signature. Each row is Ed25519-signed in-process at write time using the operator's identity key (no GPG subprocess, no signing latency). Replay reconstructs identical state byte-for-byte. The daemon is a cache; the journal is the ledger.

COMPOSES_FROM

WAL append → hash chain (`prev_hash`) → Ed25519 sign (`sig`) → replay reconstructs state. Owned State (II) makes the writer unique; Anchor Exclusivity (VIII) keeps two daemons from forking the chain.

Source: `src/util/audit.rs`, `src/kernel/signing.rs`.

PROBLEM_04 // TWO_AGENTS_ONE_FILE

## Two agents edit the same file at the same time. Who wins?

The second writer always sees the conflict. Every file carries a generation counter in `.ostk/gen_table.jsonl`. Edits are compare-and-swap: the agent declares the counter it read; if another agent has bumped it, the write fails loudly with a diff. For non-overlapping edits, Hot PR auto-merges; for overlapping edits, the second agent re-reads and re-tries. Nothing disappears between you and what your agents actually did.

COMPOSES_FROM

gen_table generation counters · OCC compare-and-swap · Hot PR three-tier merge · Path AND Handle (V) makes the file uniquely addressable to both readers.

Source: `src/kernel/cas.rs`, `src/kernel/hotpr.rs`, `src/serve/tools/fs_ops.rs`.

PROBLEM_05 // SHIP_TO_SOMEONE_ELSE

## You want to hand your agent setup to a different operator and have it just run.

`application/v1` packages an agent system as a content-addressed, signed directory with declared capabilities, supervisor trees, and a manifest of claims. The receiving operator runs `ostk app install`; the substrate validates the signature, registers the capability declarations, and the supervisor starts. The application's runtime existence IS its slice of the claim graph keyed by `(app_id, install_id, start_id)`. When the run finishes, the original publisher can verify what happened against the audit chain the receiving operator publishes.

"I can hand you my application as a signed file. You install it. You start it. It runs. When it finishes, I can verify what happened against the audit chain you publish." — application/v1 §1

COMPOSES_FROM

application/v1 manifest · Ed25519 attestation · claim-graph lifecycle (9 ratified schemas) · capability declarations · supervisor restart strategies.

Source: `docs/spec/llmos-application.md` (https://github.com/os-tack/haystack/blob/main/docs/spec/llmos-application.md) v0.1, `src/commands/app/`.

PROBLEM_06 // SEE_EVERYTHING_WITHOUT_POLLING

## You want to see what every agent did, in real time, without writing pollers or running queries.

The kernel writes the audit ring; indexers tap it as logical-decoding subscribers (Postgres-style); projections land at stable paths in a VFS namespace. You query a directory listing, not a log. `cat /fleet/proc` gives you the live process table; `ls /needles` gives you open work; every projection rebuilds from journal replay if it goes stale. No polling, no replication, no separate truth source.

COMPOSES_FROM

audit ring (WAL) · indexer subscribers in `dispatch::fire` · VFS namespace projections · forthcoming `kernel::registry` typed read API (EPIC →1652).

Source: `docs/spec/llmos-observation-substrate.md` (https://github.com/os-tack/haystack/blob/main/docs/spec/llmos-observation-substrate.md), `src/kernel/graph/indexer/`, `src/kernel/vfs/namespace.rs`.

THE_COMPOSITION

## Six problems, one shape.

Each answer above is two or three primitives composed against a clear failure mode. The kernel makes the composition mechanically possible — bounded waits, owned state, dead-process reaping, namespace isolation, handle uniqueness, anchor exclusivity, connection continuity, and four others (the ten invariants). The substrate makes the kernel queryable. The daemon makes it long-lived. The ABI makes it agent-callable. Drivers extend it. `application/v1` makes it portable.

What you don't have to know: which file holds which counter, which subsystem listens on which signal, which invariant is implemented in which phase. Those live in the kernel and the spec. The architecture is the composition story above; the rest is implementation.

→ KERNEL_SPEC (NORMATIVE) The ten invariants in full normative form. RFC 2119 keywords. Phase-by-phase rollout map. (https://github.com/os-tack/haystack/blob/main/docs/spec/llmos-posix-kernel.md) → OBSERVATION_SUBSTRATE (NORMATIVE) Audit ring, subscriber dispatch, VFS namespace contract. (https://github.com/os-tack/haystack/blob/main/docs/spec/llmos-observation-substrate.md) → APPLICATION_V1 (NORMATIVE) Signed manifest schema, claim graph, supervisor vocabulary, lifecycle verbs. (https://github.com/os-tack/haystack/blob/main/docs/spec/llmos-application.md) → MCP (BOTH DIRECTIONS) Inbound bridge for external clients; outbound proxy for ostk-driven agents. (/docs/mcp/)

RELATED: WHY_OSTK (/why/) SESSION_TOPOLOGY (/docs/session-topology/) NEEDLES (/docs/needles/) MCP_BRIDGE (/docs/mcp/)

---

# Five Laws

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/five-laws/

GOVERNANCE // FOUNDATIONAL_LAWS

# Five Laws

These are not guidelines. They are load-bearing constraints. Every design decision must satisfy all five. If a feature violates a law, the feature is wrong.

01

## The write path is invisible

Agents edit files normally. They use whatever tools their model provider gives them. The OS coordinates underneath. No new API. No coordination protocol. No SDK.

IN_PRACTICE Your agents never learn a new API. They write files. That's it.

02

## Agents are ephemeral

Agents crash. They hit context limits. They lose memory. They get killed. This is the expected operating condition. State lives in the filesystem. When an agent dies, nothing is lost.

IN_PRACTICE Session death does not mean work death. The OS survives its agents.

03

## Coordinate through the filesystem

No message buses. No inboxes. No pub/sub. No RPC between agents. If agent A needs agent B to know something, agent A writes a file. ls and cat are your debugging tools.

IN_PRACTICE The filesystem IS the communication channel. Every coordination artifact is a file you can read.

04

## Optimistic concurrency

No locks. No mutexes. Agents write freely and concurrently. When two agents edit the same file, the kernel detects the conflict via generation counters and resolves it. Conflict resolution, not conflict prevention.

IN_PRACTICE Agents never block each other. Two agents can edit the same file at the same time. The OS handles it.

05

## Invisible infrastructure, always

If you have to think about the OS, the OS failed. No config files to learn. No YAML to write. No dashboard to check. The install is one command. Init is one command. Boot is one command.

IN_PRACTICE Zero config, zero learning curve for agents. The OS works or it doesn't.

## Why laws, not principles

Principles are aspirational. Laws are constraints. A principle says "try to keep infrastructure invisible." A law says "if the agent learns a new API, the design is wrong."

The team tests every feature proposal against all five. No exceptions. No escape hatches.

RELATED: ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) CAPABILITY_PINS (/docs/pins/) TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/)

---

# Kernel Specification

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/kernel-spec/

SECTION_02 // KERNEL_SPEC

# Kernel Specification

The canonical architecture document. Seven primitives on a Unix substrate, Five Laws, a memory hierarchy mapped to LLM inference, and a dual-CPU scheduler.

## The Substrate

The kernel builds on battle-tested Unix primitives. Six syscalls are the foundation.

`rename(2)` Atomic file replacement (tmp + fsync + rename)

`flock(2)` Critical sections for CAS commits

`fsync(2)` Durability before rename

`O_APPEND` Lockless audit log append (under PIPE_BUF)

`stat(2)` Heartbeat liveness via mtime

`O_CREAT|O_EXCL` Exclusive create for lock files

## Seven Primitives

1

`atomic_write`

Substrate

Without it: Torn reads, crash inconsistency

2

`gen + CAS`

Coordination

Without it: Concurrent writers clobber silently

3

`Hot PR`

Coordination

Without it: Every concurrent edit needs human judgment

4

`audit log`

Coordination

Without it: No happens-before ordering, no attribution

5

`identity`

Coordination

Without it: Writer tags lost, agents collide on names

6

`digest injection`

Agent interface

Without it: LLM can't observe filesystem state cheaply

7

`304 elision`

Agent interface

Without it: Re-reads destroy context budget

## Memory Hierarchy

Classical computer memory mapped to LLM inference primitives.

Registers

Context window Active working memory Base rate

L1 cache

Prompt cache Fleet-shared prefix 10% read

RAM

.ostk/ files Durable kernel state Free

Disk

Source code Project truth Free

Swap

boot.md, history Cold context Free

The prompt cache at Anthropic's edge is content-addressed and per-organization. Two ostk processes on different machines with the same API key share cache entries. Adding agents decreases per-agent cost.

## Dual-CPU Scheduler

### Human CPU

LATENCYSeconds to hours

JUDGMENTAuthoritative

RINGRing 0

### LLM CPU

LATENCYSeconds/turn

JUDGMENTContext-bounded

RINGRing 3

Both are first-class execution resources. The CpuDriver trait abstracts the boundary — below it, the kernel doesn't know whether it's talking to a human via TTY or an LLM via HTTPS.

## Design Principles

01 Everything that matters goes in .ostk/ as a file with a documented schema

02 Every feature must be debuggable with jq, grep, awk, git

03 Byte-stability is existentially load-bearing for fleet cache economics

04 Agents are ephemeral; knowledge is persistent

05 Secrets never enter the LLM context

06 Everything is a file (Plan 9 literalized)

RELATED: ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) SESSION_TOPOLOGY (/docs/session-topology/) FIVE_LAWS (/docs/five-laws/)

---

# CLI Surface

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/commands/

REFERENCE // CLI

# CLI Surface

The CLI is two surfaces composed. Five workflows show how their verbs chain into what you actually do.

For verb-by-verb help, run `ostk help ` (System ABI; man pages) or `ostk --help` (operator overlay; clap-style). For the full agent-callable schema, `ostk abi list`. This page is about how the verbs compose.

WORKFLOW_01 // BOOT_A_PROJECT

## First time on a repo. Get a working setup.

Three commands. `ostk init` creates `.ostk/` with a humanfile, an Agentfile, and pin caps. `ostk daemon` starts the kernel; if it's already running, the next ostk RPC will reuse it. `ostk tui` attaches and gives you the live window — fleet, work queue, tack input.

TERMINAL

```
$ cd ~/projects/your-repo
$ ostk init # creates .ostk/ — humanfile, agentfile, pin caps
$ ostk daemon & # anchor process, holds socket + scheduler + registries
$ ostk tui # attach the terminal UI
```

COMPOSES_FROM

`init` (creates `.ostk/` substrate) · `daemon` (formerly `listen`; the anchor process) · `tui` (transient client; attach/detach freely).

Source: `src/commands/init.rs`, `src/commands_use/listen.rs`, `src/commands/tui.rs`.

WORKFLOW_02 // DRIVE_AN_AGENT_OVER_MCP

## You already use Claude Code, Cursor, or another MCP client. Wire ostk in once.

The kernel exposes itself as an MCP server over stdio. Your agent tool launches `ostk mcp` as a subprocess and the bridge forwards to the running daemon (or serves in-process if no daemon is up). After restart, the agent's tool surface gains `mcp__ostk__bash`, `fs_ops`, `read`, `search` and the rest of the kernel verbs — every call audited, every write gen-tracked, every escape pin-checked.

TERMINAL

```
# in your client's MCP config (e.g. .mcp.json)
{ "mcpServers": { "ostk": { "command": "ostk", "args": ["mcp"] } } }

$ ostk mcp diag # reconcile the kernel's tool surface with CORE_SEED_LANGUAGE
```

COMPOSES_FROM

`mcp` (stdio bridge to the daemon) · the System ABI (the verbs your client sees) · pin caps + gen_table layered transparently. Full wiring guide at /docs/mcp (/docs/mcp/).

Source: `src/main.rs` (`Commands::Mcp`), `src/serve/server.rs` (`run_mcp_bridge`), `src/commands_use/mcp_diag.rs`.

WORKFLOW_03 // RUN_A_ONE_SHOT

## You have an Agentfile that describes a worker. Run it.

An Agentfile is a declarative spec: identity, capabilities, model, budget, task. `ostk run` spawns the worker, applies pin caps from the file, and runs the agent loop until completion or budget cap. Every action lands in `.ostk/journal.jsonl` attributed to the worker. `ostk learn` can discover sandbox restrictions by observing the worker's filesystem access — useful when you don't know yet which paths the worker actually needs.

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk agentfile new fix-bug-27 > .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.toml
$ $EDITOR .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.toml # declare task, model, budget, pins
$ ostk run .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.toml
```

COMPOSES_FROM

`agentfile new / lint / show / validate` (lifecycle) · `run` (spawns the agent loop) · pin caps · the agent's drain snapshot at every turn boundary so the worker can be revived if the daemon restarts.

Source: `src/commands/run.rs`, `src/commands/agentfile_cmd.rs`, `src/commands/learn.rs`.

WORKFLOW_04 // OBSERVE_THE_FLEET

## Fleet's running. What's it doing right now?

`ostk ps` gives you the live process table with the Process Identity Quad — pid, ppid, pgid, sid — the same shape POSIX gave you for shell processes. `ostk pstree` renders the fleet as a tree rooted at live ppid edges. `ostk show ` is the universal query — find an agent, a needle, a decision, a commit by name. The audit chain itself is just `tail -f .ostk/journal.jsonl`.

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk ps # pid ppid pgid sid alias status
$ ostk pstree # fleet as a tree
$ ostk show fix-bug-27 # find anything by name/keyword
$ tail -f .ostk/journal.jsonl # the audit chain
```

COMPOSES_FROM

Process Identity Quad (invariant VI) makes `ps`/`pstree`/`pkill` mean what they mean in POSIX · the observation substrate projects journal rows onto a VFS namespace · `show` queries the projection. No polling, no replication.

Source: `src/commands/ps.rs`, `src/commands/pstree.rs`, `src/commands/show.rs`.

WORKFLOW_05 // SIGN_AND_SHIP

## You want to hand a snapshot to someone else and have them verify it.

`ostk bail pack` bundles a signed, portable OS package — Agentfiles, pin caps, the audit chain slice that produced them, your operator signature. Send the bail file. Recipient runs `ostk bail verify`; the chain replays, the signature checks, and they get a yes/no on whether what's inside matches what you sent. `ostk os audit` verifies your local journal end-to-end — every prev_hash, every Ed25519 signature.

`application/v1` generalizes this — a content-addressed signed application directory with declared capabilities and supervisor trees, installed via `ostk app *` verbs. Spec is ratified (https://github.com/os-tack/haystack/blob/main/docs/spec/llmos-application.md); CLI verbs land in a future release.

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk bail pack --out fix-bug-27.bail
$ scp fix-bug-27.bail other-machine:
other-machine $ ostk bail verify fix-bug-27.bail
$ ostk os audit # local: end-to-end journal integrity check
```

COMPOSES_FROM

`bail pack/verify` (signed package) · journal hash chain (audit-trail integrity) · Ed25519 in-process signing · `os audit` (replay verification) · `trust` namespace for key rotation and revocation.

Source: `src/commands/bail.rs`, `src/commands/os.rs` (`audit` subcommand), `src/kernel/signing.rs`, `src/util/audit.rs`.

THE_TWO_SURFACES

## System ABI + operator overlay.

Every verb above belongs to one of two surfaces. The System ABI is the agent-callable layer — 27 verbs at v1.0.0, schema'd, hash-stamped, frozen between major releases. Agents reach it through the MCP bridge. The operator overlay is the human-driver — clap-style commands free to evolve, frequently aliased and renamed (`ostk listen` → `daemon`, `ostk mcp call` → `driver call`, etc.). Verbs that begin with `_` (e.g. `_agent`) are kernel-internal primitives, hidden from `--help`.

SYSTEM_ABI

27 verbs. Agent-callable through MCP. Stable schema between majors.

`ostk abi list` — all 27 with signatures

`ostk abi show ` — full schema

`ostk abi version` — current ABI hash

`ostk abi diff ` — vs git ref

`ostk help ` — man page

OPERATOR_OVERLAY

Clap-style operator commands. Evolves freely. Free to alias, rename, deprecate.

`ostk --help` — full surface

`ostk --help` — per-verb

`ostk hs ` — short alias

`ostk show ` — universal query

`ostk inspect` — runtime introspection

The split is what lets the operator surface ship daily improvements while the agent-callable surface stays stable. Agents see ABI, not CLI. Verbs change names; ABI hashes change versions.

RELATED: ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) MCP (/docs/mcp/) AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) SESSION_TOPOLOGY (/docs/session-topology/)

---

# MCP — both directions

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/mcp/

REFERENCE // MCP

# MCP — both directions

ostk speaks MCP in both directions. Inbound: an external client (Claude Code, Cursor, anything) drives ostk as an MCP server. Outbound: ostk-driven agents call external MCP services (linear, github, postgres, etc.) via a userspace proxy.

## QUICK_REFERENCE

`ostk mcp` Run the stdio MCP bridge. Invoked by external MCP clients as a subprocess. Forwards to a running daemon, falls back to in-process serve.

`ostk mcp diag` Reconcile the kernel's MCP tool surface against `CORE_SEED_LANGUAGE`. Exits 1 on drift.

`ostk driver list` List configured outbound MCP servers from `HUMANFILE`'s `mcp:` section.

`ostk driver call ` Invoke a tool on a configured outbound MCP server (ephemeral subprocess).

`ostk driver serve ` Run an outbound MCP subprocess persistently on a Unix domain socket.

The outbound verbs were renamed from `ostk mcp {list,call,serve}` to `ostk driver {list,call,serve}` when `ostk mcp` (no subcommand) was reclaimed for the inbound bridge. Legacy `ostk mcp ...` still routes to the new `ostk driver ...` via `McpLegacy` dispatch in `src/main.rs`.

DIRECTION_01 // INBOUND_BRIDGE

## Drive ostk from an external MCP client

Your agent tool (Claude Code, Cursor, your own client) launches `ostk mcp` as a subprocess and speaks the MCP JSON-RPC protocol over stdio. The bridge translates that into kernel calls. Your agent gets gen-tracked writes, hash-chained audit, and pin-cap enforcement without changing its UI.

Source: `src/main.rs` (`Commands::Mcp { command: None }` branch — runs `run_mcp_bridge`), `src/serve/server.rs` (`run_mcp_bridge`, `bridge_stdio_to_daemon`). The bridge resolved the "two isolated kernels" problem (→1309 Phase 2B) — the Claude Code MCP process and `ostk daemon` used to have separate `ServerState`s that couldn't see each other's work.

### Configuration

Most MCP clients use the same `mcpServers` shape. Add this entry to your client's MCP config:

TERMINAL

```
{
"mcpServers": {
"ostk": {
"command": "ostk",
"args": ["mcp"]
}
}
}
```

Claude Code (project-scoped) `.mcp.json`

Claude Code (global) `~/.claude/settings.json`

Cursor `~/.cursor/mcp.json`

Other clients consult their MCP docs — schema is consistent

### Verify the surface

Restart the client. Ask the agent to list its tools. You should see ostk-namespaced tools appear:

EXPECTED_TOOLS (subset)

`mcp__ostk__bash` — kernel-mediated shell with audit + compression

`mcp__ostk__read` — file read with gen_table tracking

`mcp__ostk__fs_ops` — file mutation with OCC conflict detection

`mcp__ostk__search` — unified code/files/needles/decisions search

`mcp__ostk__lock`, `spawn`, `interact`, `session`, `tack`, `help` — coordination primitives

The authoritative list is `ostk mcp diag`. Tool count varies by kernel version. Names are namespaced as `mcp____` by the MCP client — so `mcp__ostk__bash` is the bash verb routed through your `ostk` server entry.

### First write — see the audit chain

Have the agent edit a small file. In your terminal:

TERMINAL

```
$ tail -f .ostk/journal.jsonl
{"event":"tool.fs_ops","path":"src/lib.rs","gen_before":4,"gen_after":5,"writer":"claude-code-1","ts":"2026-04-26T19:42:11Z","prev_hash":"...","sig":"..."}
{"event":"tool.bash","cmd":"cargo check","exit_code":0,"duration_ms":2841,...}
```

Each row is hash-chained against the previous (`prev_hash`) and Ed25519-signed in-process (`sig`). Two agents editing the same file get a CAS conflict instead of a silent overwrite. Capability boundaries from `.ostk/pins//pin.caps` apply uniformly.

### Inbound troubleshooting

Agent reports "no MCP server named ostk" Config file path or JSON syntax. Validate with `cat .mcp.json | jq .`. Restart the agent tool — most clients only read MCP config at startup.

Tools listed but every call fails Run `ostk mcp diag` directly. If it errors, check `ostk init` ran in this project — the bridge resolves the project root by walking up from CWD looking for .ostk/.

Writes succeed but no audit row appears You're probably writing through the agent's native filesystem tools, not the ostk-namespaced ones. Restrict the agent to mcp__ostk__* tools (most MCP clients have an allowlist).

Two daemons fight for the socket `ostk daemon restart` (swaps a rebuilt binary cleanly) or `pkill -f "ostk daemon"` then re-invoke. The bridge auto-recovers from a clean socket.

Permission errors on write Pin caps deny by default. Check `.ostk/pins/default/pin.caps`. T3 (no GPG identity) gets read-only by design — see /docs/trust-model.

DIRECTION_02 // OUTBOUND_PROXY

## Call external MCP services from ostk-driven agents

Your ostk-driven agent needs to talk to Linear, GitHub, Postgres, or any other MCP-speaking service. ostk's `driver` subcommand is a userspace proxy — agents invoke `ostk driver call ` and the proxy connects, sends one JSON-RPC request, prints the response, exits.

Deliberately at arm's length: the kernel never speaks MCP protocol bytes. Output flows through the same compression + audit pipeline as any shell output. Source: `src/commands/mcp_proxy.rs` (`run_list`, `run_call`, `run_serve`, `run_persistent_call`, `run_ephemeral_call`, `run_jsonrpc_session`).

### Configuration

Outbound MCP servers are declared in `.ostk/HUMANFILE` under an `mcp:` section (not `ostk.toml`). Simple key-value: name → command line.

TERMINAL

```
# .ostk/HUMANFILE

mcp:
linear: npx -y @anthropic/linear-mcp-server
github: gh mcp-server
postgres: npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres
```

Source: `src/commands/mcp_proxy.rs` lines 7–11, 53, 134–147 (HUMANFILE parsing).

### Execution modes

EPHEMERAL (default)

One subprocess per `ostk driver call`. Connect, JSON-RPC once, exit.

Higher latency per call, zero resident memory. Source: `mcp_proxy.rs` `run_ephemeral_call`.

PERSISTENT

`ostk driver serve ` runs a long-lived subprocess on a Unix domain socket; subsequent calls reuse it.

Lower latency, resident memory. Source: `mcp_proxy.rs` `run_serve`, `run_persistent_call`.

### How it works

01

Operator declares servers in HUMANFILE mcp: section. No kernel registration, no boot-time discovery.

02

Agent calls shell("ostk driver call linear list-issues"). The shell command runs as userspace subprocess.

03

ostk driver connects to the server over stdio, sends one JSON-RPC request, reads the response, exits. Output flows through the normal compression + audit pipeline as any shell output does.

04

Compare to built-in tools (file:edit, shell, etc.) which DO route through the kernel directly. Outbound MCP is deliberately at arm's length.

## DIRECTIONS_AT_A_GLANCE

ASPECT

INBOUND (ostk mcp)

OUTBOUND (ostk driver)

Direction

External client → ostk

Ostk-driven agent → external service

Transport

stdio (subprocess)

stdio (subprocess) or unix socket (persistent)

Tool scope

Kernel verbs (bash, fs_ops, read, search, ...)

Whatever the external server exposes

Audit path

Kernel-direct (every call → audit row)

Shell wrapper (call appears as bash row + nested response)

Pin cap enforcement

Yes (kernel-mediated)

No (the proxy does not impose ostk policy on the remote server)

Compression

Yes (uniform)

Yes (output passes through shell compressor)

Configuration

Client's MCP config (mcpServers)

.ostk/HUMANFILE mcp: section

RELATED: START_HERE (/start/) HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/) PIN_CAPS (/docs/pins/) ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) SECRETS (/docs/secrets/)

---

# Trust Model

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/trust-model/

REFERENCE // TRUST

# Trust Model

Trust composes from three layers: GPG-rooted identity tiers (who you are), capability pins (what you can touch), and Ed25519-signed audit (what you actually did). Below: four problems, four compositions, then the model.

The kernel determines your tier at boot — no UI, no flags, just GPG state. T0 anchors are hardcoded in the binary (ceremony cannot reach T0). T1 is cross-signed write access; T2 is GPG-without-cross-sign; T3 is anonymous. Source: `src/kernel/identity.rs`.

PROBLEM_01 // WHAT_CAN_I_DO_AS_INSTALLED

## I just installed ostk. What's the default?

T3 — anonymous, read-everything, write-blocked. Without a GPG key you can boot, observe the fleet, run commands that don't mutate, and read every file in `.ostk/`. Writes through kernel verbs (`fs_ops`, `bash` with redirect, `run`) are denied by the deny-all default in pin caps. The kernel announces your tier on every `ostk boot` — no ambiguity about what you have.

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk boot
trust: T3 (anonymous, read-only)
HUMANFILE: not found
boot confidence: 0.30 (restricted mode)
```

COMPOSES_FROM

Tier resolver (no GPG → T3) · pin caps with `deny write-everything` default · Owned State (invariant II) — there's no UI override for tier; the kernel decides.

Source: `src/kernel/identity.rs` (tier resolver), `src/serve/policy.rs` (pin cap evaluation).

PROBLEM_02 // GRADUATE_TO_WRITE

## I want to graduate from anonymous to write-capable on a project.

Two-step ceremony. First, generate a GPG key — that gets you to T2 (GPG present but uncrossed; reads everything, narrow write exceptions per pin caps). Second, get someone at T1 or T0 to cross-sign your key — that gets you to T1 (write access on the project they cross-signed for). The cross-signature is checked at boot via `gpg --check-sigs`; if it's there, the kernel reads your tier as T1.

TERMINAL

```
# T3 → T2: generate a GPG key
$ gpg --full-generate-key
$ ostk boot
trust: T2 (read + narrow exceptions)

# T2 → T1: have a T0 or T1 holder cross-sign your key
$ ostk trust sign # run by the cross-signer
$ ostk boot
trust: T1 (cross-signed, write access)
```

T0 is unreachable by ceremony. T0 fingerprints are hardcoded in `src/kernel/identity.rs` and shipped with the binary. To gain T0 your fingerprint has to land in a kernel release — that's the substrate's anchor of last resort.

COMPOSES_FROM

GPG key generation · cross-signature ceremony · `ostk trust sign` verb · kernel boot-time tier resolution · T0 hardcoded anchor list (5 fingerprints in v4.4.x).

Source: `src/commands/trust.rs` (sign / rotate / revoke), `src/kernel/identity.rs` (T0_TRUST_ANCHORS, `check_gpg_cross_signature`).

PROBLEM_03 // VERIFY_BEFORE_INSTALL

## I want to verify what I'm about to install.

Three layers compose. Binary signatures — every released tarball ships with an `.asc` GPG signature against the operator's release key, the install script verifies before extracting. Bail packages — `ostk bail verify` walks the embedded audit chain, checks every prev_hash and Ed25519 row signature, confirms the package contents match what was signed. Local audit — once installed, `ostk os audit` verifies your local journal end-to-end at any time. The verification path doesn't rely on trusting ostk; it relies on the math.

TERMINAL

```
# Binary install: signature checked automatically by install.sh
$ curl -fsSL https://ostk.ai/install.sh | sh
verifying GPG signature... ok

# Verify a bail package someone sent you
$ ostk bail verify ./incoming.bail
chain: 412 rows, prev_hash continuous, sig verified, T0 anchor matches

# Verify your own journal
$ ostk os audit
audit: 18,442 rows, hash chain ok, signatures verified
```

COMPOSES_FROM

Release-asset GPG signatures (.asc) · bail's embedded chain + Ed25519 row signatures · `os audit` replay · T0 anchor list as the verification root. No external CA, no network call.

Source: `src/commands/bail.rs`, `src/commands/os.rs` (audit subcommand), `src/util/audit.rs` (hash chain), `src/kernel/signing.rs` (Ed25519 in-process).

PROBLEM_04 // REVOKE_A_COMPROMISED_KEY

## My private key leaked. How do I revoke it without losing history?

The audit chain stays intact; what changes is what the substrate trusts going forward. `ostk trust revoke` records a revocation event in the journal — keyed by content hash and fingerprint, signed by an authority key (T0 if you can reach one, T1 with cross-sign capability otherwise). The kernel reads the revocation list at boot; rows signed before revocation remain valid (they were valid at write time) but the revoked key can no longer sign new rows. Generate a fresh key, get it cross-signed, you're back to T1 under a new identity.

TERMINAL

```
# Record the revocation
$ ostk trust revoke --reason "leak 2026-04-26"

# Cut a fresh key, restart the ceremony
$ gpg --full-generate-key
$ ostk trust sign # run by a T0/T1 holder
```

COMPOSES_FROM

Revocation event in the journal (content-hash + fingerprint) · authority signature on the revocation · kernel boot-time revocation list check · audit chain unchanged (history is what it was, signed at write time).

Source: `src/commands/trust.rs` (revoke subcommand), `src/kernel/identity.rs` (revocation list), `docs/spec/llmos-posix-kernel.md` Invariant III on the unchangedness of past audit rows.

THE_COMPOSITION

## Three layers compose into the trust model.

Every problem above composes the same three layers. Identity says who you are; capability pins say what you can touch; the audit chain says what you actually did. The kernel doesn't ask you to trust it — it gives you the math to verify each layer.

IDENTITY

Four GPG-rooted tiers. T0 hardcoded; T1 cross-signed; T2 GPG-without-cross-sign; T3 anonymous.

`src/kernel/identity.rs`

CAPABILITY

Pin caps deny by default. Tier sets the floor; pin caps (/docs/pins/) set the ceiling.

`src/serve/policy.rs`

AUDIT

Every action hash-chained, Ed25519-signed in-process. Replay with `ostk os audit`.

`src/util/audit.rs`

Why three? Because each layer fails differently, and a security model that conflates them is brittle. Identity tells you who's at the keyboard; pins tell you what they can do at this moment; audit tells you what they actually did. Identity can be revoked without invalidating history. Pins can change per project without changing identity. Audit is append-only and signed at write time — yesterday's truth doesn't change because today's keys did.

One historical note: GPG remains canonical for tier determination; Ed25519 is for per-action audit signing. The rationale: GPG carries cross-signing semantics (web of trust) that Ed25519 doesn't, and the operator's GPG keyring is the system the operator already trusts. Source: `src/kernel/identity.rs` doc-comment.

RELATED: PIN_CAPS (/docs/pins/) ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) BAIL (/docs/bail/) CLI (/docs/commands/)

---

# Pin Caps

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/pins/

# Pin Caps

Pin caps are the policy layer between identity and audit. Tier sets the floor (you can't write past it); custom pin profiles add ceilings on top. The merge is union of denies — pins never relax tier defaults.

Pin caps are evaluated at every write through the tool layer. They live at `.ostk/pins//pin.caps`; the active profile is selected by `OSTK_PIN` env var, or falls back to the tier-default. Source: `src/serve/policy.rs`, `src/kernel/file.rs`.

PROBLEM_01 // DEFAULT_BY_TIER

## I haven't written any pin caps. What does an agent get by default?

Whatever your tier-default pin allows. The kernel resolves your trust tier at boot (T0/T1/T2/T3 — see trust model (/docs/trust-model/)) and applies the matching default profile. T0 is unrestricted; T1 denies `write-kernel` and `modify-governance`; T2 also denies `write-src`; T3 denies all writes. You start safe, then opt into more permissive pins as needed.

TIER

WRITES_ALLOWED

DEFAULT_DENIES

T0

unrestricted

(none — full governance)

T1

project source

write-kernel, modify-governance

T2

narrow

write-kernel, modify-governance, write-src

T3

none

ALL writes (allows_write=false)

COMPOSES_FROM

Tier resolver (boot-time GPG check) · tier-default pin profile · pin caps evaluator at every write · Owned State (invariant II) — the kernel decides, no operator override.

Source: `src/kernel/identity.rs` (tier resolver), `src/serve/policy.rs` (pin caps evaluator), `src/kernel/file.rs` (write-time enforcement).

PROBLEM_02 // BOUND_A_WORKER

## I want a fix-bug worker to touch `src/` but not `infra/` or `secrets/`.

Three steps. `ostk issue_pin fix-bug` creates a profile at `.ostk/pins/fix-bug/pin.caps` and a tack-script at `pin.boot`. Edit the caps file: list the deny tokens (path-scoped) and any deny-verb lines. Reference the profile from the worker — set `OSTK_PIN=fix-bug` in the Agentfile or env. Every write through the kernel checks the active profile before bytes hit disk.

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk issue_pin fix-bug
created: .ostk/pins/fix-bug/pin.caps
created: .ostk/pins/fix-bug/pin.boot

$ cat .ostk/pins/fix-bug/pin.caps
deny: write-kernel modify-governance
deny-path: infra/ secrets/ .env*
deny-verb: :shutdown
deny-verb: bail pack --private

# Apply to the worker:
$ OSTK_PIN=fix-bug ostk run .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.toml
```

COMPOSES_FROM

`issue_pin` command · pin.caps file format (deny tokens, deny-path, deny-verb) · `OSTK_PIN` env var selects active profile · merge with tier-default at evaluator time.

Source: `src/commands/issue_pin.rs`, `src/serve/policy.rs`.

PROBLEM_03 // MERGE_ORDER

## My pin tries to permit `write-src` but the agent still gets denied. Why?

Pin caps are attenuation only. The kernel takes the union of deny tokens — your tier-default's denies plus your custom profile's denies. A pin can add restrictions but cannot remove them. A T2 user setting `OSTK_PIN=permissive` still gets `write-src` denied because T2's tier-default contains it. Tier is the floor; pins are extra ceiling.

Worked example for a T2 operator with custom pin `"infra-eng"`:

```
tier-default (T2): deny: write-kernel modify-governance write-src
infra-eng pin: deny: write-kernel deny-path: secrets/
(no allow lines — pins can't have them)
union (effective): deny: write-kernel modify-governance write-src
deny-path: secrets/
```

The agent gets strictly more restrictive — write-src denied (from tier) plus secrets/ denied (from pin). To unlock write-src, the operator has to graduate to T1 — that's the whole point of the trust ladder.

COMPOSES_FROM

Tier-default (floor) + custom pin (ceiling) → union of denies → final cap set. Attenuation-only merge semantics; pins never relax tier defaults.

Source: `src/serve/attenuation.rs`, `src/serve/policy.rs`.

PROBLEM_04 // DISCOVER_THE_RIGHT_PINS

## I don't know what paths my worker actually needs. How do I find the minimal pin?

`ostk learn` runs the worker in observation mode — it watches every filesystem call the agent makes and emits the minimal set of `LIMIT` directives that would have permitted that run. You read the directives, decide what to keep, drop into the Agentfile or pin.caps. Empirical bounding instead of guesswork. The technique generalizes: run learn over your representative tasks; compare the union of paths; pin accordingly.

TERMINAL

```
# Run the worker under observation (60s default, --duration overrides)
$ ostk learn .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.toml --duration 120

# Output: minimal LIMIT directives the agent actually needed
LIMIT read-path src/ tests/ Cargo.toml
LIMIT write-path src/ tests/ target/
LIMIT verb cargo build cargo test

# --apply writes them back into the Agentfile directly
$ ostk learn .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.toml --apply
```

COMPOSES_FROM

Observe mode (nono sandbox in passthrough-with-logging) · path-set extraction from observed syscalls · `LIMIT` directive emission · optional `--apply` to write back to the Agentfile.

Source: `src/commands/learn.rs`, `src/kernel/sandbox.rs` (observation mode).

THE_COMPOSITION

## Tier sets the floor. Pins add ceilings. The kernel evaluates.

Three primitives compose into the answer for "what can this agent actually do?" — your tier-default pin from the GPG trust check (the floor; can't relax), your custom pin profile selected by `OSTK_PIN` (the ceiling; only adds restrictions), and the evaluator that checks every write at the tool layer before bytes hit disk. Combined with the OS-level sandbox covered in architecture problem 02 (/docs/architecture/#problem_02_agent_tries_to_escape), that's three layers of defense for every kernel-mediated action.

CAPABILITY_TOKENS_REFERENCE

`deny: write-kernel` — all writes under `.ostk/`

`deny: modify-governance` — `.primefile`, `GOVERNANCE.md`, `ENTITYFILE`, HUMANFILE, Agentfiles

`deny: write-src` — project source files

`deny-path: ` — literal or prefix-match path block

`deny-verb: ` — exact or prefix-match command block

Pin profiles are the per-worker knob. Tier defaults are the per-operator floor. The trust model determines tier; the operator authors profiles; the evaluator merges and enforces. None of the three is load-bearing on its own.

RELATED: TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/) AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) CLI (/docs/commands/)

---

# Approval Chain

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/approval-chain/

REFERENCE // APPROVAL_CHAIN

# Approval Chain

Every tool call an agent requests passes through seven ordered checks before it runs. First match wins. The chain is deliberately sequenced from cheapest-and-most-certain (pin.caps deny) to most-expensive-and-most-ambiguous (user prompt). The whole sequence lives in `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs` lines 1470–1680.

## PRELUDE // CAPABILITY_CLASSES

Before the chain runs, every tool call is classified into one of eleven capability classes ordered by privilege. The classifier lives at `src/cpu/mod.rs:557–696`.

`FileRead` Read, Glob, Grep — no side effects

`FileEdit` Edit — mutates existing files in place

`FileWrite` Write — creates or overwrites files

`ShellRead` cat, ls, ps, git log — read-only shell

`ShellWrite` cp, mv, rm, echo > file, git commit

`ShellExec` curl, cargo build, npm install — process/network

`ShellSecret` gpg, ssh-keygen, ostk secret — key material

`KernelRead` ostk ps, ostk clock, ostk show — read-only kernel state

`KernelWrite` ostk commit, ostk hay, ostk work add — mutates kernel state

`KernelSpawn` ostk run, ostk spawn — creates agent processes

`KernelSecret` ostk secret — key material through kernel

## SEVEN_STEPS

01

### PIN_CAPS_HARD_DENY

For write-class capabilities (FileEdit, FileWrite, ShellWrite), the kernel loads pin.caps via load_pin_caps_result() and calls denies_write(target_path). If the token list includes write-kernel, modify-governance, or write-src, and the path matches, the call is denied immediately.

BEHAVIOR_UNDER_FAILURE

If OSTK_PIN is set but pin.caps fails to parse, PinCapsResult::Error returns true — fail-closed. Source: agent_loop.rs:1513–1533, 1522 (Error → true).

source: `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1504–1564`

02

### DESTRUCTIVE_CHECK

For ShellWrite, ShellExec, ShellSecret, and KernelSpawn classes, extract the command string and call destructor::detect_destructive(cmd). Returns Some if the command matches a destructive pattern. A fired dangerous signal (→1245) forces this check even for ShellRead. If destructive_hit is true, the call is prompted — always, regardless of later steps.

BEHAVIOR_UNDER_FAILURE

The destructive hit overrides every later auto-approve path. An Autonomous agent with a destructive command still prompts. Source: agent_loop.rs:1691–1693 ("destructive always prompts").

source: `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1565–1592, 1691`

03

### KERNEL_READ_AUTO

If the capability class is exactly KernelRead (read-only kernel state: ostk ps, ostk clock, ostk show), the call is auto-approved. This is the only class that gets a free pass regardless of mode or allow-list — kernel reads have no side effects and need no supervision.

BEHAVIOR_UNDER_FAILURE

KernelWrite, KernelSpawn, and KernelSecret do NOT get this treatment. They fall through to the mode gate. Source: agent_loop.rs:1593–1595.

source: `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1593–1595`

04

### PERMISSION_MODE_GATE

Four modes defined at src/cpu/mod.rs:517–527. Autonomous → allow all. Auto → allow reads (FileRead, FileEdit, FileWrite, ShellRead, KernelRead). Governed → deny all (continue chain). Plan → deny all except KernelRead (which was already allowed at Step 3).

BEHAVIOR_UNDER_FAILURE

A dangerous signal overrides Auto mode's auto-approval — a dangerous ShellRead still prompts. Plan mode short-circuits with a terminal deny at agent_loop.rs:1619–1636. Source: agent_loop.rs:1596–1617, 1601–1617.

source: `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1596–1636, src/cpu/mod.rs:517–527`

05

### SESSION_ALLOW_LIST

runtime_allowed is a Mutex<HashSet<String>> held on the session. When the operator approves a call with the "a" key (approve all for this class), the tool name goes into this set. Subsequent calls to the same tool name are auto-approved.

BEHAVIOR_UNDER_FAILURE

In-memory only. Not persisted to disk. Revoked on daemon restart or explicit mid-session revocation via TUI. Source: agent_loop.rs:1637–1642.

source: `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1637–1642`

06

### AGENTFILE_TOOL_PATTERNS

config.tool_patterns is a Vec<(class_label, Option<path_pattern>)> parsed from the Agentfile TOOL directives. A pattern matches if the class label equals the call's class AND (the pattern is None for bare class match, OR the target path contains the pattern as a substring). Privileged classes (ShellExec, ShellSecret, KernelSpawn) additionally require Autonomous mode — pattern match alone is not enough.

BEHAVIOR_UNDER_FAILURE

Substring match, NOT glob or regex. TOOL shell:write(src/) matches any target path containing "src/". Full TRUST hierarchy (pin > HUMANFILE > default) is a TODO at agent_loop.rs:1670–1673. Source: agent_loop.rs:1643–1690.

source: `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1643–1690`

07

### USER_PROMPT

If none of the prior steps allowed the call, the approval source (the TUI modal in interactive use, or a mock in tests) is asked. The modal renders agent alias, tool name, and the raw input JSON preview. Keybindings: y = approve, n = deny, a = approve all for this class (adds to Step 5 allow-list), Enter = deny (safety default).

BEHAVIOR_UNDER_FAILURE

No approval source configured → the call is denied by default. Source: agent_loop.rs:1705–1711 and src/fcp_screen/action.rs:286 (Enter → deny).

source: `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1705–1711`

## DECISION_FLOW

The actual match, copied from `agent_loop.rs:1691–1703`:

needs_approval decision

```
let needs_approval = if destructive_hit {
true // Step 2: destructive always prompts
} else if kernel_auto {
false // Step 3: kernel:read auto-approved
} else if mode_allows {
false // Step 4: mode gate auto-approved
} else if is_session_allowed {
false // Step 5: session allow-list
} else if agentfile_allows {
false // Step 6: Agentfile pattern match
} else {
true // Step 7: fall through to user prompt
};
```

Step 1 (pin.caps hard deny) happens earlier and short-circuits with a terminal deny — it never reaches this decision block. Step 1 is structurally different from 2–7: it denies, where 2–7 decide between "allow silently" and "prompt the user."

## MODE × CLASS AUTO-APPROVE MATRIX

Which combinations auto-approve (✓) vs require a prompt (?). Destructive commands force a prompt regardless of this table. Source: `agent_loop.rs:1596–1617`, `src/cpu/mod.rs:517–527`.

CAPABILITY_CLASS PLAN GOVERNED AUTO AUTONOMOUS

FileRead ? ? ✓ ✓

FileEdit ? ? ✓ ✓

FileWrite ? ? ✓ ✓

ShellRead ? ? ✓* ✓

ShellWrite ? ? ? ✓

ShellExec ? ? ? ✓

ShellSecret ? ? ? ✓

KernelRead ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

KernelWrite ? ? ? ✓

KernelSpawn ? ? ? ✓

KernelSecret ? ? ? ✓

✓ auto-approved by the mode gate.
? falls through to session allow-list → Agentfile patterns → user prompt.
✓* ShellRead auto-approves in Auto mode UNLESS the dangerous signal fires, in which case it prompts (→1245 PR-A, agent_loop.rs:1601–1606).
KernelRead is the only class that gets auto-approved at Step 3 regardless of mode — visible as ✓ across the whole row.

RELATED: TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/) CAPABILITY_PINS (/docs/pins/) AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) ISOLATION_TIERS (/docs/isolation-tiers/)

---

# Isolation Tiers

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/isolation-tiers/

REFERENCE // ISOLATION_MODEL

# Isolation Tiers

Four containment levels, plus a credential-proxy pattern at the API edge. What your Agentfile asks for, what the kernel actually enforces, and what still says "reserved" in the parser.

Live today: Tier 0 (unsandboxed, default) and Tier 1 (nono sandbox, opt-in via `ISOLATION nono`).

Tier 2 (Docker) and Tier 3 (Firecracker microVM) are parsed but reserved. The `ISOLATION docker` and `ISOLATION firecracker` directives validate in `src/agentfile/parser.rs:77–80`, but the spawn path does not wire them up yet. Firecracker has a draft POC in `src/commands/vm_init.rs` and `src/commands/firecracker.rs`; Docker has neither. Using a reserved value today falls back to Tier 0 behavior.

## FOUR_TIERS

T0

### UNSANDBOXED

`ISOLATION none` LIVE — default

MECHANISM

No kernel enforcement. MCP tool layer is the only boundary. If an agent fork/exec's a shell, it has whatever filesystem and network access the OS gives the parent process.

ESCAPE_SURFACE

Trivial. Any tool can shell out and bypass pin caps. Use only when you already trust the agent (local, signed Agentfile) and the surrounding process is itself sandboxed.

source: `src/agentfile/parser.rs:349 (IsolationTier::None default)`

T1

### NONO_SANDBOX

`ISOLATION nono` LIVE — Phase S1

MECHANISM

Process-level kernel enforcement via the nono crate. Landlock on Linux (kernel ≥ 5.13), Seatbelt on macOS (sandbox_init). Fallback: seccomp-notify on pre-6.1 kernels — weaker, can stall if supervisor hangs.

ESCAPE_SURFACE

Any unauthorized open()/unlink()/write() returns EACCES at the syscall layer. Works even if the agent exec()s a raw shell — Landlock/Seatbelt enforce for the process and all descendants. Must be applied before any I/O (async-signal-safe on Linux).

source: `src/kernel/sandbox.rs:1–169, apply() at 157–159`

T2

### DOCKER

`ISOLATION docker` RESERVED — parsed, not wired

MECHANISM

Namespace isolation (PID/mount/network/UTS) + cgroups. Shared kernel. ~500 ms boot, ~10 MiB overhead per spec.

ESCAPE_SURFACE

Namespace escapes require a kernel CVE or misconfigured mount. Safer than Tier 1 for untrusted code, but still one kernel bug away from host.

source: `src/agentfile/parser.rs:77 (parsed), no spawn path`

T3

### FIRECRACKER_MICROVM

`ISOLATION firecracker` DRAFT POC — parsed, reserved

MECHANISM

Hardware isolation via KVM. Separate kernel per agent. Host launches a microVM (qemu-system + Alpine rootfs), virtiofs shares .ostk/, vsock ports 52 (boot) and 55 (identity) for the handshake. Linux native; macOS uses TCP emulation.

ESCAPE_SURFACE

Highest bar: requires breaking the hypervisor itself. Host does not trust the guest. Guest runs PID 1 init that mounts /proc, /sys, virtiofs.

source: `src/commands/vm_init.rs:1–11, src/commands/firecracker.rs:1–100, parser.rs:65–67`

## How nono Actually Works

The only tier that currently enforces anything. Mechanical steps:

01

Parser reads ISOLATION + TOOL + LIMIT directives from the Agentfile. Builds a CapabilitySet (sandbox.rs:72–139).

02

Universal allowlist: /usr, /lib, /lib64, /bin, /sbin, /etc, /System (read-only). Project cwd (read). /tmp, /private/tmp, /var/tmp (read-write). Phase S1 enforces this universal set; fine-grained TOOL-derived caps are deferred.

03

Kernel forks the agent subprocess.

04

Before any I/O, the child calls nono::Sandbox::apply(caps). On Linux this invokes the Landlock LSM; on macOS it calls sandbox_init.

05

Irreversible. Kernel enforces for the process and every descendant. Unauthorized syscalls return EACCES. No way to relax without killing the process.

Source: `src/kernel/sandbox.rs:1–169`, `src/agentfile/parser.rs:112–175` (build_sandbox_config). Phase S1 notes on deferred fine-grained caps: sandbox.rs:14–18.

## Not a Tier: The Credential Proxy

The v1 spec mentions "cognitive isolation" and "cache partition per API key" alongside the four process-level tiers. These are not isolation tiers in the ostk code — they are design patterns for credential handling at the API boundary:

- The agent never sees the real API key. A nono-sandboxed proxy loads it from the system keystore and injects it into outbound requests over a Unix socket.
- The socket uses `SO_PEERCRED` verification so only the authorized agent process can speak to the proxy.
- Keys are held in `Zeroizing` to prevent memory leaks.
- Every proxied call is written to `.ostk/journal.jsonl`.

See `docs/spec/nono-agent-isolation.md §5` for the design. Implementation is split across `src/kernel/secret.rs` and the proxy wire-up. This is credential containment, not process containment.

## ISOLATION × PERMISSIONS

Isolation tier pairs with a permission mode from `LIMIT permissions`. The tier decides what the process can do at the OS layer; the mode decides how much human oversight each tool call gets.

ISOLATION governed (default) supervised restricted

none Full access, POST verify only Human approves each tool call Read-only tools

nono Auto-expand caps within kernel policy Human per-call, Landlock enforced Read-only, Landlock enforced

docker (reserved) (reserved) (reserved)

firecracker (draft POC) (draft POC) (draft POC)

Most common real-world combo: `ISOLATION nono` + `LIMIT permissions governed` — least-privilege process sandbox, autonomous execution, kernel-enforced policy for capability expansion. Source: `src/agentfile/parser.rs:201–207, 622–626`.

## EXAMPLE

agents/lint-fix.af

```
FROM claude-sonnet-4-6

PROMPT Fix all ESLint warnings in src/.

TOOL shell
TOOL file:read
TOOL file:edit

LIMIT budget_usd 2
LIMIT permissions governed

ISOLATION nono
```

The agent boots into a Landlock-sandboxed process. It can read the universal allowlist plus cwd, write to /tmp and the project tree through the file:edit kernel primitive, but any raw shell command that tries to touch `/etc/passwd`, `~/.ssh`, or the kernel state directory returns EACCES. No TOOL-level deny list required — the kernel enforces it below the tool layer.

RELATED: AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) CAPABILITY_PINS (/docs/pins/) TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/) ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/)

---

# Agentfile

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/agentfile/

# Agentfile

An Agentfile is a Dockerfile for agents. Plain-text directives in a stable order; the kernel reads it once, signs it, and supervises everything that follows. Below: four problems an operator has when authoring one, and the compositions that answer them.

Agentfiles live at `.ostk/agents/.Agentfile` (per-project workers) or `.ostk/profiles/.Agentfile` (reusable templates). The lifecycle verbs are `ostk agentfile new / list / show / lint / validate`. Run with `ostk run `. Source: `src/commands/agentfile_cmd.rs`, `src/commands/run.rs`.

PROBLEM_01 // WRITE_YOUR_FIRST

## I want to spawn a worker for one task. Where do I start?

`ostk agentfile new fix-bug-27` scaffolds a starter Agentfile from a profile template. Edit two things: the `FROM` directive (which model — anthropic, openrouter, ollama/, mlx/) and the `TASK` directive (the prompt that defines the work). Optional: tighten `LIMIT budget_usd` and `LIMIT turns`. Then `ostk run` spawns the worker.

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk agentfile new fix-bug-27
created: .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.Agentfile

$ cat .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.Agentfile
FROM claude-sonnet-4-6
LIMIT budget_usd 2
LIMIT turns 30
LIMIT wall_clock 600
TASK Fix the bug described in needle →27.
Write a regression test, run cargo check, commit when green.

$ ostk run .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.Agentfile
```

COMPOSES_FROM

`agentfile new` (scaffolder) · the FROM/LIMIT/TASK directives (the spec) · `run` (spawns the agent loop) · pin caps from `OSTK_PIN` or tier-default · drain snapshots at every turn.

Source: `src/commands/agentfile_cmd.rs` (`new` subcommand), `src/commands/run.rs`.

PROBLEM_02 // DECLARE_THE_FULL_SPEC

## What can I actually declare? Beyond model + task.

Six directive families compose. Each one corresponds to a substrate concept the kernel will enforce — they're not hints, they're contracts. Most workers need only FROM + LIMIT + TASK; the rest are for fleet workers, supervisor trees, and reusable profiles.

FROM required Which model. anthropic prefixes (claude-sonnet-4-6), openrouter prefixes, ollama/, mlx/, or a profile reference (FROM-PROFILE base-coder).

LIMIT optional Resource bounds. budget_usd (rejected at parse time if 0/unlimited/negative), turns (agent-loop iterations), wall_clock (seconds), tokens (cumulative).

TASK required The prompt that defines the work. Multi-line via continuation. The agent loop sees this as its initial user message.

PIN optional Active pin profile name. Equivalent to setting OSTK_PIN=<name> at spawn. Resolves to .ostk/pins/<name>/pin.caps.

RESTART optional Supervisor restart strategy: one_for_one, one_for_all, rest_for_one, simple_one_for_one. Used by the application/v1 supervisor when this agent is part of a tree.

HEALTHCHECK optional Liveness probe. Cross-validates with RESTART and LIFETIME. The lint pass catches incompatible combinations.

COMPOSES_FROM

Directive parser · per-directive validators (e.g. `parse_budget_usd` rejects 0/unlimited/negative) · cross-directive linter (RESTART/LIFETIME/HEALTHCHECK consistency) · the kernel's runtime contract for each declared field.

Source: `src/commands/agentfile_cmd.rs` (lifecycle), `src/serve/attenuation.rs` (LIMIT parsing), `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs` (runtime enforcement).

PROBLEM_03 // SHARE_ACROSS_PROJECTS

## I keep retyping the same scaffolding. How do I share it?

Profiles. `.ostk/profiles/.Agentfile` is a reusable template. Any per-project Agentfile can `FROM-PROFILE base-coder` to inherit its directives. The receiving Agentfile can override or extend; conflicts go to the layered-spec resolver. Profiles can be machine-wide (under `~/.local/share/ostk/profiles/`) for cross-project reuse, or shipped as part of a signed bail.

TERMINAL

```
# Create a reusable profile
$ cat .ostk/profiles/base-coder.Agentfile
FROM claude-sonnet-4-6
LIMIT budget_usd 5
LIMIT turns 50
PIN default

# Inherit from it in a per-task worker
$ cat .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.Agentfile
FROM-PROFILE base-coder
LIMIT budget_usd 2 # override: tighter for this one
TASK Fix bug →27 ...
```

COMPOSES_FROM

Profile directory layout · `FROM-PROFILE` directive · layered-spec resolver (per-task overrides profile; profile overrides built-in defaults) · profile signing for cross-machine portability.

Source: `src/commands/agentfile_cmd.rs` (FROM-PROFILE resolution), `src/commands/migrate.rs` (legacy FROM-PROFILE migration, →1422).

PROBLEM_04 // VERIFY_BEFORE_RUN

## I want to know my Agentfile is valid before I burn an LLM call on it.

Three layers of pre-flight. `ostk agentfile show` renders the parsed result with the signature status — fast sanity check. `ostk agentfile lint` runs cross-directive rules: RESTART without LIFETIME, HEALTHCHECK against an unreachable verb, FROM-PROFILE that points at a non-existent profile, LIMIT keys with malformed values. `ostk agentfile validate` is the full pre-flight: parse + sign-verify + lint, returns an OAE.4-style outcome envelope you can pipe into a CI gate.

TERMINAL

```
$ ostk agentfile show .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.Agentfile
parsed: 5 directives, 0 unknown
signature: not signed (T2 author)
```
