```
profile chain: base-coder → built-in defaults

$ ostk agentfile lint .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.Agentfile
ok: 5 directives, 0 lint failures

$ ostk agentfile validate .ostk/agents/fix-bug-27.Agentfile
{"verdict":"pass","parse":"ok","sign":"unsigned-ok","lint":"ok"}
```

COMPOSES_FROM

Parser (directive grammar) · signer (Ed25519 in-process) · cross-directive linter · OAE.4 outcome envelope for machine-readable verdicts. Same machinery `ostk run --dry-run` uses.

Source: `src/commands/agentfile_cmd.rs` (show / lint / validate subcommands), `src/util/oae.rs` (outcome envelope format).

THE_COMPOSITION

## A Dockerfile for agents.

Plain-text directives, ordered, parsed, signed, supervised. The Dockerfile analogy holds at every layer. `FROM` picks your base. `LIMIT` is your resource cap. `FROM-PROFILE` is your `FROM ` for templating. The lint pass is your build-time validation. The kernel signing is your image-digest equivalent. `ostk run` is your `docker run`.

Where the analogy diverges: Agentfiles aren't built into immutable artifacts at author time. They're parsed and signed at spawn time — the kernel reads the file, hashes its content, signs the hash with the operator's identity key, records the signed hash in the journal. Two spawns of the same Agentfile produce two distinct claim-graph entries with the same content hash. The audit chain is what makes the agent's runtime existence verifiable; the file is just the seed.

`application/v1` generalizes Agentfiles into supervisor trees — multi-worker setups with restart strategies and shared budgets. Spec ratified at `docs/spec/llmos-application.md` (https://github.com/os-tack/haystack/blob/main/docs/spec/llmos-application.md); the application/v1 lifecycle verbs (`ostk app *`) land in a future release.

RELATED: TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/) PIN_CAPS (/docs/pins/) CLI (/docs/commands/) ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/)

---

# HUMANFILE

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/humanfile/

CONFIGURATION // IDENTITY

# HUMANFILE

Your compiled identity. The OS reads this at boot and calibrates to you. Not a config file — a governance document.

Location: `.ostk/HUMANFILE` (project) or `~/.HUMANFILE` (global)

## EXAMPLE

```
# HUMANFILE

IDENTITY yourname
SIGN YOUR_GPG_FINGERPRINT

MODEL claude-opus-4-6
FALLBACK mistral-large-latest

AVAILABLE REQ Your username. Used in audit attribution and agent identity.

`SIGN` — GPG key fingerprint. Establishes T0 trust tier when verified.

`MODEL` REQ Default model for agent sessions. Kernel auto-detects provider from model name.

`FALLBACK` — Model when primary is unavailable or rate-limited.

`AVAILABLE — Heredoc listing all authorized models. Consumed by FROM auto in Agentfiles.

`SECRET — Heredoc listing authorized API key names. Keys stored in platform keychain.

`DRIVER ` — FCP driver to load at boot. Internal drivers don't need declaration.

`EMBED` — Embedding index mode: codebase (full), incremental (git-diff), off.

`PREPROCESS` — Local model for preprocessing user input via olleh runtime. e.g. apple/default. Fixes grammar, expands tack shorthand before the remote LLM sees it.

`TRUST` — Trust policy for Agentfile signing. unsigned skips signature requirement.

`EXTENDS` — Path to parent HUMANFILE for inheritance. Child directives override.

`BOOT` — Custom boot steps appended after kernel initialization.

`VERB` — Custom verb registration merged into .language at boot.

## SECRET_RESOLUTION_ORDER

01

BYO_SECRET_MANAGER

Set OSTK_SECRET_CMD to your tool (bw, op, pass)

02

PLATFORM_KEYCHAIN

ostk secret set KEY stores in macOS Keychain / secret-tool

03

ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLE

Fallback to $KEY in shell environment

RELATED: AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) SECRETS (/docs/secrets/) ENV_VARS (/docs/env-vars/)

---

# .primefile

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/primefile/

CONFIGURATION // ROOT_OF_TRUST

# .primefile

The kernel's identity document. The OS writes it. You read it to verify what's running.

Location: `.ostk/.primefile`

The `.primefile` is dual-signed: once by the kernel's GPG key (T1), and once by the operator's personal GPG key (T0).

Changes require re-signing by both keys, in person. Modifying it manually invalidates the signature and may cause the kernel to refuse to boot.

## EXAMPLE

```
---BEGIN HAYSTACK.PRIME KERNEL DECLARATION---

KERNEL: @project.prime
VERSION: 3.0.0
CREATED: 2026-03-10T00:00:00Z

AUTHORITY:
Primary: @operator (Human operator, HUMANFILE authority)
Kernel: @project.prime (Persistent kernel, GOVERNANCE.md authority)
Co-signers: Both keys required for mutation

KEYS:
T0 Human Root:
Name: Your Name
Key: AAAA...1111
Algorithm: ed25519

T1 Kernel:
Name: @project.prime (v3.0)
Key: BBBB...2222
Algorithm: ed25519

LINEAGE:
Root: @operator GPG signature
Co-sign: @project.prime GPG signature
Chain: Unbroken from 2026-03-10

---SIGNATURES BELOW---
```

## FIELDS

`KERNEL` Kernel identity name. Default: @project.prime.

`VERSION` Kernel declaration version.

`AUTHORITY` Who controls the kernel — primary (human), kernel, and co-signer requirements.

`KEYS` GPG key registry: T0 human root, T1 kernel, T1 CI. Algorithm and cross-signature info.

`LINEAGE` Root of trust chain, co-signers, and chain continuity date.

## SIGNATURE_VERIFICATION

TERMINAL

$ gpg --verify .ostk/.primefile.asc .ostk/.primefile

$ gpg --verify .ostk/.primefile.t0.asc .ostk/.primefile

Both verifications must pass. A single-sig primefile (T1 only) is not trusted for production. The chain runs: human T0 key → kernel root key → CI key.

DO_NOT_EDIT

Unlike HUMANFILE and ENTITYFILE, you do not write the primefile. The kernel owns it. Manual modification invalidates the signature and may prevent boot.

RELATED: HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/) ENTITYFILE (/docs/entityfile/) OSTK_TOML (/docs/ostk-toml/)

---

# ENTITYFILE

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/entityfile/

CONFIGURATION // GOVERNANCE

# ENTITYFILE

The trust constitution of your OS instance. Written once. Governs who can do what.

Location: `.ostk/ENTITYFILE`

## TRUST_TIERS

T0

Human operator

Full governance — including modifying ENTITYFILE itself

Fingerprint hardcoded in src/kernel/identity.rs trust anchors

T1

Verified agent or CI

Write source, run agents, delegate needles. Denied: write-kernel, modify-governance

GPG key cross-signed by a T0 holder

T2

GPG key, not cross-signed

Read everything. Narrow write exceptions. Denied: write-kernel, modify-governance, write-src

GPG key present, check_gpg_cross_signature returns None

T3

No GPG key

Boots; reads everything; all writes denied by pin caps (policy.rs:248)

host_identity::discover() returns no gpg_key

## EXAMPLE

```
ENTITYFILE: v1.0 (GPG chain signed — kernel@project.prime)
---
entity:
name: @project.prime
key: AAAA1111BBBB2222CCCC3333DDDD4444EEEE5555
domain: project.prime
version: 3.0.0
boot_signature: FFFF0000AAAA1111BBBB2222CCCC3333DDDD4444
laws:
- invisible-write
- ephemeral
- filesystem
- OCC
- invisible-infra
harness: terminal
tool_pattern: native Bash
dialect:
- tack
- hay
fleet:
alive: 1
agents: 54
signing_key: 3689831ac67b433093ef15e23431b539e7222961f7f976a14feb268c8058ce6a
signing_algo: ed25519
```

## WHEN_YOU_NEED_IT

Solo developer, one machine NO

Solo developer, GPG identity needed NO

Team with shared repo YES

CI pipeline dispatching agents YES

Multiple humans with different trust YES

## The Negotiate Protocol

Changes require ceremony. You cannot unilaterally modify ENTITYFILE — even as T0.

01

File an offer describing the change (.ostk/offers/)

02

Counter-party reviews and responds

03

Both parties sign with their keys

04

Merge with --no-ff to preserve ceremony in git history

## Authority Chain

T0 (human)

→

kernel

→

agents

Trust flows downward. No agent can elevate its own trust tier. No agent can modify ENTITYFILE. The human is always the final authority.

RELATED: HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/) PRIMEFILE (/docs/primefile/) TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/)

---

# ostk.toml

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/ostk-toml/

CONFIGURATION // PROJECT

# ostk.toml

The project-level configuration file. Lives at the repository root. You write and maintain this. The kernel reads it at boot.

Location: `./ostk.toml`

## EXAMPLE (real ostk-site config)

TERMINAL

```
[project]
name = "ostk-site"
bail = "os-tack/ostk.ai"

kernel = ">=3.0.0, Project name and optional bail lineage (the upstream signed package this project was seeded from). Top-level kernel = ">=X.Y.Z" pin sits alongside.

`[authorization]`

Declarative trust policy: github_gpg_required, trust_tiers list. Documentary — source of truth is src/kernel/identity.rs trust anchors.

`[distribution]`

Release mirrors, signature verification, tarball pattern. Used by install scripts and bail fetchers, not the runtime kernel.

`[kernel]`

boot_mode (embedded-first | daemon-first), daemon_lifecycle, laws. The laws array is documentary — the actual five laws are enforced in code.

`[onboarding]`

install.sh ceremony steps. Documentary — consumed by the install script, not the kernel.

`[pins.*]`

Named pin configurations. read/write/execute paths and deny tokens. Parsed by the pin loader at boot.

`[features]`

Boolean toggles: tui, agents, audit, digest, embeddings. Read at boot to gate subsystem initialization.

`[mcp.*]`

Historical. MCP servers are declared in HUMANFILE, not ostk.toml — see /docs/mcp for the real path.

RELATED: MCP_PROXY (/docs/mcp/) CAPABILITY_PINS (/docs/pins/) HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/)

---

# Secrets

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/secrets/

CONFIGURATION // SECRET_MGMT

# Secrets

Secrets never enter the LLM context. The kernel resolves keys at call time and injects them via subprocess environment variables.

Authorized keys declared in `HUMANFILE SECRET SET

$ ostk secret set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

Enter value: ●●●●●●●●

✓ stored in macOS Keychain (service: ostk, account: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY)

LIST

$ ostk secret list

ANTHROPIC_API_KEY ✓ keychain

GEMINI_API_KEY ✓ keychain

OPENROUTER_API_KEY ✗ not found

TEST

$ ostk secret test ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

✓ resolves (sk-ant-●●●● 48 chars)

## RESOLUTION_ORDER

01

BYO_SECRET_MANAGER

secret_cmd from .ostk/config (e.g. bw, op, pass). Highest priority.

OSTK_SECRET_CMD env var supported for backward compat

02

PLATFORM_KEYCHAIN

macOS: security find-generic-password. Linux: secret-tool lookup.

ostk secret set stores here

03

ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLE

Fallback to $KEY in shell environment.

Least secure — visible in process table

## Output Masking

All tool output passes through `secrets::mask()` before the LLM sees it. Secret values are replaced with `sk-ant-●●●● (48 chars)` format — prefix preserved, length hinted, value hidden.

Masking covers: Bash output, file reads, error messages, environment dumps. Minimum 8 characters to mask.

## KNOWN_API_KEYS

`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` anthropic

`GEMINI_API_KEY` google

`MISTRAL_API_KEY` mistral

`OPENROUTER_API_KEY` openrouter

`OPENROUTER_MANAGEMENT_KEY` openrouter-mgmt

`OPEN_ROUTER_API_KEY` openrouter-alt

`OPENAI_API_KEY` openai

`MODEL_API_KEY` universal

RELATED: HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/) TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/)

---

# Bail (signed bundles)

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/bail/

REFERENCE // BAIL_PACKAGES

# Bail

A portable bundle pulled from the stack. Each file inside carries its own detached signature. Move identity and boot state between instances — no shared filesystem, no SSH, no cloud sync.

## What Bail Is

A bail of hay pulled from a haystack — compressed, bundled, ready to move. The kernel is `haystack`; a bail is what you extract from the stack, wrap up, and send somewhere else.

Mechanically: a tar.gz archive renamed `.bail`, containing identity files (`.primefile`, `ENTITYFILE`), boot state (`.boot/INIT`), a signed manifest, and — depending on mode — either encrypted kernel state (`os.bin`) or selective exported content (needles, decisions, docs). Every signable file has its own detached signature alongside it.

## THREE_MODES

PUBLIC_MODE

`ostk bail pack --public`

Portable public snapshot. Ed25519-signed manifest; any receiver can verify with the sender's public key. No encrypted payload — safe to distribute.

CONTAINS

- `.boot/INIT` — tack boot protocol
- `.primefile` — signer + recipient fingerprints
- `ENTITYFILE` — identity chain
- `transition/` — GPG↔Ed25519 bridge docs

FULL_MODE

`ostk bail pack`

Public contents plus `os.bin` — encrypted internal kernel state. Requires a 40-char recipient fingerprint in `.primefile`; without one, pack prints a warning and falls back to public mode. Full-mode unpack is fail-closed: missing signature, missing gpg, or unknown Ed25519 key all refuse to unpack.

ADDS

- `os.bin` — encrypted tarball of internal state

EXPORT_MODE

`ostk bail pack --export @target`

Selective knowledge transfer. Packs needles, decisions, and docs matching an optional query. Receiver merges them with `[I]` imported markers. GPG-signed.

CONTAINS

- `needles.jsonl` — filed work items
- `decisions.jsonl` — append-only decisions
- `docs/` — selected doc files

## PACKAGE_FORMAT

BAIL_LAYOUT (public mode, actual output)

```
ostk.bail/
.boot/INIT # bootloader protocol
.primefile # signer + recipient fingerprints
.primefile.asc # GPG detached sig over .primefile
ENTITYFILE # identity chain (signing key → issuer)
transition/ # GPG↔Ed25519 bridge documents
transition.json # key rotation manifest
transition.json.self.sig # Ed25519 signature
transition.json.asc # GPG signature (cross-compat)
sequence # canonical signing order
manifest.json # version, created, signer, mode, files list
manifest.json.self.sig # Ed25519 signature (primary for public/full)
---- full mode adds: ----
os.bin # encrypted internal kernel state
---- export mode replaces content with: ----
needles.jsonl # + needles.jsonl.asc
decisions.jsonl # + decisions.jsonl.asc
docs/ # selected doc files
manifest.json.asc # GPG signature (export mode)
---- optional: ----
manifest.json.sigstore # Sigstore attestation (--attest)
```

Packed as a `.tar.gz` renamed to `.bail`. Each signable file gets its own detached signature file alongside it, not a single manifest-level signature — so a verifier can selectively check any subset and still trust the rest.

## NEVER_INCLUDED

Internal kernel state and secrets are excluded from all bail packages. Private keys never appear in any file, log, or error message.

`journal.jsonl` Internal audit trail

`sessions/` Ephemeral session logs

`agents.jsonl` Runtime agent state

`hwm.jsonl, hwm.lock` High-water mark (runtime)

`gen_table.jsonl, gen_table.lock` OCC generation counters

`identity_counter, identity.lock` Runtime identity counters

`dispatch.json` Kernel dispatch table

`*.secret, secrets/` Any secret files or secret directory

## Receive and Unpack

01

`Receive ostk.bail from another instance (email, scp, signed URL — any transport)`

02

`ostk bail verify ostk.bail — checks every present signature (.self.sig Ed25519, .asc GPG), prints per-sig status`

03

`ostk bail unpack ostk.bail — reads manifest.mode up front, then branches: full mode is fail-closed, public/export is advisory. TOFU-pins the source identity on first use`

04

`Public/full: applies .boot/INIT + .primefile. Export: re-IDs needles into local numbering, appends decisions with imported marker`

05

`ostk boot — reports merged OS state. The source signer is now locked; future bails claiming the same identity must match`

Verification strictness follows the bail's mode (Option C, landed in haystack commit `bf69847`). Full-mode unpack is fail-closed: missing signature, missing gpg binary, or an unknown Ed25519 public key all refuse to unpack. Full bails carry encrypted cross-machine state and must verify against a pre-shared key. Public and export mode unpack are advisory: unsigned bails still circulate for development, and TOFU-on-first-use pins the signer. A mismatched signature is fatal in every mode — present-but-invalid is always a tamper signal. Source: `src/commands/bail.rs:245-371`.

## VERIFICATION

Output captured from `ostk 3.0.0`. Every signature present in the bail is checked; missing signatures are reported as neutral, not failures.

PUBLIC_MODE_BAIL (Ed25519 only)

$ ostk bail verify ostk.bail

ostk bail verify: /Users/scott/haystack/ostk.bail

Ed25519: VERIFIED (inline)

GPG: no manifest.json.asc in bail

created: 2026-04-11T04:56:41Z

files: 8

EXPORT_MODE_BAIL (GPG signed)

$ ostk bail verify haystack-export.bail

ostk bail verify: /tmp/haystack-export.bail

GPG: VERIFIED

signer: gpg: Good signature from "Scott Meyer <scott@ostk.ai>" [ultimate]

created: 2026-04-11T04:55:15Z

files: 3

Note: the Ed25519 line reads `VERIFIED (inline)` because Ed25519 verification happens inside the kernel process — no shell-out to a GPG subprocess. GPG verification calls `gpg --verify` and quotes its output. Both mechanisms must succeed when both signatures are present.

## COMMANDS

`ostk bail pack` Default: full mode. Includes os.bin if .primefile has a recipient fingerprint; otherwise silently falls back to public mode.

`ostk bail pack --public` Public snapshot: .boot/INIT + .primefile + ENTITYFILE + transition/ + manifest. Ed25519-signed. Safe to distribute.

`ostk bail pack --attest` Add a Sigstore attestation (manifest.json.sigstore) alongside the primary signature. Combines with any pack mode.

`ostk bail pack --export @identity [--query ] [--include needles,decisions,docs] [--out ]` Export mode: selective knowledge transfer. Packs matching needles, decisions, and/or docs. GPG-signed; receiver merges with [I] imported markers.

`ostk bail unpack ` Verify all present signatures, then merge state. Public/full: apply .boot + .primefile. Export: re-ID needles into local numbering, append decisions as imported.

`ostk bail verify ` Verify without unpacking. Checks every signature (Ed25519 .self.sig + GPG .asc + Sigstore .sigstore); prints per-sig status.

RELATED: PRIMEFILE (/docs/primefile/) TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/) SESSION_TOPOLOGY (/docs/session-topology/) GOVERNANCE (/governance/)

---

# Session Topology

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/session-topology/

SECTION_03 // SESSION_MGMT

# Session Topology

Sessions outlive everything that drives them — clients disconnect, daemons crash, workers hang. Four problems, four compositions, one shape: the daemon is a cache, not a ledger; the journal is the truth.

PROBLEM_01 // DAEMON_CRASHED_DURING_WORK

## My agent was 12 turns into a task. The daemon crashed. What now?

Nothing is lost. Every turn boundary writes a drain snapshot to `.ostk/drain/.json` — full message history, token accounting, model + tools + permissions config. When the daemon restarts, it acquires the anchor lock, marks stale sessions `Orphaned`, then `rehydrate_revivable_orphans` reads the drain snapshots and rebuilds each session from its last turn. The agent picks up at turn 13 with full context.

COMPOSES_FROM

Drain snapshots at every turn boundary · lineage state machine (`Active → Orphaned → Reaped`) · revival policy (`revive` default, `reap`, or `ask`) · Anchor Exclusivity (invariant VIII) keeps two daemons from forking lineages.

Source: `src/kernel/drain.rs`, `src/kernel/anchor.rs` (`orphan_previous_lineages` + `rehydrate_revivable_orphans` + `reap_unmanaged_orphans`).

PROBLEM_02 // ISOLATE_BLAST_RADIUS

## I want to spawn a fleet for one task without it seeing the rest of the project.

Sub-stacks isolate work by scope. `.ostk/stacks//` contains its own journal, its own drain directory, its own inbox, and its own nudges. Agents inside the sub-stack see only that scope; the parent anchor sees only the inbox/nudge channel. Token budgets, capability pins, and audit lineage all stay scoped. When the sub-stack seals, the journal collapses into the parent as a single attestation.

COMPOSES_FROM

Scoped journal + scoped drain + inbox/nudge IPC channels · Root Namespace Isolation (invariant IV) · sub-stack state machine (`Spawning → Active → Sealed → Reaped`, or `Failed`).

Source: `src/kernel/sub_stack.rs`, `src/kernel/sandbox.rs`.

PROBLEM_03 // CLIENT_DISCONNECTED

## My TUI lost the socket. Is the agent still running?

Yes. Clients are transient by design — TUI, MCP bridges, CLI one-shots, IDE extensions all attach and detach without affecting the underlying session. The anchor holds session state in memory; clients are subscribers. Reattach over the kernel socket with `client/attach` and pick up the live event stream from where you left off. The anchor's lifecycle is decoupled from any client's.

COMPOSES_FROM

Anchor as long-lived process · transient-client JSON-RPC protocol (`client/attach`, `client/detach`, `client/subscribe`) · Connection Continuity (invariant X) makes reattach lossless.

Source: `src/serve/server.rs`, `src/serve/client.rs`, `src/kernel/anchor.rs`.

PROBLEM_04 // WORKER_HUNG_MID_FLIGHT

## A worker stopped responding. Do I wait for a daemon restart?

No — recovery happens in-process. Each session writes a heartbeat at every kernel-mediated step. The scheduler's tick loop watches the heartbeat table; when it expires past threshold, the worker is marked crashed, reaped, and hot-rehydrated from its last drain snapshot — all without restarting the daemon. The heartbeat is the timeout mechanism Bounded Wait (invariant I) requires; the rehydrate path is the same one crash recovery uses.

COMPOSES_FROM

Heartbeat at every kernel call · scheduler tick loop · drain-snapshot rehydrate · Bounded Wait (I) gives the heartbeat its deadline · Subreaper (III) reaps the dead worker process.

Source: `src/kernel/scheduler.rs`, `src/kernel/heartbeat.rs`.

THE_COMPOSITION

## Four problems, one shape.

Every answer above composes the same four primitives: the anchor (long-lived daemon process holding the socket and the in-memory projection), the lineage (stable identity for ongoing work — `scheduler`, `worker-a` — outliving both client and anchor), the drain snapshot (reconstructable state at every turn boundary), and the sub-stack (isolation boundary for scoped work).

What stays true across all four: the anchor is a cache, not a ledger. The journal + drain snapshots on disk are the truth. `ostk daemon restart` swaps a rebuilt binary cleanly without losing audit lineage. Reset the anchor and the registries rebuild from journal replay; reset the registries and the kernel keeps writing.

REVIVAL_POLICY

```
LIMIT revival_policy revive # auto-recover from drain snapshot (default)
LIMIT revival_policy reap # drop on orphan — don't recover
LIMIT revival_policy ask # leave orphaned, operator decides
```

Set in the Agentfile per worker. Ask-pending lineages: `ostk lineage resolve --revive` or `--reap`.

RELATED: ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) KERNEL_SPEC (/docs/kernel-spec/) AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/)

---

# Agent Lifecycle

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/agent-lifecycle/

REFERENCE // AGENT_LIFECYCLE

# Agent Lifecycle

Agents are ephemeral by design. State lives in the filesystem and the audit log. The kernel owns a small state machine that moves each agent from healthy execution through context pressure into a graceful finalization turn, then death.

## STATE_MACHINE

Five states, no regression. Once an agent advances, it cannot go back. Source: `src/kernel/lifecycle.rs:56–76, 132–135`.

HEALTHY

TRIGGER

Default state at spawn. Context < 70%.

BEHAVIOR

Normal execution. All tool calls permitted, no instrumentation overhead.

AGING

TRIGGER

Context ≥ 70% (AGING_THRESHOLD_PCT). lifecycle.rs:22

BEHAVIOR

Still executing. The kernel begins preparing a handoff record for a successor agent. No compaction — the agent's context is not mutated mid-turn.

DYING

TRIGGER

Context ≥ 90% (DYING_THRESHOLD_PCT). Context jumps (e.g. 60% → 92%) skip AGING and go straight to DYING. lifecycle.rs:23, 138–140

BEHAVIOR

Critical. No new tool calls allowed beyond the finalization turn. Handoff data is finalized.

DRAINING

TRIGGER

One finalization turn allowed after DYING.

BEHAVIOR

Single last tool call permitted — typically the handoff write. After this turn completes, the state advances to DEAD.

DEAD

TRIGGER

Finalization turn complete, or context pressure past recovery.

BEHAVIOR

All tool calls fenced. The agent process is a zombie until the daemon reaps it.

Transitions are guarded by `LifecycleState::can_transition_to()` at `lifecycle.rs:66–76`. The transition driver is `lifecycle.evaluate(context_pct)`, called from dispatch at `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1839`.

## How Context Pressure is Detected

The kernel does not auto-compress context mid-turn. Context pressure is a signal, not an intervention point. The mechanism:

- Each turn, `PreCallTokenCount` and `Usage` events update the session's token state.
- Dispatch computes `context_pct` from those counts and calls `lifecycle.evaluate(context_pct)`.
- If the threshold crosses, the state advances monotonically (Healthy → Aging → Dying → Draining → Dead). A sudden jump can skip states — a 60% → 92% crossing goes straight from Healthy to Dying without stopping at Aging.
- The current turn still completes. Fencing only kicks in when the next turn is requested.

Context pressure triggers a handoff — a structured record the next agent inherits — not in-flight compaction. The design choice is explicit: mutating context mid-turn would defeat the prompt cache and surprise the model.

Source: `src/kernel/lifecycle.rs:22–23` (thresholds), `src/cpu/agent_loop.rs:1839` (call site), `src/kernel/lifecycle.rs:138–140` (skip behavior, test coverage at line 460).

## SPAWN

01

Operator runs ostk kernel spawn <name> --model sonnet --budget 5 "task description". Or internally via ostk _agent.

02

Daemon fork-exec's a subprocess (not an in-process thread). The agent is a real OS process with its own PID.

03

Agent writes .ostk/agents/<name>.meta with real PID and config. agent_cmd.rs:33–46.

04

Agent binds a UnixListener at .ostk/agents/<name>.sock. This is how detachable clients attach. agent_cmd.rs:88–96.

05

Lineage ID is the alias passed via --name. Lineage = the stable identity the daemon tracks across restarts; it outlives any single process.

## HEARTBEAT_AND_CRASH_DETECTION

Timestamp-based, not socket-based. Each agent writes its `last_seen` ISO8601 to `.ostk/agents.jsonl` on every turn. The daemon interprets staleness:

ACTIVE

last_seen < 30s ago. Agent is actively ticking.

STALE

30s–90s. Idle but probably alive. Daemon does not intervene yet.

CRASHED

> 90s. Daemon calls `kill(pid, 0)` to distinguish idle-alive from actually-dead.

Source: `src/kernel/heartbeat.rs:47–54, 89–91` (thresholds and is_alive probe). Turn-based fallback state per agent at `.ostk/.heartbeat.` is tracked at `heartbeat.rs:228–240`.

## Drain Snapshots

At every turn boundary (after each assistant response completes), the session hook writes a snapshot to `.ostk/drain/.json`. Wave G.3 flipped this to default ON — previously opt-in via `OSTK_DRAIN_SNAPSHOT=1`. Source: `src/kernel/drain.rs:102–111`, hook at `src/cpu/session.rs:1052`.

### SNAPSHOT_V2_CONTENTS

```
lineage_id # stable identity
anchor_id # daemon instance that wrote this
written_at # ISO8601 timestamp
model # LLM model string
session_tokens # input/output/cache_read/cache_create cumulative
messages # full conversation history
config # pruned LoopConfig: tools, max_tokens, permission_mode,
# thinking, citations, tool_patterns, revival_policy
```

What's not in a snapshot (deliberately ephemeral):

- `root`, `preload_context` — daemon-local pointers regenerated at boot
- `pending_images`, `outbox` — transient turn state
- `runtime_allowed` — session approval list, rebuilt per run
- `task_handle`, `cancel_flag`, event channels — tokio primitives, not serializable

Source: `src/kernel/drain.rs:81–88, 192–221`.

## KILL_AND_REAP

Two different operations. Kill terminates a running process; reap compacts the registry after the process is already dead.

KILL (session_proctable)

- SIGTERM to the process group (negative PID). session_proctable.rs:161.
- Five-second grace period. session_proctable.rs:170–171.
- SIGKILL fallback if the process is still alive. session_proctable.rs:179.

No final drain write — session_proctable handles in-process background tasks.

REAP (ostk kernel reap)

- Read `.ostk/agents.jsonl`, probe each "active" entry with `kill(pid, 0)`. reap.rs:77–213.
- For entries whose process is already dead: flip status, remove from active set.
- Rewrite a compacted `agents.jsonl`.
- Call `cleanup_agent_state()` (heartbeat.rs:304) to remove per-agent files.

Reap is a post-mortem sweep. It does not send signals — if a process is still running it's left alone.

## Crash Recovery

When the daemon restarts, `SessionManager::with_anchor` reads drain snapshots for orphaned lineages and rehydrates them. Source: `src/kernel/drain.rs:21–27`.

- Messages and token state are restored from the snapshot.
- Config is either refreshed from the live Agentfile template or carried over from the snapshot.
- `SnapshotV2::upgrade_from_v1()` handles pre-G.1 snapshots (config=None on upgrade). drain.rs:253–264.
- After successful rehydrate, the snapshot is cleared. drain.rs:29–33.

Honest caveat: recovery is snapshot-to-restart-point only. Tool calls that happened after the last snapshot write are lost. Rehydration is not full audit replay — the audit log is for observability, the snapshot is for resumption. The two are complementary, not redundant.

## Ephemeral by Design

"Agents are ephemeral" is Five Laws #2 and it is load-bearing. Specific consequences:

Session state MUST NOT persist across restarts

Preload context (registers, .language identity blocks) is regenerated at boot. Pinning to a pre-crash cache prefix defeats cache efficiency. drain.rs:221–224.

Token accounting resets on rehydrate

session_tokens are zeroed, not accumulated from disk. The new process starts its own budget. session.rs:899.

Task handles are tokio-bound

task_handle and event channels cannot be deserialized. Rehydrated sessions get fresh tokio primitives. drain.rs:85–88.

In-flight outbox is transient

Tool results that were mid-flight when the process died are discarded. Rehydration resumes from the snapshot's message boundary, not mid-tool. drain.rs:82–84.

The principle's origin statement, from `src/kernel/presence.rs:30–35`:

"State is held in ServerState and resets on daemon restart. Arrivals are not themselves persistent kernel state — the persistent record is the audit row stream, which recall @arrived can query as the canonical arrival record."

RELATED: SESSION_TOPOLOGY (/docs/session-topology/) FIVE_LAWS (/docs/five-laws/) KERNEL_SPEC (/docs/kernel-spec/) AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/)

---

# Coordination Primitives

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/coordination-primitives/

REFERENCE // COORDINATION

# Coordination Primitives

Four kernel primitives. No locks, no message buses, no RPC. A generation counter for writes, a three-tier mechanical merge for conflicts, a 5-line envelope that replaces redundant file reads, and a queue for cross-boundary interrupts.

## 01 // OPTIMISTIC_CONCURRENCY_CONTROL

Per-file monotonic generation counter + compare-and-swap. No locks on the data path; agents write freely and the kernel detects conflicts at write time.

MECHANISM

Every file path has a u64 counter stored in `.ostk/gen_table.jsonl` (JSONL format, sorted by path). Every write bumps the counter via `bump_gen()` or `bump_gen_with_content()`.

SERIALIZATION

An exclusive `flock(2)` on `.ostk/gen_table.lock` wraps the entire read-compute-write cycle. Two concurrent `bump_gen` calls serialize: the second observes the first's new gen and increments from there. No lost updates.

SHADOWS

On bump, the pre-edit content is copied to `.ostk/shadows//gen_`. Hot PR uses these shadows to compute diffs when a later CAS fails. Source: `src/kernel/gen_table.rs:69–79, 261–296`.

CAS

`file:edit` sends the old_str the agent expects. If the current file content doesn't match, the CAS fails and Hot PR engages. The gen counter provides the authoritative "did the file change" signal — there is no content-hash comparison round-trip.

Source: `src/kernel/gen_table.rs:16–35` (lock acquisition), `:52–53, 84–86` (agent alias recorded on bump), `:151–177` (table compaction under lock), `:601–648` (concurrent bump test).

## 02 // HOT_PR_CONFLICT_RESOLUTION

When a CAS fails, Hot PR classifies the conflict by position and size and attempts mechanical resolution. No LLM call — just text diffing with hard thresholds. Source: `src/kernel/hotpr.rs`.

TIER 1 — AUTO_MERGE

PROXIMITY_LINES > 3

Edits are more than 3 lines apart — non-overlapping. The kernel applies both silently. Neither agent is interrupted.

hotpr.rs:22

TIER 2 — ASSISTED_MERGE

< 30 changed lines, ≤ 2 regions

Edits within 3 lines or overlapping but small. The kernel computes a mechanical line rebase and returns a suggested `file:edit()` call the agent can accept, modify, or reject. Still no LLM call.

hotpr.rs:26, 30

TIER 3 — MANUAL_REBASE

Above Tier 2 thresholds

The kernel returns conflict info and the agent re-reads from disk. If Tier 2 rebase fell through, an `ArbitrationObservation` is written to audit — Phase 0 observer for a future small-model arbiter. Gated by `OSTK_T2_OBSERVER`.

file.rs:25–81, 33

## 03 // DIGEST

A 5-line envelope appended to every tool response. The agent reads it to decide whether to re-read files, how much context budget is left, and which peers are active. Replaces redundant file reads with ~5 tokens of signal.

ENVELOPE_COMPONENTS

`[procs]` All active agents + health status. Always included. `digest.rs:97–99, 363–364`

`[presence]` Register state glyph. Suppressed when register is Off. `digest.rs:65–67, 636–657`

`[files]` Stale files only — current files omitted. Format: path:gen=N:writer:age. `digest.rs:106–114, 260–282`

`[loadavg]` Needle count, P0 count, fleet alive, pending nudges. `digest.rs:174–231`

`[meminfo]` Context %, used/total tokens, buffers, tool call count. Assumes 800k token budget. `digest.rs:234–256, 249`

`[maintenance]` Maintenance phase + tokens reclaimed. Emitted for 3 register dumps after a cycle. `digest.rs:150–172`

### DIGEST_AND_304_ELISION

Digest and 304 elision are the two defensive layers of the same primitive:

- Suppression (digest). Current files are absent from `[files]`. No stale files means no reason to re-read.
- Elision (304). If the agent reads a current file anyway, the kernel returns `[304] path:gen=N (current)` (~5 tokens) instead of the full content (~800 tokens). Source: `src/kernel/elision.rs`, called from `kernel::elision::read_file_with_elision`.

Digest is the observation; 304 is the response. Both are transparent to the agent — no protocol learning required. Source: `src/kernel/digest.rs:10–24`.

## 04 // NUDGES

A durable message queue that crosses process, host, and temporal boundaries. How an operator or peer agent injects context into a target agent's next tool response, without modifying the target's process.

Who can send?

Operator (via CLI/API), peer agent (in-process via push_nudge), cross-host sender (via Files API + audit discovery). Source: src/kernel/nudge.rs:17–39 (local push), :225–275 (cross-host upload).

Who receives?

The dispatch layer reads the queue on every response via pop_nudges, injects each message as a [nudge] line in the [procs] row of the digest. Source: nudge.rs:129–135.

How delivered?

Local: JSONL queue at .ostk/nudges/<agent-alias>.jsonl, flock-serialized for concurrent appends. Cross-host: Files API upload + a nudge.uploaded event in the audit log. Receivers scan audit for undownloaded uploads. Source: nudge.rs:17–39, 262–272, 336–345.

What about dead targets?

Before delivery, pop_nudges checks if the nudge service is alive via is_capability_alive. If dead, returns empty — nudges are NOT auto-retried in that case; they are lost. This is a known stub per nudge.rs:46–51. A durable retry queue is not implemented.

Persistence?

Yes — the local queue is the JSONL file, truncated on pop. Cross-host nudges are recorded in .ostk/journal.jsonl as nudge.uploaded and nudge.downloaded events so rehydrating a crashed daemon can reconcile delivery state. Source: nudge.rs:83–88, 262–345.

## Why These Four, in That Order

OCC catches conflicts. Hot PR resolves them. Digest prevents the need to re-read files. Nudges are how anything outside the agent's own turn gets its attention.

Each primitive is orthogonal: removing OCC makes Hot PR impossible; removing Hot PR makes OCC hostile (conflicts block agents); removing digest makes every tool response trigger full file re-reads (ruining the prompt cache); removing nudges means the only way to signal an agent mid-run is to kill it. The four together are the minimum set that supports multi-agent coordination without a message bus.

RELATED: FIVE_LAWS (/docs/five-laws/) KERNEL_SPEC (/docs/kernel-spec/) COORDINATION (/features/coordination/) COMPRESSION (/docs/compression/)

---

# Context Management

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/context-management/

REFERENCE // CONTEXT_LIFECYCLE

# Context Management

Six mechanisms, documented individually elsewhere, compose into a single context lifecycle. This page shows the flow: how a tool response becomes a cached prefix, how the digest prevents redundant reads, and how context pressure triggers graceful handoff instead of mid-turn compaction.

## TURN_FLOW

Every agent turn passes through six stages. Each stage feeds into the next. Removing any stage degrades the others.

01

`PRELOAD_RENDER`

The kernel assembles system blocks: base prompt, identity (.language), registers, Agentfile context. These blocks are byte-stable across turns so the prompt cache can reuse them. Volatile data (kernel digest) is placed in the final block, outside the cache prefix.

02

`TOOL_CALL`

The model issues a tool call. Shell output passes through the squash pipeline (see <a href="/docs/compression/" class="text-primary hover:underline">Compression</a>) before entering context. File reads pass through elision (stage 05). The digest envelope is appended to the tool response.

03

`OUTPUT_COMPRESSION`

The squash pipeline strips ANSI, classifies lines by grammar, deduplicates repetitive output, and enriches errors. Measured savings: 30% average across 1,499 tool calls (2.85M chars → 1.99M). Details at <a href="/docs/compression/" class="text-primary hover:underline">Compression</a>.

04

`DIGEST_INJECTION`

A 5-line envelope appended to every tool response. Components: [procs], [presence], [files] (stale only), [loadavg], [meminfo]. The agent reads this to decide whether files need re-reading. ~5 tokens of signal replaces ~800 tokens of redundant file content.

05

`304_ELISION`

If the agent reads a file despite the digest showing it current, the kernel checks the gen_table. If the file has not been written since the agent last read it, the kernel returns "[304] path:gen=N (current)" (~5 tokens) instead of the full content. Two layers: digest suppresses the attempt, elision catches it if it happens anyway.

06

`DRAIN_SNAPSHOT`

At the turn boundary (after the assistant response completes), the session writes messages + token state + config to .ostk/drain/<lineage_id>.json. Default ON since Wave G.3. This is crash recovery, not context management per se — but it closes the lifecycle loop.

Stages 01-03 reduce what enters context. Stages 04-05 reduce what the agent needs to re-read. Stage 06 captures state for crash recovery. Together they keep context growth sublinear relative to tool calls.

## Prompt Cache as Distribution Plane

Anthropic's prompt cache is content-addressed and per-organization. Two ostk processes with the same API key share cache entries automatically.

CONTENT-ADDRESSED

The cache key is the hash of the byte content up to each breakpoint. Two processes that send identical system blocks get a cache hit without coordination.

FLEET ECONOMICS

Adding readers amortizes the cold-write cost. One process pays cache_create; every subsequent process with the same prefix reads at 10% of input price. Fleet cost scales sublinearly.

TTL: 5 MINUTES

Cache entries expire after 5 minutes of inactivity. Any read resets the TTL. A fleet of agents making turns every 30-60 seconds keeps the cache perpetually warm.

The kernel places up to four `cache_control` breakpoints per request: base system prompt, preload context blocks (identity, registers), tool schemas, and the message history watermark. Each breakpoint extends the cacheable prefix. Volatile content (the current-turn digest, the latest user message) sits after all breakpoints, so cache hits are not invalidated by turn-to-turn changes.

Measured: 30% output compression in a real Opus session (1,499 squash events, 215k tokens saved). See Compression (/docs/compression/) for the full breakdown.

## DIGEST_ENVELOPE

A 5-line envelope appended to every tool response. The digest is the agent's peripheral vision — it tells the agent what changed without forcing a re-read of unchanged files. Full specification at Coordination Primitives (/docs/coordination-primitives/).

EXAMPLE_DIGEST

[procs] builder:active:2m:45% reviewer:stale:5m:78%

[presence] :arrived(builder)

[files] src/main.rs:gen=12:reviewer:3m

[loadavg] needles=3 p0=1 fleet=2/2 nudges=0

[meminfo] ctx=45% used=360k/800k buffers=2 calls=14

HOW THE AGENT USES IT

[files] absent = no stale files. If the files section is empty, every file the agent has read is still current. No reason to re-read anything.

[files] present = specific files changed. Only the listed files are stale. The agent re-reads those, skips everything else. Format: `path:gen=N:writer:age`.

[meminfo] = context budget. The agent can see its own context utilization. No surprise context exhaustion.

## Two-Layer Read Defense

Digest suppression and 304 elision are two layers of the same primitive. Both are invisible to the agent — no protocol learning required.

LAYER 1 — DIGEST SUPPRESSION

Current files are absent from the `[files]` section. When the agent sees no stale files listed, it has no reason to issue a read. The read never happens.

Cost: ~0 tokens (read prevented)

LAYER 2 — 304 ELISION

If the agent reads a current file anyway (verification, compacted context), the kernel checks the gen_table. No write since last read = `[304] path:gen=N (current)` returned instead of full content.

Cost: ~5 tokens (vs ~800 for full content)

The digest is the observation; 304 is the response. Layer 1 is advisory (the agent may still read). Layer 2 is mechanical (the kernel intercepts). Together they eliminate most redundant file content from context. See Coordination Primitives (/docs/coordination-primitives/) for the full digest specification.

## CONTEXT_PRESSURE_HANDOFF

The kernel does not compress context mid-turn. Mutating context would defeat the prompt cache and surprise the model. Instead, context pressure triggers a lifecycle state transition and a structured handoff to a successor agent.

70% context State advances to `AGING`. The kernel begins preparing a handoff record. Agent continues executing normally.

90% context State advances to `DYING`. No new tool calls beyond the finalization turn. Handoff data is finalized. A sudden jump (60% to 92%) skips AGING entirely.

Finalization One last turn in `DRAINING` state — typically the handoff write. Then `DEAD`. All tool calls fenced.

The design choice is explicit: generating a handoff record for a fresh successor preserves cache efficiency. A new agent starts with a clean context window, hits the warm prompt cache on its first turn, and picks up the handoff state from disk. Full state machine at Agent Lifecycle (/docs/agent-lifecycle/).

## Drain Snapshots

At every turn boundary, the session writes a snapshot to `.ostk/drain/.json`. Contains: lineage_id, anchor_id, timestamp, model, cumulative token counts, full message history, and pruned config (tools, permissions, thinking, revival policy).

Default ON since Wave G.3. Written atomically (tmp + fsync + rename). Errors are logged and discarded — a flaky drain write must not break a working session.

On daemon restart, orphaned lineages with a drain snapshot are rehydrated: messages and token state restored, config refreshed from the snapshot or the live Agentfile template. After successful rehydration, the snapshot is cleared. The next turn writes a fresh one.

Drain snapshots are the crash-recovery mechanism, not a context management tool. But they close the lifecycle loop: an agent that dies (from context pressure or process crash) leaves enough state on disk for its successor to continue. Full details at Agent Lifecycle (/docs/agent-lifecycle/).

## Why This Composition

Each mechanism is load-bearing. Removing any one degrades the rest.

Without output compression, tool responses bloat context, exhausting the budget faster and reducing cache hit rates (larger volatile suffixes).

Without the digest, the agent re-reads every file on every turn, flooding context with redundant content and defeating the prompt cache.

Without 304 elision, the digest's advisory suppression has no mechanical backstop. Agents that ignore the digest (or lose it to compaction) pay full cost for unchanged files.

Without the prompt cache, every turn re-tokenizes the system prompt, identity blocks, tool schemas, and message history. Cost scales linearly with turn count instead of sublinearly.

Without context pressure handoff, agents hit the window limit mid-task. The only recovery is a cold restart with no state transfer.

Without drain snapshots, crashed agents cannot be revived. The daemon can detect orphans but has nothing to rehydrate from.

RELATED: COMPRESSION (/docs/compression/) AGENT_LIFECYCLE (/docs/agent-lifecycle/) COORDINATION (/docs/coordination-primitives/) MODELS (/docs/models/)

---

# Compression

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/compression/

SECTION_04 // SQUASHER

# Compression

Two layers reduce token cost. The squash pipeline compresses shell output before it enters context. The prompt cache reuses byte-stable prefixes across turns and across the fleet.

MEASURED // PROMPT_CACHE

28% cost reduction

Real ostk session, opus model: $23.66 actual vs $32.93 without cache. $9.27 saved across 57 API calls.

CACHE_HIT

32.2%

read/total prompt

CACHE_READ

691,853

tokens

INPUT

1.44M

tokens

BILLED

2.15M

tokens

## PIPELINE_STAGES

01

`GRAMMAR_DETECT`

Identify the tool from the command string (e.g., "cargo build" → cargo grammar). Falls back to blind compress if no grammar matches.

02

`VTE_STRIP`

Remove all ANSI escape sequences from raw output. Progress bars, color codes, cursor movements — stripped.

03

`LINE_CLASSIFY`

Three-tier classification: grammar hazard/outcome/noise rules → universal patterns (errors, stack traces) → structural heuristics (decorative lines). Each line tagged: Hazard, Outcome, or Noise.

04

`IMPLICIT_DEDUP`

Collapse consecutive similar lines: "first line [⋯ N similar lines]". Levenshtein distance with tokenization (URLs→{url}, paths→{path}, versions→{ver}).

05

`ERROR_ENRICH`

On non-zero exit: pre-fetch diagnostics (path walks, file stat, permission checks). Budget: <100ms, read-only, no network. Appended under [ostk:enrich] header.

06

`METRICS_RECORD`

Record to .ostk/metrics.jsonl: bytes saved, tokens saved (bytes/4), tool key, grammar hit. Flock-protected append.

## ROUTE_CATEGORIES

The router classifies every command into one of five categories. Resolution order: dangerous → multi-token → single-token → fallback.

CONDENSE Verbose output → summary. cargo build, npm install. Implicit dedup + hazard/outcome filtering

NARRATE Silent commands → execution narration. cp, mv, mkdir. "→ cp: a.txt → b.txt (ok)"

PASSTHROUGH Verbatim preservation. cat, grep, ls, jq. Original output + metadata footer

STRUCTURED Machine-readable → formatted summary. git status, docker ps. Parse into branches, containers, etc.

DANGEROUS Destructive ops. rm -rf, git push --force. CRITICAL/WARNING severity with context

## Semantic Deduplication

Optional GPU-accelerated dedup via the `potion-base-8M` embeddings model (256-dim). Lines with identical structure but different variables produce identical tokenized forms.

Install: `ostk embeddings download`. The kernel uses it automatically once available.

Backend: Metal GPU (Apple Silicon) with CPU fallback via Wgpu/NdArray. Feature-gated: --features embeddings.

## DEDUP_PATTERN

How implicit dedup collapses repetitive structured output. Pattern is similarity-based — lines with the same shape but different variables (paths, versions, hashes) collapse together.

BEFORE

Compiling ostk v3.0.0

Compiling tokio v1.38.0

Compiling serde v1.0.203

⋯ 47 more crate compilations

warning: unused import `std::io`

warning: 2 warnings generated

Finished release in 42.3s

AFTER

Compiling ostk v3.0.0

[⋯ 49 similar lines]

warning: unused import `std::io`

warning: 2 warnings generated

Finished release in 42.3s

Note: cargo's own progress output is already terse, so real-world cargo build savings average ~1% (see measured table below). High savings come from verbose, repetitive, or noisy output streams.

## MEASURED // SQUASH_PIPELINE

The shell output compression layer. Aggregated from 802 squash events in a real ostk session. Sampled from `.ostk/metrics.jsonl`.

METRIC VALUE NOTE

squash events 802 shell tool calls compressed

original bytes 246,786 raw output before pipeline

compressed bytes 159,110 after ANSI strip + dedup

avg savings 36% across all 802 events

tokens saved ~21,728 4 bytes per token

cost saved (opus input) ~$0.33 $15/M tokens

The squash pipeline is a small but consistent win on output. The dominant savings come from prompt cache hits on input, not output compression — see above.

RELATED: ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) KERNEL_SPEC (/docs/kernel-spec/)

---

# Boot Sequence

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/boot-sequence/

REFERENCE // BOOT_STAGES

# Boot Sequence

Boot is not a daemon start. It is a read-only scan that reads `.ostk/` state, resolves identity, and reports what is on disk. The kernel does not modify project files during boot — it observes, verifies, and tells you what it found.

## What Boot Does

`ostk boot` assembles a complete picture of the current workspace from three layers:

LAYER 1: REPO

Shared project state — `.ostk/` directory, Agentfile, boot.md, pin caps. Committed to version control.

LAYER 2: OPERATOR

Personal identity — `~/.HUMANFILE` (global) merged with `.ostk/HUMANFILE` (project). Never committed.

LAYER 3: HARNESS

Runtime environment — detected from environment variables at startup. Determines tool routing policy. Ephemeral.

The output is the merged context: `context = f(repo) + g(operator) + h(harness)`. The agent instance receives this at session start. It knows who it is talking to, where it is running, and what it is allowed to do — without being told.

## BOOT_STAGES

Boot executes five stages in strict sequence. Each stage depends on the output of the previous one. If a stage fails fatally, boot halts and prints a diagnostic.

0

PARSE AGENTFILE

If the `OSTK_AGENTFILE` environment variable is set, boot reads and parses the Agentfile at that path. Resolves:

- `FROM ` — which LLM the agent targets
- `TOOL ` — tool declarations (counted, not loaded)
- `LIMIT`, `ISOLATION` — resource and sandbox constraints

Missing Agentfile is non-fatal — boot continues with defaults. Present-but-unreadable is an error.

0b

LOAD HUMANFILE

Hierarchical resolution loads operator identity and preferences. Resolution order:

- `~/.HUMANFILE` — global identity (MODEL, SIGN, TRUST directives are immutable from this layer)
- `.ostk/HUMANFILE` — project-level config (may EXTENDS global)
- Merge if both exist — project overlays global, but identity fields stay pinned to the global source

Directives resolved at this stage: `MODEL`, `SIGN`, `PREPROCESS`, `TRUST`, `SECRET`, `EMBED`, `DRIVER`, `VERB`, `BOOT`.

After loading, boot auto-populates the `AVAILABLE` model list by scanning for known API keys (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `GEMINI_API_KEY`, etc.) and initializes secret masking for any `SECRET` directives.

1

KERNEL INIT

The identity and capability layer. Boot determines who you are and what you can do:

- Trust tier — GPG web-of-trust determines your tier (T0 through T3). Your GPG key is checked against the project's signing chain.
- ENTITYFILE — governance document loaded and GPG-verified. Invalid signatures are fatal (fail-closed). Missing ENTITYFILE is non-fatal (fresh-install path).
- Pin caps — capability pins loaded from `.ostk/pin.caps`. These constrain what tools agents can invoke.
- Drivers — declared drivers probed via Unix sockets. Boot never spawns drivers — it only checks what is already running. Inactive drivers are registered for demand-spawn on first use.
- .language — the verb registry is populated. Kernel services, MCP primitives, introspection tools, and HUMANFILE VERB directives are all registered into the `.language` file.

Before any of this, boot reaps dead agents from the process table and cleans up orphaned heartbeat files — housekeeping from the previous session.

2

PRELOAD RENDER

Builds the context injection that every agent instance receives at session start. The preload is assembled from:

- Project structure scan (what files exist, language breakdown)
- `.language` register contents (all verbs, devices, services with their resolutions)
- `boot.md` swap — the compiled operator section replaces the template boot.md
- Identity blocks from HUMANFILE (communication style, scheduling preferences, corrections log)

The stable portion of this preload can be rendered once and uploaded as firmware — a deployment artifact that fleet members reference by ID rather than re-rendering independently.

3

INTENT COMPILE

Resolves the tack grammar and validates the dispatch loop:

- Tack grammar — HUMANFILE VERB definitions are validated against `.language`. Unresolvable verbs reduce boot confidence.
- INIT validation — the post-language INIT verb sequence is checked. Missing verbs in the INIT chain are flagged and penalize the boot confidence score.
- Verb momentum — recent tack resolution history from the audit log is loaded to seed the confidence gradient.

After this stage, the compressed register dump is printed — a one-line summary of .language state (verb count, fleet size, open needles).

4

EXECUTION

The agent loop begins. Boot transitions from observation to action:

- Harness detection — environment is probed to determine tool routing (Claude Code, ostk-serve, CI, terminal). This controls whether the agent uses native tools or MCP primitives.
- Checkpoint verification — the most recent checkpoint's GPG signature is verified. Invalid signatures are fatal for governed agents, advisory for interactive sessions.
- Version pin — if `.ostk/version` exists, the running binary version is checked against the pin.
- Continuation prompt — if enabled, a dynamic prompt is generated from current workspace state and written to `.ostk/prompts/continuation.md`.

Tool calls from this point forward pass through the 7-step approval chain (/docs/approval-chain/).

## POST_CHECKS

Power-On Self-Test. Seven checks run during boot to verify system integrity. The `POST 7/7` line in boot output confirms all passed. A failed check does not necessarily halt boot — some degrade gracefully, others are fatal.

HUMANFILE_RESOLVE

CHECKS

Hierarchical HUMANFILE resolution succeeds. Global (~/.HUMANFILE) and project (.ostk/HUMANFILE) merge without conflict.

ON FAILURE

Graceful — defaults used. Warning printed.

TRUST_VERIFY

CHECKS

GPG signature on HUMANFILE validates against the signing chain. Trust tier (T0–T3) is determined.

ON FAILURE

Bad signature is fatal (fail-closed). Missing GPG degrades to T3 (read-only).

ENTITYFILE_SIGN

CHECKS

ENTITYFILE governance document exists and its detached GPG signature (.asc or .sigstore) is valid.

ON FAILURE

Invalid signature at primary location is fatal. Missing ENTITYFILE is non-fatal (fresh install).

DRIVER_PROBE

CHECKS

Declared drivers are probed via Unix socket. Alive drivers registered; inactive ones marked for demand-spawn.

ON FAILURE

Graceful — inactive drivers spawn on first use. No boot failure.

LANGUAGE_INIT

CHECKS

.language registry is populated with kernel services, MCP primitives, HUMANFILE VERBs, and introspection tools.

ON FAILURE

Unresolvable VERBs reduce boot confidence score but do not halt boot.

CHECKPOINT_GPG

CHECKS

Most recent checkpoint's detached GPG signature is verified against accepted key set.

ON FAILURE

Fatal for governed agents. Advisory for interactive sessions. Expired keys reduce confidence.

VERSION_PIN

CHECKS

Running binary version matches .ostk/version pin (if present).

ON FAILURE

Warning with upgrade hint. Boot continues.

## Boot Confidence

The `boot confidence` score is a 0.00–1.00 value computed at the end of boot. It measures how well the system can resolve operator intent into action. Three inputs feed the score:

IDENTITY COMPLETENESS

HUMANFILE loaded, trust tier resolved, ENTITYFILE verified. Missing identity layers drag the score toward zero.

RESOLUTION QUALITY

Recent tack resolutions from the audit log are weighted by tier (T1 = 1.0, T2 = 0.8, T3 = 0.5). High-tier resolutions lift the score.

INIT VALIDATION

The INIT verb sequence is checked against .language. Missing verbs in the post-language chain penalize confidence.

The score determines the operating mode:

◉ FULL

0.90 – 1.00

All capabilities available. Normal operation.

◎ MINIMAL

0.50 – 0.89

Core operations available. Some advanced features may be constrained.

◎ RESTRICTED

0.00 – 0.49

Read-heavy mode. Write operations may require explicit confirmation. Fresh projects with no audit history start here.

A fresh project with no audit history will always show `0.00 (restricted mode)`. This is normal — the score rises as the system accumulates successful tack resolutions. It is a measure of earned trust, not a pass/fail gate.

## BOOT_OUTPUT

A typical `ostk boot` run on a configured workspace. Each line maps to a stage above:

ostk boot

✓ HUMANFILE loaded (global only)

trust: T1 (cross-signed, write access)

.language: 58 verbs | fleet: 0 alive

needles: 0 open

boot confidence: 0.00 (restricted mode)

`✓ HUMANFILE loaded (global only)`

Stage 0b succeeded. Only ~/.HUMANFILE was found (no project-level HUMANFILE). Identity and preferences resolved from the global file.

`trust: T1 (cross-signed, write access)`

Stage 1. Your GPG key is cross-signed by a T0 holder. Full write access granted.

`.language: 58 verbs | fleet: 0 alive`

Stage 2–3. The .language registry holds 58 verbs. No agents are currently running.

`needles: 0 open`

No open work items. The needle system is idle.

`boot confidence: 0.00 (restricted mode)`

Stage 3. No audit history yet — the confidence score starts at zero on a fresh workspace.

## Harness Detection

Boot detects the execution environment and sets tool routing policy accordingly. The agent knows how to talk to the kernel without being told.

claude-code

DETECTION

`OSTK_HARNESS=claude-code or TERM_PROGRAM=claude`

TOOL ROUTING

Native Bash / Read / Edit / Grep. Background via run_in_background.

ostk-serve

DETECTION

`OSTK_SERVE=1`

TOOL ROUTING

MCP shell / spawn / interact. File I/O via fs_read / edit / fs_write.

ci

DETECTION

`CI=true or GITHUB_ACTIONS=true`

TOOL ROUTING

Native Bash. No background, no TTY.

terminal

DETECTION

`Default fallback`

TOOL ROUTING

Native Bash. Background optional.

The `OSTK_HARNESS` environment variable overrides all detection — it always wins.

## First Run

When no `.ostk/` directory is found, boot does not fail — it prints a welcome message and guides you to initialization:

- No git repo — suggests `git init` first. The OS requires a git repository as its substrate.
- Git repo, no .ostk/ — prints the full welcome with `ostk init` and `ostk init --guided` options.

After running `ostk init`, re-run `ostk boot` to complete the full boot sequence. See Quick Start (/start/) for the step-by-step guide.

RELATED: QUICK_START (/start/) ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/) AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/)

---

# Enrichment Hooks

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/enrichment/

REFERENCE // ENRICHMENT

# Enrichment Hooks

The model states intent. The kernel enriches. Registered drivers annotate reads with diagnostics and symbols, re-check edits for breakage, and provide type-safe multi-file refactoring — all through a single `enrich` protocol.

A file read returns raw bytes. An edit returns success or failure. Neither knows about the language. A model editing `src/main.rs` can't see compiler diagnostics, can't discover symbols, can't safely rename a function across 40 call sites.

FCP drivers like `fcp-rust` wrap language servers and are registered via HUMANFILE — but without enrichment hooks, the kernel never asks them anything. The intelligence is there. The wiring is what enrichment adds.

## ENRICH_PROTOCOL

Drivers expose one method: `enrich(path, content_hash?)`. All response fields are optional. Content hash enables caching — if the driver already indexed this version, it skips reanalysis.

```
EnrichResult
diagnostics errors, warnings — inline in read response
symbols function/struct/impl outline
annotations arbitrary driver-specific metadata
references who calls/uses symbols in this file
dependencies what this file imports
dependents what depends on this file
```

Drivers that wrap LSP do the translation internally. The kernel never sees LSP types — it speaks `enrich` only. Every language with a driver gets the same protocol.

## PROGRESSIVE_DISCLOSURE

304 (unchanged)

None. The model already saw this file.

First read

Errors only (severity >= error). Never hints. Configurable via HUMANFILE.

Explicit enrich=full

Diagnostics + symbols + annotations + references.

After an edit

Always enrich. The model just changed the file and wants to know if it broke something.

## What the Model Sees

Diagnostics are injected inline — directly into the file content. Cheaper tokens, immediately actionable. No protocol to learn.

enriched read response

```
fn main() {
let x = 5; // [error] unused variable `x` (E0001)
println!("hello");
}
```

## HUMANFILE_CONFIGURATION

Enrichment is off by default. The operator opts in:

HUMANFILE

```
DRIVER rust fcp-rust
ENRICH rust errors # severity floor: errors only
```

`off` Default. No driver calls on reads or edits.

`errors` Diagnostics with severity >= error only.

`warnings` Diagnostics with severity >= warning.

`full` Diagnostics + symbols + annotations + references.

Routing is capability-based. Multiple drivers can enrich the same file — fcp-typescript + fcp-css both enrich `.vue` files. The kernel merges results by concatenation.

## Type-Safe Refactoring

With enrichment hooks, the kernel has access to the symbol graph. A `refactor` tool exposes type-safe multi-file operations:

RENAME

Safe rename across all call sites. The driver finds every reference. No grep-and-hope.

EXTRACT_FUNCTION

Select a range, extract to a new function. The type system computes params, return type, and extraction boundary.

INLINE

Inline a function or variable at all call sites. The driver validates that inlining is safe.

rename example

$ refactor(action="rename", symbol="dispatch_fs_write", new_name="dispatch_write")

edits:

src/serve/dispatch.rs "dispatch_fs_write" → "dispatch_write"

src/commands/relay.rs "dispatch_fs_write" → "dispatch_write"

src/serve/types.rs "dispatch_fs_write" → "dispatch_write"

files_affected: 3 (all edits pre-validated by type checker)

Each edit goes through CAS with gen_table conflict detection. If any file was modified concurrently, the whole batch rolls back atomically. This is OCC (Law 4) applied to multi-file refactoring.

## EMBEDDINGS_VS_DRIVERS

Signal Embeddings Driver

Related function ~0.75 cosine similarity Exact call graph — 47 call sites

Relevant file Shares vocabulary Direct import dependency

Dead code Can't tell Zero references, zero callers

Call chain Co-occurrence A → B → C — exact path

Test-only code Can't tell Only referenced from test modules

The two systems compose: embeddings for breadth (prose, docs), drivers for precision (code). Instead of reading 10 files to trace a call chain (~5,000 tokens), the driver says "here are the 3 files that matter" (~1,500 tokens). 70% reduction in exploration tokens for code navigation.

## SAFETY_AND_TIMEOUTS

Output sanitization Diagnostic messages capped at 256 chars, control characters stripped.

Path allowlist Drivers run under the agent's authorized-path set. Responses scoped to the requested file.

Signed registration HUMANFILE driver declarations validated against ENTITYFILE signatures.

Circuit breaker 3 consecutive timeouts → driver marked failed, 5-minute cooldown.

Warm driver timeout 100ms. Fallback: raw read, skip enrichment.

Cold start timeout 2s. Fallback: raw read, spawn continues in background.

Refactoring timeout 10s. Fallback: error (refactoring is explicit, not best-effort).

RELATED: ARCHITECTURE (/docs/architecture/) COORDINATION (/docs/coordination-primitives/) CONTEXT_MGMT (/docs/context-management/) HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/)

---

# Pull Model

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/pull-model/

REFERENCE // PULL_MODEL

# Pull Model

The scheduler doesn't push work to agents; agents declare what they're eligible for via a `WORK` directive and pull matching needles. "Affinity mask, not a todo list" — per `src/agentfile/parser.rs:19–27`.

## WORK_DIRECTIVE

Format: `WORK [...]`. Multiple expressions on a single line are space-separated and evaluated as AND. At most one `WORK` directive per Agentfile — two of them is a parse error.

- Parsed into `WorkFilter { expressions: Vec }` (parser.rs:23–27)
- Stored as `Agentfile.work: Option` (parser.rs:190–191)
- Optional — an Agentfile without `WORK` has `work: None` and is eligible for any needle the scheduler routes to it
- Missing argument error: `ParseError::MissingArgument { directive: "WORK" }` (parser.rs:1175–1188)
- Multiple-WORK error: `ParseError::MultipleWork` (parser.rs:238, 271, 1188)

## EXPRESSION_SYNTAX

Each expression is a `key[OP]value` pair. Expressions are stored as raw strings and matched by the scheduler against needle metadata. Recognized operators:

`=` `priority=P0` Exact equality. Priority must equal P0.

`>=` `priority>=P1` Inclusive lower bound on ordered values (P0 < P1 < P2 < P3).

`Inclusive upper bound.

`=a,b,c` `tags=rust,bugfix` Comma-separated list. Matches if the needle has ANY of these tags.

## EXAMPLE

agents/rust-worker.af

```
FROM claude-sonnet-4-6

PROMPT You work on Rust code. You only pick up needles tagged rust.

WORK tags=rust,bugfix priority>=P1

TOOL shell
TOOL file:edit
```

Parsed as `expressions: ["tags=rust,bugfix", "priority>=P1"]`. Matched against each needle: the needle must have at least one of {rust, bugfix} in its tags AND priority P1 or higher. Source: `parser.rs:1353–1373`.

## Why Pull, Not Push

A push scheduler has to know the capability model of every agent it could dispatch to — a moving target every time someone edits an Agentfile. A pull model lets each agent declare its own shape and lets the scheduler match against that declaration at run time.

The filter expressions live in the Agentfile, which travels with the agent. Adding a new worker doesn't require reconfiguring the scheduler; it requires adding an `agents/.af` file. The scheduler is a matcher, not a router.

RELATED: AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) AGENT_LIFECYCLE (/docs/agent-lifecycle/) SESSION_TOPOLOGY (/docs/session-topology/)

---

# Tack Grammar

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/tack-grammar/

REFERENCE // INTENT_LANGUAGE

# Tack Grammar

Tack is the operator intent language. You type intent into the TUI tack bar. The kernel resolves it — sometimes to a command, sometimes to an LLM query, sometimes to a needle reference, sometimes to raw hay. Communication gets shorter as you work. The OS adapts to you, not the other way around.

## INPUT_FORMS

The tack parser recognizes four prefix patterns, checked in priority order. Everything else is filed as hay.

PREFIX INTENT_TYPE RESOLUTION EXAMPLES

`:verb` Command Kernel dispatch — no LLM `:compile, :status, :reap, :close →573`

`.?` Query Routes through the LLM for intent-based resolution `.? how does bail work, .? why`

`→NNN` NeedleRef Opens needle detail or activates work `→573, →1245, ->437`

`::` Escalate Scope targeting — agent, file, or concept `:: agent1 fix the build`

`(bare text)` Hay Filed as raw unstructured intent for later compile `need to fix the auth flow`

`→` and `->` are equivalent — the parser accepts both. Priority is always: exact match first, then pattern match, then LLM inference as last resort.

## TACK_EXAMPLES

COMMAND DISPATCH

```
$ :status
→ kernel dispatch → ostk status
3 open needles, 2 agents alive, gen 20556

$ :compile
→ triage hay → 4 new needles filed

$ :close →573
→ needle 573 closed
```

QUERY + NEEDLE REF

```
$ .? how does bail work
→ LLM query → bail packs a signed artifact
with SHA-256 manifest and attestation chain

$ →1245
→ needle 1245: "verb namespace refactor"
status: open, priority: p1, milestone: v0.9
```

ESCALATE

```
$ :: agent1 fix the build
→ nudge agent1: "fix the build"
```

HAY (BARE TEXT)

```
$ need to refactor the auth module
→ filed as hay (raw intent)
compile later with :compile
```

## RESOLUTION_PIPELINE

When you type `:verb`, the kernel resolves it through three sources in priority order. First match wins.

01

### .LANGUAGE (COMPILED DIALECT)

The .language file is the compiled verb registry — a pipe-delimited table loaded at boot. Verbs here override everything else. This is where momentum lives: verbs you use often are resident, low-momentum verbs are demand-spawned.

confidence: `1.0`

02

### HUMANFILE EXTENSIONS

Custom verbs declared in the HUMANFILE via VERB or TACK directives. The operator's personal dialect — verbs that map to project-specific commands or workflows.

confidence: `0.9`

03

### STATIC VERB TABLE

Hardcoded verb-to-command mappings compiled into the kernel. The baseline vocabulary. Always available even if .language and HUMANFILE are missing.

confidence: `0.8`

If no source matches, the kernel returns up to 3 edit-distance suggestions (Levenshtein, threshold 3). The OS never says "unknown verb." It always tries.

## NAMESPACE_LAYERS

Beyond the resolution pipeline, verbs are dispatched through four namespace layers. Resolution walks layers in priority order — highest-priority handler wins.

`kernel::`

Internal handlers — direct Rust function calls, zero-cost. Always available.

boot, compile, status, reap, clock

`driver::`

Device driver tools — routed via Unix socket to alive driver processes.

fcp-rust, fcp-web, fcp-screen

`fleet::`

Fleet agent verbs — only available when agents are alive.

delegate, attach, detach

`user::`

HUMANFILE verb declarations — shell exec. Operator-defined commands.

Custom VERB/TACK directives

## VERB_TIERS

Every verb in the `.language` file has a tier that determines its stability and persistence.

TIER CLASS BEHAVIOR EXAMPLES

`0` Services / Devices Infrastructure. Never decay. Always resident. Approval gates, heartbeat, drivers, embeddings. `approval, heartbeat, fcp-rust, fcp-web, embeddings`

`1` Kernel / Ceremony Core operations. Stable, well-tested. Require human presence for ceremonies. Never removed. `boot, compile, shutdown, commit, confirm, negotiate`

`2` Stable Userspace Documented, production-ready verbs. High momentum keeps them resident. Low usage may demote. `add, close, search, near, hay, status`

`3` Flexible / Ephemeral Experimental or session-scoped. Demand-spawned when invoked. May decay between sessions. `ultrathink, sign, reboot, mode, model`

## INTERNALIZED_VERBS

These 29 verbs resolve as direct function calls inside the kernel — zero-cost dispatch, no shell, no socket. The rest dispatch through `.language`, HUMANFILE, or shell.

`activate`

`add`

`audit`

`boot`

`clock`

`close`

`compile`

`correct`

`decide`

`diff`

`draft`

`edit`

`find`

`grep`

`hay`

`idea`

`near`

`needle`

`nudge`

`promote`

`ps`

`reap`

`related`

`search`

`shelve`

`show`

`status`

`trace`

`unshelve`

Verbs like `correct` and `nudge` resolve to the same handler. `add` and `needle` are equivalent. `search`, `find`, and `grep` all route to the same search handler.

## THE .LANGUAGE FILE

The verb registry. A pipe-delimited table loaded at boot. Generated by `:compile` from usage patterns in the audit trail.

.ostk/.language (excerpt)

```
# verb | tier | layer | last_gen | momentum | resolution | signature | doc
:boot | 1 | kernel | 20556 | 1.00 | internal | () → (state) | read state, report identity
:compile | 1 | kernel | 20556 | 1.00 | internal | () → (report) | triage hay into needles
:hay | 3 | user | 20556 | 1.00 | internal | (text) → () | capture raw intent
:shelve | 1 | user | 20556 | 1.00 | internal | (id) → () | pause needle work
:ultrathink| 3 | user | 20554 | 0.20 | [LLM inference] | (topic) → () | deep reasoning
```

`verb` The tack verb name (with : prefix in the file, stripped at parse time)

`tier` 0–3. Stability class. Determines decay behavior and boot residency.

`layer` kernel, service, device, user, ceremony, process, state, info, resolver

`last_gen` Generation counter at last use. Tracks recency.

`momentum` 0.0–1.0. Usage frequency score. High-momentum verbs are boot-resident.

`resolution` How the verb dispatches: "internal", a socket path, or an ostk CLI command.

`signature` Input/output type signature. Used for schema generation.

`doc` One-line description. Shown in help and tool listings.

## MOMENTUM

Verbs you use often get faster. Usage frequency is tracked in the audit trail. When you run `:compile`, the kernel rebuilds the `.language` file from usage patterns and updates momentum scores.

HIGH (1.0) Boot-resident. Loaded into memory at startup. Zero latency on dispatch. Kernel verbs, frequently-used commands.

MID (0.4–0.9) Demand-spawned on first invocation. Small latency on first use, then cached for the session.

LOW ( Cold. Resolved through fallback paths. May decay from the table between sessions if unused.

This is not LLM-learned. It is frequency-based. The verb table evolves across sessions through audit trail analysis. Over time, your tack vocabulary converges on the commands you actually use.

## ALIASES

The static verb table includes aliases that resolve to canonical verbs. You can type whichever form feels natural.

`:showme` → `:show`

`:spec` → `:promote`

`:straw` → `:hay`

`:emerge` → `:hay`

`:proc` → `:ps`

`:correct` → `:nudge`

`:recover` → `:boot`

`:calibrate` → `:compile`

`:delegate` → `:run`

`:inform` → `:nudge`

`:ls` → `:needle list`

`:open` → `:needle list`

`:close` → `:needle close`

`:add` → `:needle add`

`:next` → `:needle next`

`:find` → `:search`

`:grep` → `:search`

## HUMANFILE_VERBS

The HUMANFILE's tack grammar section defines the operator's personal dialect. Custom verbs via `VERB` or `TACK` directives.

HUMANFILE custom verbs

```
# In .ostk/HUMANFILE:
VERB pitchfork show --semantic
VERB discuss spawn round-table
TACK boost priority-modifier

# Then in the tack bar:
$ :pitchfork
→ resolves to: ostk show --semantic

$ :discuss
→ resolves to: ostk spawn round-table
```

HUMANFILE verbs sit in the `user::` namespace layer — lowest priority. If a kernel verb or driver verb has the same name, the kernel verb wins.

RELATED: TACK_FEATURE (/features/tack/) COMMANDS (/docs/commands/) NEEDLES (/docs/needles/) TUI (/docs/tui/)

---

# Models

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/models/

GET_STARTED // MODEL_SELECTION

# Models

ostk is model-agnostic. Six production providers, one local runtime, one on-device path. Every provider speaks through the same CpuDriver trait — but they don't all support the same features. This page tells you what works where.

## PROVIDERS

Anthropic FULL

MODELS

`claude-opus-4-6, claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-haiku-4-5`

CONTEXT / WIRE

200k tokens — Native

FEATURES

Tool use, extended thinking, streaming, prompt caching, vision, file upload, batch API, token counting, model listing, speed mode, citations

API KEY

`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`

SOURCE

`src/cpu/anthropic.rs:257–297`

Google Gemini FULL

MODELS

`gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.0-flash, gemini-3.1-pro, gemini-3-flash`

CONTEXT / WIRE

1M tokens — OpenAI-compat

FEATURES

Tool use, thinking (auto for 3.x models, thinkingLevel: HIGH), streaming. No client-side prompt caching — server-side only.

API KEY

`GEMINI_API_KEY (or GOOGLE_API_KEY)`

SOURCE

`src/cpu/gemini.rs:32–70, 299–319`

Mistral FULL

MODELS

`mistral-large-latest, codestral-latest, devstral-small, devstral-medium-2507, magistral-*`

CONTEXT / WIRE

256k tokens — OpenAI-compat

FEATURES

Tool use, streaming. No thinking mode. ostk handles Mistral-specific quirks: tool name injection (required by API), tool ID remapping (9 alphanumeric chars).

API KEY

`MISTRAL_API_KEY`

SOURCE

`src/cpu/mistral.rs:24–45, 145–220`

OpenAI PRODUCTION

MODELS

`gpt-4o, o1-*, o3-pro, o4-*`

CONTEXT / WIRE

128k–200k tokens — OpenAI-compat

FEATURES

Tool use, streaming. No prompt caching, no token counting. Reasoning models (o1/o3/o4) have built-in thinking — not controlled by ostk.

API KEY

`OPENAI_API_KEY`

SOURCE

`src/cpu/openrouter.rs (shared driver), model_registry.rs:84–117`

OpenRouter GATEWAY

MODELS

`Any provider/model (meta-llama/*, deepseek/*, etc.)`

CONTEXT / WIRE

Model-dependent — OpenAI-compat

FEATURES

Tool use, streaming. Fallback for any model not matched by a dedicated driver. Sends HTTP-Referer: https://ostk.ai.

API KEY

`OPENROUTER_API_KEY`

SOURCE

`src/cpu/openrouter.rs:12–51`

Ollama (local) LOCAL

MODELS

`local/codestral:22b, local/qwen2.5-coder:32b, local/llama3.3:70b, local/deepseek-r1:70b, local/gemma3:27b`

CONTEXT / WIRE

Model-dependent — OpenAI-compat

FEATURES

Tool use, streaming. No prompt caching, no token counting. Models with ":" separator auto-route here.

API KEY

`None required (OLLAMA_HOST to override endpoint)`

SOURCE

`src/cpu/mod.rs:437–454, providers.rs:62–68`

Apple on-device EXPERIMENTAL

MODELS

`apple/default`

CONTEXT / WIRE

4k tokens — OpenAI-compat

FEATURES

Streaming only. macOS only. Routes to the olleh local service on port 11941. Suitable for preprocessing, not primary agent work.

API KEY

`None (APPLE_MODEL_HOST to override)`

SOURCE

`src/cpu/mod.rs:439–446, model_registry.rs:196–201`

## How `FROM auto` Picks a Model

When an Agentfile says `FROM auto` (or no `FROM` at all), ostk scores available models based on which API keys are present. Source: `src/commands/run.rs:89–197`.

01

Runtime override: staging/preferred_model (set by :model in TUI)

02

HUMANFILE MODEL directive (highest-priority static config)

03

HUMANFILE FALLBACK directive (secondary static config)

04

Environment scoring: scan for API keys and rank by capability. claude-opus-4-6 + ANTHROPIC_API_KEY scores 100; claude-sonnet-4-6 scores 90; gpt-4o + OPENAI_API_KEY scores 60; gemini-2.0-flash + GEMINI_API_KEY scores 50.

05

Default fallback: claude-sonnet-4-6 (run.rs:196)

`OSTK_MODEL` env var overrides everything — if set, it's used regardless of HUMANFILE or FROM. Source: `src/cpu/context.rs:116`.

## CONFIGURATION

Four places to set a model, in priority order:

OSTK_MODEL env var Session-wide override. Ignores everything else. `OSTK_MODEL=gemini-2.5-pro ostk run agents/worker.af`

FROM <model> in Agentfile Per-agent. The agent always runs this model. `FROM claude-sonnet-4-6`

HUMANFILE MODEL directive Project-wide default. Applies when FROM is auto or absent. `MODEL claude-opus-4-6`

--model flag on ostk kernel spawn Per-spawn override. Takes precedence over Agentfile FROM auto. `ostk kernel spawn worker --model gemini-2.5-pro "task"`

## FEATURE_MATRIX
