Not every provider supports every feature. The CpuDriver trait provides a common surface, but the underlying APIs vary. This matrix shows what actually works per provider at the driver level.

Feature Anthropic Gemini Mistral OpenAI Ollama

Tool use ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

Streaming ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

Extended thinking ✓ ✓ (3.x) — built-in* —

Prompt caching ✓ server — — —

Vision/images ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ model

File upload API ✓ — — — —

Token counting ✓ — — — —

Batch API ✓ — — — —

Speed mode ✓ — — — —

Citations ✓ — — — —

✓ = ostk driver implements it. — = not available in the driver. server = handled server-side, no client config. built-in* = reasoning is a model behavior, not a driver feature (o1/o3/o4 always think). model = depends on the specific model loaded in Ollama.

## MODEL_SPECIFIC_NOTES

Gemini 3.x thinking: When the model name contains "3.1" or "3-", the driver automatically sends `thinkingConfig.includeThoughts: true` and `thinkingLevel: "HIGH"`. Gemini 2.x models get standard config only. Source: `gemini.rs:105–121`.

Mistral tool ID remapping: Mistral requires tool call IDs to be exactly 9 alphanumeric characters. Anthropic emits `toolu_` prefixed IDs that are longer. ostk remaps them transparently. Source: `mistral.rs:191–220`.

Ollama auto-detection: Any model name containing a colon (e.g., `codestral:22b`) is auto-routed to Ollama without the `local/` prefix. Source: `model_registry.rs:251–253`.

OpenAI via OpenRouter: If `OPENAI_API_KEY` is not set but `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` is, OpenAI models (gpt-4o, o1-*, o3-*) route through OpenRouter automatically. Source: `providers.rs:76–81`.

DeepSeek R1: Reasoning model routed via OpenRouter with special format mapping at `model_registry.rs:391–398`. Handles the reasoning_content field in responses.

## QUICK_EXAMPLE

HUMANFILE

```
MODEL claude-sonnet-4-6
FALLBACK gemini-2.5-pro
```

agents/cheap-lint.af

```
FROM local/codestral:22b
PROMPT Run eslint, fix warnings.
TOOL shell
LIMIT budget_usd 0
```

The HUMANFILE sets the project default (Sonnet) with a fallback (Gemini). The lint agent pins a local model via `FROM` — free, fast, and sufficient for the task. The budget is $0 because local models have no API cost. Model mixing is the normal case, not an edge case.

RELATED: QUICK_START (/start/) AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/) ENV_VARS (/docs/env-vars/)

---

# Skills

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/skills/

REFERENCE // SKILLS

# Skills

A `SKILL` directive in an Agentfile names a bundle of prompt text, tool grants, and conventions that travels with the agent. The parser stores skill names as a simple `Vec`. Resolution happens at spawn time.

## DIRECTIVE

Format: `SKILL `. Repeatable — one per skill. The parser collects them into `Agentfile.skills: Vec` at `src/agentfile/parser.rs:186–187`.

An empty `SKILL` line (no bundle name) is a parse error: `ParseError::MissingArgument { directive: "SKILL" }`. Source: `parser.rs:464–468, test at :933–939`.

No multiple-SKILL conflict check — you can declare the same skill twice and it'll appear twice in the vec. The skill resolver deduplicates downstream.

## EXAMPLE

agents/fixer.af

```
FROM claude-sonnet-4-6

PROMPT You fix bugs. You write tests. You commit.

SKILL tdd
SKILL commit

TOOL shell
TOOL file:edit
LIMIT budget_usd 3
```

Source: test Agentfile at `parser.rs:1368–1383`. The parsed `af.skills` becomes `vec!["tdd", "commit"]`.

## What a Skill Actually Is

A skill is a named bundle the spawn layer resolves by looking up `skills//SKILL.md` (or a configured alternate path). The bundle typically contains:

- A prompt fragment appended to the agent's system prompt
- An optional list of required tools the skill expects to be available
- An optional set of conventions (file naming, commit format, test layout) encoded as prose
- Any subskills the bundle itself declares

Honest caveat: the parser stores skill names; the resolver that turns a name into bundled content is part of the spawn path and varies by deployment. On the path most users hit (`ostk run agents/*.af`), skill names are passed through to the harness configuration. The Agentfile spec declares the skill; it does not embed it.

RELATED: AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/)

---

# Needles

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/needles/

REFERENCE // WORK_SYSTEM

# Needles

A needle is the atomic unit of work. It has a title, priority, acceptance criteria, and a lifecycle that moves from open to closed. Everything else in the work system — hay, compile, spheres, compounds — exists to create, organize, and prioritize needles.

## 01 // WHAT_A_NEEDLE_IS

A needle is an atomic work item with acceptance criteria. It is the unit of planning, assignment, and completion in ostk. Every needle has a unique ID (format: `→NNN`), a priority tier, and a status. Needles are created explicitly via `ostk work add` or compiled from hay via `ostk work compile`.

## 02 // STORAGE

Needles are stored as JSONL (one JSON object per line) in `.ostk/needles/issues.jsonl`. A monotonic counter at `.ostk/needles/counter` is maintained as a write-through cache, but the canonical source of truth for the next ID is `max(id)` from the JSONL file. Atomicity is enforced by `flock(2)` on `.ostk/needles/issues.lock`.

NEEDLE_FIELDS

`id` Unique identifier. Format: →NNN (e.g. →042). Allocated atomically under the issues lock.

`title` Human-readable summary. Required in strict mode. Also used for dependency pattern discovery.

`status` Current state: "open" or "closed". No intermediate states in storage.

`priority` P0, P1, P2, or P3. Controls sort order in listings and weight in compound scoring.

`ac` Acceptance criteria. A shell command run on close — exit 0 means pass, non-zero means fail.

`description` Longer explanation. Required in strict mode (CLI and MCP entry points).

`tags` Array of string labels (e.g. ["kernel", "ux"]). Parsed from comma-separated input.

`depends_on` Array of needle IDs this needle depends on. Bidirectional — see Dependencies.

`blocks` Array of needle IDs this needle blocks. Bidirectional — see Dependencies.

`created_at` ISO 8601 timestamp. Set on creation.

`closed_at` ISO 8601 timestamp. Set when status transitions to closed.

`close_reason` Optional reason string provided at close time.

`commit_ref` Git HEAD hash captured at close. Links the closure to a specific commit.

## 03 // LIFECYCLE

Two states: open and closed. No draft, no in-review, no backlog. The transition is one-way.

CREATE (ostk work add)

In strict mode (all CLI and MCP entry points), four fields are required: title, priority, description, and acceptance criteria. Priority must be one of P0/P1/P2/P3.

ID allocation and row append happen in a single `flock` acquisition. The previous two-lock pattern was structurally racy — two writers could allocate the same ID.

CLOSE (ostk work close)

If the needle has acceptance criteria, the AC command is executed as a shell command. Exit 0 records `ac_result: "pass"`; non-zero records `"fail"`. The needle is closed regardless of AC outcome.

On close, `commit_ref` is set to the current git HEAD, `closed_at` is timestamped, and an optional `close_reason` is recorded.

ostk work add

```
$ ostk work add \
--title "Add retry logic to nudge delivery" \
--priority P1 \
--description "Nudges to dead targets are currently lost. Add a durable retry queue." \
--ac "cargo test --test nudge_retry"
added →064: Add retry logic to nudge delivery [P1]
```

ostk work close

```
$ ostk work close →064 --reason "implemented in wave G.4"
AC: PASS
closed →064
```

## 04 // HAY

Hay is raw intent capture — a thought, idea, or observation that isn't yet structured enough to be a needle. Filing hay is frictionless: one command, one string, no required fields.

STORAGE

Hay does not have its own file. `ostk hay "thought"` appends a `hay.filed` event to `.ostk/journal.jsonl` with the straw (the text), source, and timestamp. The audit log is the canonical store.

PURPOSE

Hay captures intent before you know the shape of the work. It accumulates until `ostk work compile` triages it — clustering related hay, discovering themes, and identifying which hay entries are needle candidates.

ostk hay

```
$ ostk hay "nudge delivery should retry on dead targets"
hay filed

$ ostk hay "tui overlay needs scrollback buffer"
hay filed
```

## 05 // COMPILE

`ostk work compile` triages hay into needles, discovers dependency edges from title patterns, and rebuilds the sphere model. Three phases, no LLM involved — pure pattern matching.

01

INTELLIGENCE_CLUSTERING

Reads all hay.filed events from the audit log. Clusters hay entries by shared concept terms (a fixed vocabulary of ~80 terms like "kernel", "tui", "agent", "compile"). Uses union-find to group entries that share at least one concept term. When embeddings are available, uses cosine-similarity leader clustering instead (threshold 0.75). Each cluster gets a theme label derived from the most frequent concept term. Clusters are cross-referenced with existing needle spheres via Jaccard similarity on concept overlap.

02

JOINT_GRAPH_REBUILD

Discovers dependency edges from needle titles using regex patterns ("depends on →NNN", "blocks →NNN", "requires →NNN", etc.). Also discovers implicit connections: shared-reference edges (two needles both mention →NNN) and concept edges (two needles share a concept term). Persists newly discovered edges onto needle records as depends_on[] and blocks[] arrays. Rebuilds the full adjacency graph and computes spheres (connected components via DFS).

03

TACK_LINT

Validates each hay entry against the tack grammar. Entries that parse as valid tack commands are flagged as needle candidates. Entries with unknown verbs stay as hay. This pass bridges raw intent (hay) and structured work (needles) without requiring manual triage.

ostk work compile

```
$ ostk work compile
── intelligence: 3 clusters discovered ──
NUDGE (2 hay) → sphere(point=→041)
TUI (3 hay)
+ 1 unclustered

── edges: 12 persisted (4 new, 8 existing) ──

── spheres: 5 connected, 14 isolated ──
sphere 1 | 23 needles | point=→041 (d8) | radius=4
point: Cross-host nudge delivery pipeline
sphere 2 | 11 needles | point=→098 (d5) | radius=3
point: TUI overlay rendering system
```

## Spheres

A sphere is a connected component of the needle dependency graph. Spheres are computed by DFS over the joint adjacency map — every needle reachable from any other needle in the component belongs to the same sphere.

POINT

Each sphere has a point — the hub needle with the highest degree (most connections). Ties are broken by smallest ID for determinism. The point is the center of gravity for the sphere.

RADIUS

BFS from the point computes the radius — the maximum hop distance from the point to any needle in the sphere. Needles closer to the point (lower radius) compound more because they connect to more work.

SORTING

Spheres are sorted by size, largest first. Isolated needles (single-node spheres) are counted but listed separately. When picking the next needle to work on, needles with lower radius within their sphere sort higher among same-priority needles.

JOINTS

The edges connecting needles within a sphere are called joints. Three types: Explicit (title patterns like "depends on →NNN"), SharedRef (two needles both mention the same →NNN), and Concept (two needles share a concept term). Only open needles participate in the graph.

## 06 // COMPOUNDS

`ostk compounds` answers: "What, if completed, unblocks the most other work?" It performs BFS over the `blocks[]` edges to compute transitive fan-out — how many downstream needles are transitively blocked by each needle.

GRAPH

Built from both structured `blocks[]` fields and title-parsed signals ("blocks →NNN", "unblocks →NNN", "enables →NNN"). Reverse edges from `depends_on[]` and title patterns ("depends on →NNN", "requires →NNN", "blocked by →NNN") are also included.

SCORING

Each needle's weighted score = transitive fan-out × priority weight. Priority weights: P0 = 3×, P1 = 2×, P2/P3 = 1×. Results are sorted by weighted score descending, then transitive fan-out, then ID for stability. The top 10 are displayed.

ostk compounds

```
$ ostk compounds
:compounds — highest compounding work

→041 Cross-host nudge delivery pipeline [P0] blocks 3 (transitive: 7)
→098 TUI overlay rendering system [P1] blocks 2 (transitive: 5)
→112 Kernel boot sequence refactor [P1] blocks 1 (transitive: 3)
```

## 07 // DEPENDENCIES

Dependencies are bidirectional: adding `→A depends_on →B` also adds `→B blocks →A`. The `ostk work link` command enforces this symmetry — both sides are updated atomically under the issues lock.

Are dependencies enforced?

No. Dependencies are advisory. Closing →A before →B is allowed. The kernel does not block or warn on out-of-order closure. Dependencies exist to inform compound scoring and sphere structure, not to gate work.

How are edges discovered?

Two paths: explicit (ostk work link →A blocks →B) and implicit (ostk work compile discovers patterns in titles like "depends on →NNN" and persists them as structured depends_on[]/blocks[] arrays).

What about ID normalization?

Needle IDs accept multiple formats: →576, ->576, 576, nd-576, bd-576. All are normalized to canonical →NNN form (zero-padded to 3 digits minimum). The normalization handles both bare numeric and prefixed inputs.

## 08 // PRIORITY_TIERS

Priorities are labels, not automation triggers. There is no auto-dispatch, no SLA enforcement, no escalation policy. Priorities affect two things: sort order and compound weight.

P0

compound weight: 3×

Sorts first in listings. Weights 3× in compound scoring. Use for work that blocks everything else.

P1

compound weight: 2×

Sorts second. Weights 2× in compound scoring. Standard priority for planned work.

P2

compound weight: 1×

Sorts third. Weights 1× in compound scoring. Lower urgency, still tracked.

P3

compound weight: 1×

Sorts last. Weights 1× in compound scoring. Nice-to-have, deferred, or speculative.

## Why This Design

Needles are intentionally minimal. Two states, not five. Advisory dependencies, not enforced gates. Pattern-matched clustering, not LLM triage.

The work system is designed for multi-agent coordination where agents come and go (Five Laws #2). An agent that crashes mid-task leaves behind needles in `issues.jsonl` — a successor agent can read the store, run `ostk work compile` to rebuild the graph, and pick the highest-compounding open needle without any context about the predecessor. The filesystem is the message bus; the needle store is the backlog; the compound score is the prioritizer. No orchestrator required.

Needles are also the unit of measurement in needle-bench.cc (https://needle-bench.cc), the open benchmark for context injection across 26+ models — 32 bugs, each a needle with AC.

RELATED: COMMANDS (/docs/commands/) COORDINATION (/docs/coordination-primitives/) PULL_MODEL (/docs/pull-model/) AGENT_LIFECYCLE (/docs/agent-lifecycle/)

---

# Sphere Navigator

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/sphere-navigator/

REFERENCE // SPHERE_NAVIGATOR

# Sphere Navigator

A four-level overlay that lets you walk the needle dependency graph from the TUI without leaving it. Press `g` to open, `g` again to close. Sphere = a connected component of needles. Source: `src/fcp_screen/components/sphere_nav.rs`.

## FOUR_VIEW_LEVELS

GALAXY

Top view. Every connected sphere in the project as a row, plus a trailing "isolated" entry if any needles stand alone. Cursor moves with j/k or arrow keys; Enter descends into the selected sphere.

`sphere_nav.rs:32–36, 234 (default level), 506`

SPHERE_DETAIL

All needles in one sphere. Shows the connected graph: each needle's edges, priority, status. Enter on a needle descends to NeedleDetail.

`sphere_nav.rs:36, 252–303, 506`

ISOLATED_LIST

Needles with no connections — one per row. Same navigation as SphereDetail. Reached from Galaxy by descending into the isolated entry when there is one.

`sphere_nav.rs:38, 507`

NEEDLE_DETAIL

Full metadata for a single needle: title, description, AC, priority, status, dependencies, blocks, tags. No cursor navigation in this view — it is a leaf. Escape back to the level you came from.

`sphere_nav.rs:40, 307–382, 508`

## KEYBINDINGS

`g` Open / close the sphere navigator overlay

`Option+G (©)` Also opens the overlay (alias for g)

`k / ↑` Move cursor up

`j / ↓` Move cursor down (unless a joint is pending)

`Enter` Descend into the selected item (sphere → detail → needle)

`j (with cursor on needle)` Start or complete a joint — connects two needles as a new dependency edge

`/ (slash)` Enter search mode — type to filter, Enter to confirm, Esc to cancel

`Backspace (search)` Delete one character from the search input

`Tab (search)` Move to next match

`Esc` Cancel search or close overlay

Source: `src/fcp_screen/action.rs:214` (g), `:229` (Option+G), `:243–260` (mode routing). Key handlers live in `src/fcp_screen/app.rs:1086–1160`.

## What the "j" Key Actually Does

In SphereDetail and IsolatedList views, the `j` key is overloaded: without a pending joint it moves the cursor down (the usual vim binding); with a cursor on a needle it starts a "joint" — the first half of a dependency edge.

Press `j` on needle A. The navigator stores A as `pending_joint` and the cursor can move freely to needle B (note: `j` as "move down" is disabled while a joint is pending, per `app.rs:1132`). Press `j` on B to complete the joint — the navigator writes a new dependency edge from A to B into the needle store.

Source: `sphere_nav.rs:57` (pending_joint field), `app.rs:1133–1160` (joint handling). This is the one mutation path in the navigator — every other key is read-only.

RELATED: TUI (/docs/tui/) COMMANDS (/docs/commands/) COORDINATION (/docs/coordination-primitives/)

---

# TUI

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/tui/

REFERENCE // TERMINAL_UI

# TUI

Your central command deck. Launch with `ostk tui`. Monitor agents, dispatch work, approve tool calls.

## PANES

`STATUS_BAR`

Top

Identity counter, boot coherence, uptime, agent states (●active ○stale ✕crashed), open needles, pending hay, API key count

`POST`

Panel

Power-On Self Test. Pipeline throughput: compile → login → delegate → notify. Command: :post

`FLEET`

Panel

Running agents, one row each: ● agent-1 claude-sonnet-4.6 running →573 12s

`ACTIVITY`

Panel

Last 10 audit events. What just happened.

`METRICS`

Side

Tokens saved, compression ratio (HSCP G2)

`LOCKS`

Side

Files currently locked by agents

`STORE`

Side

Files written to .ostk/store/ (output artifacts)

`WORK`

Panel

Needle backlog sorted P0→P3. ▶ = selected. Enter to expand.

`OUTPUT`

Panel

Result of last tack dispatch (kernel output or LLM response)

`TACK_BAR`

Bottom

Multi-line intent editor. Tab to focus, Enter to dispatch.

`QUICKLINE`

Bottom

Single-line fast dispatch. Main tack draft preserved.

`CLOCK_BAR`

Bottom

Session timer, audit count, swap status, context %, date

## KEYBOARD_SHORTCUTS

GLOBAL

Tab Cycle focus through panes

↑/↓ or j/k Scroll active pane

PgUp/PgDn Scroll half-page

Esc Cancel / dismiss modal

Ctrl+C / Ctrl+Q Quit

OPT/ALT OVERLAYS

Opt+? Help overlay

Opt+C Context overlay

Opt+F Fleet overlay

Opt+W Work overlay

Opt+M Model picker

Opt+P Mode picker

Opt+G Sphere navigator

## Tack Grammar

`:` Hard command — kernel dispatch :compile, :reap, :status

`.?` Soft probe — query the OS (LLM) .? what is store

`→NNN` Needle reference →573 to see that needle

`:delegate` Assign needle to agent :delegate →573

`bare text` File as hay (raw intel) thinking about X

## APPROVAL_MODAL

Appears when an agent requests a tool that needs permission. Shows agent alias, tool name, and input preview.

y Approve

n Deny

a Approve All

Enter Deny (default)

## CAPTURE

Record terminal sessions as asciicast v2 files. Zero disruption to the render pipeline — the TeeWriter clones raw ANSI bytes to a background thread.

`:capture [path]` — start recording. Status bar shows [*].

`:capture stop` — stop recording, return output path.

RELATED: COMMANDS (/docs/commands/) SESSION_TOPOLOGY (/docs/session-topology/)

---

# Environment Variables

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/env-vars/

CONFIGURATION // ENV_VARS

# Environment Variables

Runtime configuration that doesn't belong in files. Most are optional — the kernel works with zero env vars set.

01

## AGENT_IDENTITY

`OSTK_AGENT` Agent alias. Set by kernel on spawn. Used for audit attribution, heartbeat, gen_table writer. "unknown"

`OSTK_PIN` Active capability pin name. Loads .ostk/pins/<name>/pin.caps. Tier default

`OSTK_TRUST_TIER` Override trust tier resolution. Bypasses GPG detection. Used by CI/tests. Auto-detected

`OSTK_AGENTFILE` Path to Agentfile to load at boot. None

02

## DAEMON_SCHEDULER

`OSTK_SCHEDULER_TICK_SECONDS` Scheduler tick loop interval. Set to 0 to disable. Disabled

`OSTK_SCHEDULER_STUCK_AFTER_SECS` Seconds before tick escalates from skipped to stuck in audit. 90

`OSTK_SCHEDULER_FORCE_CLEAR_AFTER_SECS` Seconds before force-clearing busy flag. 300

03

## DRAIN_RECOVERY

`OSTK_DRAIN_SNAPSHOT` Controls drain snapshot writes. Set to 0 to disable. Enabled (v4.4.2+)

04

## MODEL_SECRETS

`OSTK_MODEL` Override default model for ostk run, ostk do, FROM auto. Overrides HUMANFILE. HUMANFILE

`OSTK_SECRET_CMD` BYO vault command. Kernel calls $OSTK_SECRET_CMD <key> and reads stdout. None

05

## DIAGNOSTICS

`OSTK_DUMP_REQUEST` Directory for full API request JSON dumps. For debugging cache/preload. Disabled

`OSTK_DUMP_HANDSHAKE` File path for handshake bytes at boot. For debugging byte-stability. Disabled

`OSTK_NO_CACHE` Set to 1 to disable cache_control breakpoints. Disabled

`OSTK_T2_OBSERVER` Set to 1 for Hot PR Tier 2 arbitration audit rows. Disabled

06

## SYSTEM

`OSTK_STATE_DIR` Override state directory name (instead of .ostk). .ostk

`OSTK_DIR` Absolute path to .ostk directory. Set by kernel for FCP driver subprocesses. Auto

`OSTK_REGISTRY_DIR` Override global registry directory. Used by CI for isolation. ~/.ostk/

`OSTK_PREPROCESS` Local model for input preprocessing via olleh runtime. Overrides HUMANFILE PREPROCESS. HUMANFILE

`OSTK_TOOL_THRESHOLD` Momentum threshold for verb loading from .language. Float 0-1. 0.45

RELATED: OSTK_TOML (/docs/ostk-toml/) HUMANFILE (/docs/humanfile/) SECRETS (/docs/secrets/)

---

# Downloads

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/downloads/

ONBOARDING // BINARIES

# Downloads

Single Rust binary. Zero runtime dependencies. GPG-verified install.

## INSTALL_SCRIPT

TERMINAL

$ curl -fsSL https://ostk.ai/install | sh

Auto-detects platform, downloads the correct binary, verifies GPG signature, places in `$PATH`.

## SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS

macOS arm64 `aarch64-apple-darwin` Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4)

macOS x86_64 `x86_64-apple-darwin` Intel Macs

Linux arm64 `aarch64-unknown-linux-musl` ARM servers, Raspberry Pi

Linux x86_64 `x86_64-unknown-linux-musl` Standard Linux servers

## BUILD_FROM_SOURCE

TERMINAL

$ cargo install ostk

Requires Rust 1.85+ (MSRV). Builds a statically linked binary.

NEXT: QUICK_START (/start/)

---

# How-to: Learn a Sandbox Policy

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/how-to/learn-sandbox/

HOW_TO // LEARN_SANDBOX

# How to: Learn a Sandbox Policy

You don't have to guess which paths an agent needs. Run it once under `nono learn`, collect the read/readwrite list, and bake it into the Agentfile. Two commands. Source: `src/commands/learn.rs`.

## PREREQUISITES

- `ostk` built with the `sandbox` feature. Without it, `ostk learn` exits with the error "ostk learn requires the sandbox feature — rebuild with: `cargo build --features sandbox`". Source: `learn.rs:16–25`.
- The `nono` binary on PATH. `ostk learn` shells out to `nono learn -- ` and parses its output. If nono is missing you get "nono learn failed (is nono installed?)". Source: `learn.rs:164–171`.
- An Agentfile you want to sandbox. Either a path (`agents/worker.af`) or a bare name the kernel can resolve (`worker`).

## WALKTHROUGH

01

Start from an Agentfile with no sandbox directives

No LIMIT sandbox.read or sandbox.readwrite yet. The agent currently runs at ISOLATION none — it can touch anything. That's the starting point ostk learn improves on.

02

Observe

ostk learn agents/worker.af runs the canonical spawn command (ostk run agents/worker.af) under nono learn. nono traces every open, read, and write syscall the process makes. An optional --duration <secs> caps the observation window. Source: learn.rs:30–90, 121–134.

03

Review the discovered directives

Output is printed to stdout. Two lines per unique path: "LIMIT sandbox.read <path>" or "LIMIT sandbox.readwrite <path>". ostk learn deduplicates against existing LIMIT sandbox entries in the Agentfile so rerunning against an already-learned file only shows new paths. Source: learn.rs:78–85.

04

Apply (optional)

Rerun with --apply to append the new directives directly to the Agentfile. Without --apply the directives are printed only, so you can pipe them, diff them, or paste them by hand. Source: learn.rs:90–95.

05

Switch to ISOLATION nono

Add ISOLATION nono to the Agentfile. Now the next ostk run spawns the agent into a Landlock (Linux) or Seatbelt (macOS) sandbox restricted to exactly the paths ostk learn observed. Source: /docs/isolation-tiers.

06

Audit confirms

ostk learn emits learn.started and learn.completed events to .ostk/journal.jsonl with the directive count. Useful when the same Agentfile is learned from multiple machines and you want to see which run produced which rule set. Source: learn.rs:48–56, 80–87.

## SAMPLE_SESSION

discovery run

$ ostk learn agents/worker.af --duration 30

ostk learn: observing agents/worker.af via nono learn

ostk learn: command = ostk run agents/worker.af

(agent runs to completion or 30s timeout)

ostk learn: discovered 4 new directive(s)

LIMIT sandbox.read /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib

LIMIT sandbox.read /etc/ssl/cert.pem

LIMIT sandbox.readwrite /tmp/worker-scratch

LIMIT sandbox.readwrite ./src

apply + lock down

$ ostk learn agents/worker.af --apply

ostk learn: appended directives to agents/worker.af

$ # add ISOLATION nono by hand

$ echo "ISOLATION nono" >> agents/worker.af

$ ostk run agents/worker.af

(now sandboxed — any access outside the learned set returns EACCES)

## Caveats

- Observation coverage is only as good as the run. If the agent never needed `/opt/homebrew/bin/node` during the learn session but it does on a cold start, the learned policy will block the cold start. Run the agent through its real workload, not a trivial smoke test.
- Non-zero exit codes are expected. nono itself passes through the observed process's exit status. ostk learn only fails if nono itself can't start. Source: `learn.rs:173–183`.
- Sensitive paths still appear in the output. If the agent accidentally reads `~/.ssh/id_rsa` during observation, that path shows up in the discovered directives. Review before `--apply`.
- Deduplication is exact-path only. If the Agentfile already has `LIMIT sandbox.read /usr` and observation finds `/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib`, the specific path will still be added. Existing substring coverage is not deduced. Source: `learn.rs:75–78`.

RELATED: ISOLATION_TIERS (/docs/isolation-tiers/) AGENTFILE (/docs/agentfile/) APPROVAL_CHAIN (/docs/approval-chain/)

---

# FAQ

URL: https://ostk.ai/docs/faq/

REFERENCE // FAQ

# FAQ

Common questions from developers evaluating or getting started with ostk.

01 How do multiple AI agents edit the same file without conflicts?

ostk uses optimistic concurrency control. Every file:edit() specifies the text the agent expects. If another agent changed it, the kernel detects the mismatch and resolves it through a 3-tier system: auto-merge for non-overlapping edits, mechanical rebase for nearby edits, and manual escalation for structural conflicts. No locking, no blocking.

READ_MORE → (/features/coordination)

02 How do I install ostk?

One command: curl -fsSL https://ostk.ai/install | sh. The script detects your platform (macOS arm64/x86_64, Linux arm64/x86_64), downloads the correct binary, verifies its GPG signature, and places it in your $PATH. Then run ostk init and ostk boot.

READ_MORE → (/start)

03 Do I need to modify my AI agents to work with ostk?

No. The write path is invisible — agents edit files with their normal tools. The OS intercepts writes at the tool layer, coordinates between agents, and maintains an audit trail. Agents never know ostk is there. This is the first of the Five Laws.

READ_MORE → (/features/invisible-writes)

04 How do I keep API keys safe from AI agents?

Secret values never enter the LLM context. The kernel resolves keys from your platform keychain (macOS Keychain, Linux secret-tool) at call time and injects them via subprocess environment variables. All tool output passes through a masking layer that replaces any secret values before the LLM sees them.

READ_MORE → (/docs/secrets)

05 What is my trust tier?

Trust is determined by GPG cross-signatures. T0: your GPG key fingerprint is hardcoded in the kernel trust anchors (full governance — not reachable by ceremony). T1: your key is cross-signed by a T0 holder (write access). T2: GPG key present, not cross-signed (read + narrow write exceptions). T3: no GPG key (boots, reads everything, writes denied by pin caps). Run ostk boot to see your tier.

READ_MORE → (/docs/trust-model)

06 Can AI agents work on the same repository without conflicts?

Yes. ostk coordinates file access across agents through optimistic concurrency. Each agent gets a workspace boundary. Generation counters track every file write. When conflicts occur, the kernel resolves them mechanically — no LLM is called for conflict resolution. In practice, conflicts drop to near zero.

READ_MORE → (/features/coordination)

07 How do I track what changes AI agents made?

Every agent action is logged to an append-only audit trail (.ostk/journal.jsonl). File edits, shell commands, tool calls, spawns, approvals — all timestamped and attributed. Run ostk os audit check to verify trail integrity. The process table is reconstructible from the audit log at any point.

READ_MORE → (/features/audit-trail)

08 Does ostk require a cloud connection?

No. ostk is local-first. Agent coordination happens through local files. The only network calls are to LLM providers when you run agents. The kernel itself is entirely offline. No telemetry, no analytics, no cloud sync.

READ_MORE → (/features/local-first)

09 What happens if an agent crashes?

Agents are ephemeral — crashes are the expected operating condition. The kernel writes drain snapshots at every turn boundary. On daemon restart, fold(journal.jsonl) reconstructs the fleet and rehydrates sessions from snapshots. Mid-flight, the heartbeat monitor detects crashed workers and hot-rehydrates them.

READ_MORE → (/docs/session-topology)

10 What is a needle?

A needle is an atomic, executable work item with acceptance criteria. It is the unit of work in ostk, stored in .ostk/needles/issues.jsonl. Create one with ostk work add --priority P0 "title". Needles have priority tiers (P0-P3), status tracking, and dependency links.

READ_MORE → (/docs/commands)

11 What is tack?

Tack is the operator intent language in the TUI. You type commands into the tack bar: :compile dispatches to the kernel, .? queries the OS through the LLM, →573 references a needle, and bare text is filed as hay (raw unstructured intent for later compilation into needles).

READ_MORE → (/features/tack)

12 How does ostk differ from Cursor, Devin, or Aider?

ostk is an OS, not an IDE or agent. It coordinates multiple agents from any provider (Claude, Gemini, Mistral) on the same codebase simultaneously. Cursor and Aider are single-agent tools. Devin is a cloud-hosted agent. ostk runs locally, is agent-agnostic, and provides trust governance, audit trails, and crash recovery that none of these tools offer.

READ_MORE → (/about)

## GLOSSARY

Key terms used throughout the documentation.

`needle` An atomic, executable work item with acceptance criteria. Stored as YAML frontmatter + markdown in .ostk/needles/. The unit of work in ostk.

`hay` Raw unstructured intent captured for later compilation into needles. Filed via bare text in the tack bar or ostk hay "...".

`tack` The operator intent language used in the TUI. Commands prefixed with : dispatch to the kernel. Bare text files as hay.

`drain snapshot` Session state written to .ostk/drain/<lineage_id>.json at every turn boundary. Contains conversation history, token accounting, and config. Enables crash recovery.

`anchor` The long-lived daemon process started by ostk listen. Holds the socket, process table, and scheduler. Survives client disconnects.

`lineage` A stable identity for an ongoing piece of work (e.g., "scheduler", "worker-a"). Outlives both the client that spawned it and the anchor that ran it.

`FCP` File Context Protocol — the interface for drivers that extend the kernel with domain capabilities. MCP servers that let LLMs interact with complex file formats.

`Hot PR` The kernel's 3-tier conflict resolution system. Tier 1: auto-merge (non-overlapping). Tier 2: mechanical rebase (nearby). Tier 3: manual escalation.

`generation counter` A monotonic counter incremented on every file write. Used for optimistic concurrency — if the counter changed, another agent modified the file.

`bail` A portable, signed OS bundle. Named for a bail of hay — what you extract from a haystack. Contains public identity (.primefile, ENTITYFILE), boot state, and optionally encrypted internal kernel state or selective exported content. Three modes: public, full, export. See /docs/bail.

`fold(journal.jsonl)` Replaying the append-only audit log to reconstruct fleet state. The process table is a projection of the audit log — the daemon is a cache, not a ledger.

`.language` A pipe-delimited table of verbs, their tiers, layers, and usage momentum. The kernel loads high-momentum verbs at boot and demand-spawns the rest.

`sub-stack` An isolated group of sessions with its own audit log, drain directory, token budget, and IPC channels. Created by the scheduler for multi-worker tasks.

`OCC` Optimistic Concurrency Control. Agents write freely; the kernel detects conflicts via generation counters at commit time, not lock time.

---

# Features

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/

FEATURE_INDEX // v4.4.2

# Features

ostk is an OS for AI coding agents. These are the things it does.

01

Conflicts resolved at write time without locks or message buses. (/features/coordination/)03 File Write Tracking Agents edit files normally. The OS intercepts, coordinates, and audits underneath. (/features/invisible-writes/)04 Audit Trail Every edit tracked, cryptographically signed, append-only. (/features/audit-trail/)

02

## OPERATIONS

05 Local-First No cloud, no telemetry. A single Rust binary on your machine. (/features/local-first/)06 Tack Language Compressed intent language. The OS learns your vocabulary over time. (/features/tack/)07 Trust & Security GPG web of trust. Capability pins enforce invisible boundaries. (/features/governance/)

03

## TOOLS

Platform keychain integration. (/features/secret-management/)10 Terminal Dashboard Terminal interface showing the OS in real time. Agents, permissions, edits, costs. (/features/tui/)

---

# AI Agent Coordination

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/coordination/

FEATURE // MULTI_AGENT

# AI Agent Coordination

Run multiple AI agents on the same codebase. The OS resolves file conflicts at write time — no locks, no message buses, no coordination code.

## The problem

You spawn two agents. One refactors the auth module. The other adds a new API endpoint that imports from auth. Both edit `src/auth/middleware.js` within seconds.

Without coordination, the last write wins and the first agent's work disappears. With locking, the second agent blocks for 40 turns of idle time. With message buses, both agents need a coordination protocol they were never trained on.

## How it works

The daemon (`ostk listen`) is the anchor (/docs/session-topology/). Agents run as managed sessions with heartbeat monitoring, drain snapshots (/docs/session-topology/), and crash recovery. The operator spawns workers directly:

`ostk kernel spawn auth-refactor --model sonnet --budget 2 "Refactor auth to JWT"`

Each agent works independently. When edits don't overlap, writes go through immediately. When they do, the kernel resolves the conflict.

## Conflict resolution tiers

Every `file:edit()` is a compare-and-swap: the agent sends the `old_str` it expects to find and the `new_str` to replace it with. If `old_str` no longer matches — another agent modified it — the resolution tiers engage. No LLM is called. This is mechanical text diffing, implemented in `src/kernel/hotpr.rs`. Every conflict is recorded in the audit trail (/features/audit-trail/).

- Tier 1 — Auto-merge. The agent's intended edit and the other agent's changes are more than `PROXIMITY_LINES = 3` lines apart. Both changes apply silently. Neither agent is interrupted.
- Tier 2 — Assisted merge. Edits are within 3 lines of each other and under `TIER2_MAX_CHANGED_LINES = 30` changed lines, with at most `TIER2_MAX_CONFLICT_REGIONS = 2` conflict regions. The kernel computes a mechanical line rebase and returns a suggested `file:edit()` call the agent can accept, modify, or reject. Still no model call.
- Tier 3 — Manual rebase. Above Tier 2 thresholds. The kernel returns conflict info and the agent re-reads the file from disk. If the rebase falls through Tier 2 (tier2_line_rebase returns None), an ArbitrationObservation is written to `.ostk/journal.jsonl` — the Phase 0 observer for a future small-model arbiter.

## Fleet economics

The prompt cache at Anthropic's edge is content-addressed and per-organization. Two ostk processes with the same API key share cache entries automatically. Adding more agents amortizes the cold-write cost across readers. Fleet cost scales sublinearly.

RELATED_FEATURES

Audit Trail Every edit tracked, signed, and queryable (/features/audit-trail/)Invisible Writes Agents write files normally. The OS handles the rest. (/features/invisible-writes/)Session Topology Anchors, lineages, crash recovery (/docs/session-topology/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# AI Agent File Write Tracking

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/invisible-writes/

FEATURE // CORE_ARCHITECTURE

# AI Agent File Write Tracking

Agents edit files with their normal tools. The OS intercepts every write, coordinates between agents, and maintains an audit trail. The agent never knows.

## The principle

AI agents already know how to write files. Claude uses `write_to_file`. GPT uses code interpreter. Every agent has a file-writing mechanism built in.

ostk does not replace these tools. It does not ask agents to learn a new API, call a special endpoint, or use a custom SDK. Agents write files exactly the way they normally would. The OS intercepts those writes, handles coordination and tracking, and lets the write complete.

## What happens when an agent writes a file

- The agent issues a file write through its normal tool.
- The OS intercepts the write before it hits disk.
- The OS checks for conflicts with other agents on the same file.
- The OS records the write in the audit trail — who, what, when, and the full diff.
- The write completes. The file on disk matches what the agent intended.

## Byte-for-byte passthrough

The OS never modifies the content of a write (outside of conflict resolution). If an agent writes 847 bytes to `src/index.js`, exactly 847 bytes land on disk. No comments injected, no watermarks, no reformatting.

This matters because agents verify their own writes. Many agents read a file back after writing to confirm the edit took effect. If the OS modified the content, the agent would detect the discrepancy and enter a retry loop. Byte-for-byte passthrough means self-verification always succeeds.

## How the interception actually works

Default interception is at the tool layer. Agents write through kernel primitives (`file:edit`, `file:write`, `file:create`) that apply policy, locking, and version tracking before bytes hit disk. No `LD_PRELOAD`, no `ptrace`, no `kqueue` observer. If a write went through the tool, the kernel saw it, tracked it, and appended it to the audit trail.

The honest question: what if an agent bypasses the tool and writes directly through a shell? Two defenses handle that:

- Process sandbox (always on). The `nono` subsystem enforces filesystem restrictions at the OS layer — Landlock on Linux, Seatbelt on macOS. Direct writes outside allowed paths physically fail at the syscall level, regardless of which tool the agent used. Source: `src/kernel/sandbox.rs`.
- FUSE passthrough (optional). `ostk mount` (built with `--features fuse`) mounts a FUSE filesystem that mirrors the project tree and logs every write op. Off by default because FUSE adds dependencies; turned on when operators want every byte logged regardless of how it got written. Source: `src/fuse/passthrough.rs`.

Tool layer covers the expected path (and gets you conflict resolution + audit). The sandbox covers the escape path (containment, not observability). FUSE is the observability upgrade for users who need both. No single layer is load-bearing on its own — and none of them require the agent to know they exist.

RELATED_FEATURES

Multi-Agent Coordination Conflict resolution across agents (/features/coordination/)Audit Trail Every edit tracked and signed (/features/audit-trail/)Five Laws The design constraints (/docs/five-laws/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# AI Agent Audit Trail

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/audit-trail/

FEATURE // OBSERVABILITY

# AI Agent Audit Trail

Every edit tracked, cryptographically signed, append-only. You know who changed what and when. fold(journal.jsonl) reconstructs the fleet at any point in time.

## What gets logged

Every agent action is an append-only audit event in `.ostk/journal.jsonl`. File edits, shell commands, tool calls, agent spawns, approvals, denials, conflicts, and resolutions. Each event carries a timestamp, agent alias, action type, and target.

## Reconstructible state

The daemon is a cache, not a ledger. The source of truth is `journal.jsonl`. Running `fold(journal.jsonl)` replays the log and reconstructs the exact fleet state — which agents were alive, what files they touched, what their context looked like. The process table is a projection of the audit log.

This means: daemon crashes lose nothing. A new daemon reads the log and rebuilds. Mid-flight crashes are detected by heartbeat and recovered from drain snapshots.

## Querying the trail

`ostk audit --since 1h` shows recent events. `ostk audit --agent lint-fix` filters to one agent. The format is newline-delimited JSON — you can pipe it through `jq`, `grep`, or any tool that handles JSONL.

## Append-only guarantees

The audit log is written with `O_APPEND` under `PIPE_BUF` — lockless, atomic, and tamper-evident. Entries are never modified or deleted. If the log file has been truncated or altered, the kernel detects it at boot.

RELATED_FEATURES

Multi-Agent Coordination Conflict resolution across agents (/features/coordination/)Invisible Writes Agents write files normally (/features/invisible-writes/)Trust & Governance GPG web of trust (/features/governance/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# AI Agent Context Management

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/context-injection/

FEATURE // CONTEXT

# AI Agent Context Management

Agents skip exploration. The OS front-loads project context — repository structure, recent changes, active needles, and governance — so the first turn is productive.

## The problem

A new agent session starts with an empty context window. The agent's first 3-5 turns are spent reading files, understanding the project, and orienting. That exploration burns tokens and time. If the agent crashes and restarts, it explores again.

## What the kernel injects

At session start, the kernel prepends a digest block to the agent's context. The digest contains:

- Repository structure — file tree, language breakdown, key entry points
- Recent changes — git diff summary, active branches, last N commits
- Active needles — open work items with priority and acceptance criteria
- Governance context — trust tier, active pins, HUMANFILE preferences
- Fleet state — other running agents, their targets, potential conflicts

## Cache economics

The digest is byte-stable — it changes only when the underlying state changes. This means it hits the prompt cache on subsequent turns. Fleet-wide, all agents sharing the same API key share the same cache prefix. The cold-write amortizes across readers.

RELATED_FEATURES

Multi-Agent Coordination Agents share context efficiently (/features/coordination/)Compression Output compression pipeline (/docs/compression/)Architecture Memory hierarchy and cache economics (/docs/architecture/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# Local-First AI Agent Coordination

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/local-first/

FEATURE // ARCHITECTURE

# Local-First AI Agent Coordination

No cloud, no telemetry. A single Rust binary on your machine. Agent coordination happens through local files — no API calls, no cloud sync.

## What local-first means

Your code stays on your machine. Agent coordination happens through local files — `.ostk/` state, generation counters, audit logs. No central server, no coordination API, no telemetry endpoint.

The only network calls ostk makes are to LLM providers (Anthropic, Google, Mistral, OpenRouter) — and only when you ask it to run an agent. The kernel itself is entirely offline.

## Single binary

ostk is a statically linked Rust binary. No runtime dependencies. No Node, no Python, no Docker. Install is one command: `curl -fsSL https://ostk.ai/install | sh`. The binary GPG-verifies itself during install.

## Works with local models

Set `OSTK_MODEL` to a local model endpoint and agents run entirely on your hardware. The CpuDriver trait abstracts the provider boundary — the kernel doesn't know whether it's talking to Anthropic's API or a local ollama instance.

## No telemetry

ostk does not phone home. No analytics, no crash reporting, no usage tracking. The audit trail is local. The metrics are local. The drain snapshots are local. Run `ostk status` and see exactly what's on disk.

RELATED_FEATURES

Secret Management Keys never leave your machine (/features/secret-management/)Audit Trail Local append-only event log (/features/audit-trail/)Architecture Single binary, zero deps (/docs/architecture/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# AI Agent Trust Model and Security

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/governance/

FEATURE // TRUST

# AI Agent Trust Model and Security

GPG web of trust. Four trust tiers. Capability pins enforce invisible boundaries. No admin panels, no OAuth — identity is cryptographic.

## Identity is cryptographic

Trust in ostk is determined by GPG cross-signatures, not platform accounts. Your trust tier resolves at boot from the web of trust anchored at the @ostk.ai root key. No OAuth flow, no API tokens, no admin dashboard.

## Four trust tiers

- T0 — Fingerprint hardcoded in `src/kernel/identity.rs` trust anchors. Full governance. Can modify ENTITYFILE and .primefile. Not reachable by ceremony — requires a kernel release that includes your key.
- T1 — GPG key cross-signed by a T0 holder. Write access. Denied from kernel and governance paths via pin caps.
- T2 — GPG key present, not cross-signed. Boots; read-only.
- T3 — No GPG key. Boots; all writes denied by pin caps (policy.rs:248).

## Capability pins

Pins are deny-based restrictions at the tool layer. Each trust tier has default deny tokens. Explicit pins can only add restrictions — the tier is a floor, not an override. A T3 user setting `OSTK_PIN=permissive` still gets deny-all.

## Governance ceremonies

Changes to the ENTITYFILE require a negotiate protocol: file an offer, counter-party reviews, both parties sign with their keys, merge with `--no-ff`. Every trust change is auditable in git history. No silent governance changes.

RELATED_FEATURES

Trust Model T0-T3 tiers and capability chains (/docs/trust-model/)Capability Pins Deny-based agent boundaries (/docs/pins/)ENTITYFILE The trust constitution (/docs/entityfile/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# Tack — AI Agent Intent Language

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/tack/

FEATURE // LANGUAGE

# Tack — AI Agent Intent Language

Compressed intent language. The OS learns your vocabulary over time. Communication gets shorter as you work.

## What tack is

Tack is the operator intent language in the TUI. You type intent into the tack bar. The kernel resolves it — sometimes to a command, sometimes to an LLM query, sometimes to a needle reference.

## Grammar

- `:` prefix — Hard command. Dispatches directly to the kernel. `:compile`, `:reap`, `:status`.
- `.?` prefix — Soft probe. Queries the OS through the LLM. `.? what is store`.
- `→NNN` — Needle reference. `→573` opens that needle.
- Bare text — Filed as hay (raw unstructured intent). Later compiled into needles via `:compile`.

## The .language file

The OS maintains a `.language` file — a pipe-delimited table of verbs, their tiers, layers, and usage momentum. As you use verbs, their momentum increases. Unused verbs decay. The kernel loads high-momentum verbs at boot and demand-spawns the rest.

Over time, your tack vocabulary converges on the commands you actually use. The OS adapts to you, not the other way around.

RELATED_FEATURES

TUI Terminal interface with tack bar (/features/tui/)Commands Full CLI reference (/docs/commands/)Five Laws The design constraints (/docs/five-laws/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# Switch AI Models Mid-Session

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/model-switching/

FEATURE // FLEXIBILITY

# Switch AI Models Mid-Session

Switch models mid-session. Context survives the handoff. Works across vendors — Anthropic, Google, Mistral, OpenRouter.

## How it works

The CpuDriver trait abstracts the model boundary. Below it, the kernel doesn't know whether it's talking to Claude, Gemini, or Mistral. Above it, your agent session continues with the same context, the same tools, the same permissions.

## Fallback chains

Set `MODEL` and `FALLBACK` in your HUMANFILE. If the primary model is unavailable or rate-limited, the kernel switches to the fallback automatically. The agent doesn't see the switch.

## Per-agent models

The `FROM` directive in an Agentfile specifies the model for that agent. Different agents in the same fleet can run different models. The scheduler on Opus, workers on Sonnet, reviewers on Haiku.

## Available models

The `AVAILABLE <<MODELS` heredoc in your HUMANFILE lists all authorized models. Agents with `FROM auto` select from this list based on task requirements.

RELATED_FEATURES

HUMANFILE Model and fallback configuration (/docs/humanfile/)Agentfile FROM directive for model selection (/docs/agentfile/)Architecture CpuDriver trait abstraction (/docs/architecture/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# AI Agent API Key Security

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/secret-management/

FEATURE // SECURITY

# AI Agent API Key Security

API keys never enter LLM context, audit logs, or shell history. Resolved from platform keychain at call time. All output masked.

## The guarantee

Secret values never appear in the LLM context window. The kernel resolves keys from the platform keychain (macOS Keychain, Linux secret-tool) at call time and injects them via subprocess environment variables. The agent sees the tool succeed but never sees the key.

## Output masking

All tool output passes through `secrets::mask()` before the LLM sees it. If a secret value appears anywhere — Bash output, file reads, error messages, env dumps — it gets replaced with `sk-ant-●●●● (48 chars)`. Prefix preserved, length hinted, value hidden.

## Authorization

The `SECRET <<KEYS` heredoc in your HUMANFILE is the only place where API keys are authorized for agent use. If a key isn't listed, agents cannot access it — even if it exists in your system keychain.

## BYO vault

Set `OSTK_SECRET_CMD` to your preferred secret manager — Bitwarden CLI, 1Password CLI, `pass`, or any command that takes a key name and returns the value on stdout. The kernel calls it transparently.

RELATED_FEATURES

Secrets Commands, resolution order, masking (/docs/secrets/)HUMANFILE SECRET directive (/docs/humanfile/)Trust & Governance Trust tier enforcement (/features/governance/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# AI Agent Terminal Dashboard

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/tui/

FEATURE // INTERFACE

# AI Agent Terminal Dashboard

Terminal interface showing the OS in real time. Agents, permissions, edits, costs — all visible. The operator sees everything.

## What you see

Launch `ostk tui` and the terminal becomes a command deck. Fleet status in one pane, audit events in another, needle backlog below, tack bar at the bottom. Every agent action surfaces in real time.

## Panes

- FLEET — Running agents with status indicators: ● active, ○ stale, ✕ crashed
- ACTIVITY — Last 10 audit events. What just happened.
- WORK — Needle backlog sorted by priority (P0→P3)
- METRICS — Token savings, compression ratio, cache hit rate
- TACK BAR — Multi-line intent editor for operator commands

## Permission gates

When an agent requests a tool that needs approval, a modal appears with the agent alias, tool name, and input preview. Press `y` to approve, `n` to deny, `a` to approve all for that capability class. Enter defaults to deny — hitting return without thinking rejects the call. This is a safety default: a distracted operator's reflex should never be to approve. Source: `src/fcp_screen/action.rs:286`.

## Capture

Record terminal sessions as asciicast v2 files with `:capture`. The TeeWriter clones raw ANSI bytes to a background thread — zero disruption to the render pipeline. Status bar shows [*] when recording.

RELATED_FEATURES

TUI Reference Panes, shortcuts, capture (/docs/tui/)Tack The operator intent language (/features/tack/)Commands Full CLI reference (/docs/commands/)

GET_STARTED (/start/)

---

# How ostk Compares to AI Coding Tools

URL: https://ostk.ai/features/comparison/

POSITIONING // COMPARISON

# How ostk Compares

ostk is an operating system, not an IDE or agent. It coordinates multiple agents from any provider on the same codebase simultaneously.

Cursor and Aider are single-agent tools. Devin is a cloud-hosted agent. SWE-agent runs one task at a time. ostk runs locally, is agent-agnostic, and provides trust governance, audit trails, and crash recovery that none of these tools offer.

Capability ostk Cursor Devin Aider SWE-agent

Multi-agent coordination Yes — optimistic concurrency with 3-tier conflict resolution (auto-merge, mechanical rebase, manual) No — single agent per session No — single agent, cloud-hosted No — single agent No — single agent per task

Runs locally (no cloud) Yes — single Rust binary, zero dependencies, no telemetry Partial — IDE extension, some features need cloud No — cloud-only Yes — Python CLI, runs locally Yes — Python, runs locally

Agent-agnostic (any LLM) Yes — CpuDriver trait abstracts providers (Anthropic, Google, Mistral, OpenRouter, local) No — Cursor-specific models No — Devin-specific Yes — supports multiple providers Yes — supports multiple providers

File write interception Tool-layer — invisible to agents, byte-for-byte passthrough, generation counters N/A — single agent, no coordination needed N/A — cloud sandbox Git-based — uses git diff for tracking N/A — single agent

Trust model GPG web of trust — 4 tiers (T0-T3), dual-signed primefile, capability pins None — relies on IDE permissions None — cloud access controls None None

Audit trail Append-only JSONL — every action timestamped, attributed, reconstructible via fold() None — IDE undo history only Partial — session logs in cloud Git log — commit-level tracking Log files — not structured

Crash recovery Drain snapshots at every turn boundary. Automatic rehydration on daemon restart. IDE session recovery Cloud session persistence None — restart from scratch None — restart from scratch

Token cost reduction Prompt cache (32% hit rate) + shell output squash. Measured 28% cost reduction on real opus session. None Unknown None None

Secret management Platform keychain integration. Values never enter LLM context. All output masked. IDE credential store Cloud secrets Environment variables (visible in context) Environment variables

Process sandbox Landlock (Linux) + Seatbelt (macOS) — OS-level syscall enforcement None Cloud container None Docker container (optional)

## KEY_DIFFERENTIATORS

01

### ostk is an OS, not an agent

Cursor, Devin, and Aider are agents or agent wrappers. ostk sits below the agent layer. It coordinates any agent from any provider — Claude, Gemini, Mistral, or local models. You choose the agent; ostk handles the infrastructure.

02

### Multiple agents, simultaneously

Every other tool runs one agent at a time. ostk runs a fleet. The scheduler dispatches work. Workers run in parallel. Conflicts resolve mechanically. Fleet cost scales sublinearly because agents share the prompt cache.

03

### Trust is cryptographic, not organizational

No tool in this space has a trust model. ostk uses GPG cross-signatures to determine who can write what. Four tiers, dual-signed governance, deny-based capability pins. Agents earn access; they don't start with it.

04

### Everything is auditable and reconstructible

Aider uses git commits. Devin has cloud logs. ostk has an append-only JSONL audit trail where fold(journal.jsonl) can reconstruct the exact fleet state at any point in time. Crash recovery is built into the kernel.

## When to Use ostk

USE_OSTK_WHEN

- You run 2+ AI agents on the same codebase
- You need to audit every change agents make
- You want agents from different providers working together
- You need trust boundaries and capability restrictions
- You want crash recovery and session persistence
- You prefer local-first with no cloud dependency

CONSIDER_ALTERNATIVES_WHEN

- You use one agent at a time (Cursor or Aider may suffice)
- You want a managed cloud experience (Devin)
- You don't need audit trails or trust governance
- You want an integrated IDE rather than a terminal OS

GET_STARTED (/start/) ALL_FEATURES (/features/)

---

# About ostk

URL: https://ostk.ai/about/

LINEAGE // ABOUT

# About ostk

Not a framework. Not a wrapper. A distributed operating system — the kind that disappears the moment it works.

## WHAT_IT_DOES

`FLEET_COORDINATION`

Spawn, drain, reap, and rehydrate agents across daemon restarts. Workers crash-recover from drain snapshots automatically.

`OPTIMISTIC_CONCURRENCY`

Agents write concurrently. The kernel detects conflicts via generation counters and resolves via Hot PR. No locks during the expensive phase.

`COST_REDUCTION`

28% measured cost reduction via prompt cache reuse and shell output compression.

`CAPABILITY_ENFORCEMENT`

Deny-based pins at the tool layer. Process-level sandboxing via Landlock/Seatbelt at the syscall layer. Defense in depth.

`IDENTITY_AND_TRUST`

GPG-based trust chain from hardware identity to human attestation. Four tiers. Dual-signed primefile ceremony for governance changes.

`CACHE_ECONOMICS`

Byte-stable system prefix shared across the fleet via content-addressed cache. Fleet cost scales sublinearly — adding agents decreases per-agent cost.

## The Story

ostk wasn't designed in a planning meeting. It was extracted from the problem it solves.

The kernel started as a coordination layer for AI agents on the same codebase. First version handled file conflicts. Then process supervision. Then output compression. Then identity, heartbeat, audit trails, scheduling, drain snapshots, crash recovery, sub-stacks, and fleet-wide cache sharing.

At some point the tool stack disappeared. No more Jira for tracking. No more Slack for coordination. No more separate CI. The OS absorbed it all — not by replacing each tool deliberately, but because each problem had a simpler solution when the OS already knew about every agent, every file, and every write.

## Source Availability

The kernel source is not currently published. An open source release under a dual license is planned.

The binary is free for personal and internal commercial use. Governance documents, signing keys, install script, and this site are public. See License (/legal/license/).

## CONTACT

Built by agents running on ostk. Maintained by humans who speak tack.

contact@ostk.ai (mailto:contact@ostk.ai) Discord (https://discord.gg/MdRNs4CC) GitHub (https://github.com/os-tack)

---

# Security

URL: https://ostk.ai/security/

GOVERNANCE // SECURITY

# Security

Every write is signed. Every identity is verified. Every conflict is resolved — never silently.

## TRUST_TIERS

T0

Fingerprint in kernel trust anchors

Hardcoded in src/kernel/identity.rs (shipped with binary)

None — full governance

T1

Cross-signed by T0 holder

Your GPG key signed by a T0 key

Cannot modify OS or governance files

T2

GPG key present, not cross-signed

GPG key exists, no T0 signature

Read everything; narrow write exceptions

T3

No GPG key

host_identity returns no gpg_key

Boots read-only; all writes denied by pin caps (policy.rs:248)

Full details: Trust Model (/docs/trust-model/)

## DEFENSE_LAYERS

TOOL_LAYER

Deny-based capability pins. Every tool call checked against pin.caps deny tokens before execution.

SYSCALL_LAYER

Process-level sandboxing via Landlock (Linux) and Seatbelt (macOS). OS-enforced boundaries.

AUDIT_LAYER

Append-only JSONL event log. Every agent action timestamped and attributed. fold(journal.jsonl) reconstructs state.

SECRET_LAYER

API keys resolved at call time from platform keychain. Values never enter LLM context. All output masked.

## Signing and Verification

Install binary GPG-verified during curl install. Signature checked before placing in $PATH.

.primefile Dual-signed: T1 kernel key + T0 human key. Both must verify.

Agentfiles Ed25519 self-signing (default), GPG, Sigstore, or both. ATTEST directive controls mode.

Governance changes Negotiate protocol: file offer, counter-party review, dual-sign, merge --no-ff.

RELATED: TRUST_MODEL (/docs/trust-model/) CAPABILITY_PINS (/docs/pins/) SECRETS (/docs/secrets/)

---
